1.note: tn Heb “commandment.” The word מִצְוָה (mitsvah) again is in the singular, serving as a comprehensive term for the whole stipulation section of the book. See note on the word “commandments” in 5:31.

1.anchor: your God instructed me to teach you so that you may carry them out in the land where you are headed
1.note: tn Heb “where you are going over to possess it” (so NASB); NRSV “that you are about to cross into and occupy.”

2.anchor: your God that you will keep all his statutes and commandments
2.note: tn Here the terms are not the usual חֻקִּים (khuqqim) and מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim; as in v. 1) but חֻקֹּת (khuqqot, “statutes”) and מִצְוֹת (mitsot, “commandments”). It is clear that these terms are used interchangeably and that their technical precision ought not be overly stressed.

2.anchor: that I am giving
2.note: tn Heb “commanding.” For stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy, “giving” has been used in the translation.

3.anchor: Pay attention, Israel, and be careful to do this so that it may go well with you and that you may increase greatly in number
3.note: tn Heb “may multiply greatly” (so NASB, NRSV); the words “in number” have been supplied in the translation for clarity.

3.anchor: , God of your ancestors,
3.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 10, 18, 23).

4.anchor: is one!
4.note: tn Heb “the Lord, our God, the Lord, one.” (1) One option is to translate: “The Lord is our God, the Lord alone” (cf. NAB, NRSV, NLT). This would be an affirmation that the Lord was the sole object of their devotion. This interpretation finds support from the appeals to loyalty that follow (vv. 5, 14). (2) Another option is to translate: “The Lord is our God, the Lord is unique.” In this case the text would be affirming the people’s allegiance to the Lord, as well as the Lord’s superiority to all other gods. It would also imply that he is the only one worthy of their worship. Support for this view comes from parallel texts such as Deut 7:9 and 10:17, as well as the use of “one” in Song 6:8-9, where the starstruck lover declares that his beloved is unique (literally, “one,” that is, “one of a kind”) when compared to all other women.sn Verses 4-5 constitute the so-called Shema (after the first word שְׁמַע, shÿma’, “hear”), widely regarded as the very heart of Jewish confession and faith. When Jesus was asked what was the greatest commandment of all, he quoted this text (Matt 22:37-38).

5.anchor: You must love
5.note: tn The verb אָהַב (’ahav, “to love”) in this setting communicates not so much an emotional idea as one of covenant commitment. To love the Lord is to be absolutely loyal and obedient to him in every respect, a truth Jesus himself taught (cf. John 14:15). See also the note on the word “loved” in Deut 4:37.

5.anchor: your God with your whole mind,
5.note: tn Heb “heart.” In OT physiology the heart (לֵב, לֵבָב; levav, lev) was considered the seat of the mind or intellect, so that one could think with one’s heart. See A. Luc, NIDOTTE 2:749-54.

5.anchor: your whole being,
5.note: tn Heb “soul”; “being.” Contrary to Hellenistic ideas of a soul that is discrete and separate from the body and spirit, OT anthropology equated the “soul” (נֶפֶשׁ, nefesh) with the person himself. It is therefore best in most cases to translate נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) as “being” or the like. See H. W. Wolff, Anthropology of the Old Testament, 10-25; D. Fredericks, NIDOTTE 3:133-34.

5.anchor: and all your strength.
5.note: sn For NT variations on the Shema see Matt 22:37-39; Mark 12:29-30; Luke 10:27.

7.note: tn Heb “repeat” (so NLT). If from the root I שָׁנַן (shanan), the verb means essentially to “engrave,” that is, “to teach incisively” (Piel); note NAB “Drill them into your children.” Cf. BDB 1041-42 s.v.

7.anchor: them to your children and speak of them as you sit in your house, as you walk along the road,
7.note: tn Or “as you are away on a journey” (cf. NRSV, TEV, NLT); NAB “at home and abroad.”

8.anchor: You should tie them as a reminder on your forearm
8.note: sn Tie them as a sign on your forearm. Later Jewish tradition referred to the little leather containers tied to the forearms and foreheads as tefillin. They were to contain the following passages from the Torah: Exod 13:1-10, 11-16; Deut 6:5-9; 11:13-21. The purpose was to serve as a “sign” of covenant relationship and obedience.

8.anchor: and fasten them as symbols
8.note: sn Fasten them as symbols on your forehead. These were also known later as tefillin (see previous note) or phylacteries (from the Greek term). These box-like containers, like those on the forearms, held the same scraps of the Torah. It was the hypocritical practice of wearing these without heartfelt sincerity that caused Jesus to speak scathingly about them (cf. Matt 23:5).

9.anchor: Inscribe them on the doorframes of your houses and gates.
9.note: sn The Hebrew term מְזוּזֹת (mÿzuzot) refers both to the door frames and to small cases attached on them containing scripture texts (always Deut 6:4-9 and 11:13-21; and sometimes the decalogue; Exod 13:1-10, 11-16; and Num 10:35-36). See J. H. Tigay, Deuteronomy (JPSTC), 443-44.

12.anchor: who brought you out of Egypt, that place of slavery.
12.note: tn Heb “out of the house of slavery” (so NASB, NRSV).

14.anchor: You must not go after other gods, those
14.note: tn Heb “from the gods.” The demonstrative pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

15.anchor: your God, who is present among you, is a jealous God and his anger will erupt against you and remove you from the land.
15.note: tn Heb “lest the anger of the Lord your God be kindled against you and destroy you from upon the surface of the ground.” Cf. KJV, ASV “from off the face of the earth.”

16.anchor: your God to the test as you did at Massah.
16.note: sn The place name Massah (מַסָּה, massah) derives from a root (נָסָה, nasah) meaning “to test; to try.” The reference here is to the experience in the Sinai desert when Moses struck the rock to obtain water (Exod 17:1-2). The complaining Israelites had, thus, “tested” the Lord, a wickedness that gave rise to the naming of the place (Exod 17:7; cf. Deut 9:22; 33:8).

17.anchor: Keep his
17.note: tn Heb “the commandments of the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

17.anchor: commandments very carefully,
17.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute before the finite verb to emphasize the statement. The imperfect verbal form is used here with an obligatory nuance that can be captured in English through the imperative. Cf. NASB, NRSV “diligently keep (obey NLT).”

18.anchor: Do whatever is proper
18.note: tn Heb “upright.”

18.anchor: so that it may go well with you and that you may enter and occupy the good land that he
18.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on the word “his” in v. 17.

20.anchor: When your children
20.note: tn Heb “your son.”

21.anchor: you must say to them,
21.note: tn Heb “to your son.”

21.anchor: brought us out of Egypt in a powerful way.
21.note: tn Heb “by a strong hand.” The image is that of a warrior who, with weapon in hand, overcomes his enemies. The Lord is commonly depicted as a divine warrior in the Book of Deuteronomy (cf. 5:15; 7:8; 9:26; 26:8).

22.anchor: And he
22.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on the word “his” in v. 17.

22.anchor: brought signs and great, devastating wonders on Egypt, on Pharaoh, and on his whole family
22.note: tn Heb “house,” referring to the entire household.

24.anchor: commanded us to obey all these statutes and to revere him
24.note: tn Heb “the Lord our God.” See note on the word “his” in v. 17.

25.anchor: We will be innocent if we carefully keep all these commandments
25.note: tn The term “commandment” (מִצְוָה, mitsvah), here in the singular, refers to the entire body of covenant stipulations.

25.anchor: our God, just as he demands.”
25.note: tn Heb “as he has commanded us” (so NIV, NRSV).

1.note: tn Heb “turned and went up.”
sn Bashan. This plateau country, famous for its oaks (Isa 2:13) and cattle (Deut 32:14; Amos 4:1), was north of Gilead along the Yarmuk River.

1.anchor: but King Og of Bashan and his whole army
1.note: tn Heb “people.”

1.anchor: came out to meet us in battle at Edrei.
1.note: sn Edrei is probably modern Deràa, 60 mi (95 km) south of Damascus (see Num 21:33; Josh 12:4; 13:12, 31; also mentioned in Deut 1:4).

2.anchor: , however, said to me, “Don’t be afraid of him because I have already given him, his whole army,
2.note: tn Heb “people.”

3.anchor: our God did indeed give over to us King Og of Bashan and his whole army and we struck them down until not a single survivor was left.
3.note: tn Heb “was left to him.” The final phrase “to him” is redundant in English and has been left untranslated.

4.anchor: We captured all his cities at that time – there was not a town we did not take from them – sixty cities, all the region of Argob,
4.note: sn Argob. This is a subdistrict of Bashan, perhaps north of the Yarmuk River. See Y. Aharoni, Land of the Bible, 314.

5.anchor: All of these cities were fortified by high walls, gates, and locking bars;
5.note: tn Or “high walls and barred gates” (NLT); Heb “high walls, gates, and bars.” Since “bars” could be understood to mean “saloons,” the qualifying adjective “locking” has been supplied in the translation.

5.anchor: in addition there were a great many open villages.
5.note: tn The Hebrew term פְּרָזִי (pÿraziy) refers to rural areas, at the most “unwalled villages” (KJV, NASB “unwalled towns”).

6.anchor: We put all of these under divine judgment
6.note: tn Heb “we put them under the ban” (נַחֲרֵם, nakharem). See note at 2:34.sn The divine curse. See note on this phrase in Deut 2:34.

6.anchor: just as we had done to King Sihon of Heshbon – every occupied city,
6.note: tn Heb “city of men.”

8.anchor: So at that time we took the land of the two Amorite kings in the Transjordan from Wadi Arnon to Mount Hermon
8.note: sn Mount Hermon. This is the famous peak at the southern end of the Anti-Lebanon mountain range known today as Jebel es-Sheik.

9.anchor: (the Sidonians
9.note: sn Sidonians were Phoenician inhabitants of the city of Sidon (now in Lebanon), about 47 mi (75 km) north of Mount Carmel.

9.anchor: call Hermon Sirion
9.note: sn Sirion. This name is attested in the Ugaritic texts as sryn. See UT 495.

9.anchor: and the Amorites call it Senir),
9.note: sn Senir. Probably this was actually one of the peaks of Hermon and not the main mountain (Song of Songs 4:8; 1 Chr 5:23). It is mentioned in a royal inscription of Shalmaneser III of Assyria (saniru; see ANET 280).

10.anchor: all the cities of the plateau, all of Gilead and Bashan as far as Salecah
10.note: sn Salecah. Today this is known as Salkhad, in Jordan, about 31 mi (50 km) east of the Jordan River in the Hauran Desert.

10.anchor: and Edrei,
10.note: sn Edrei. See note on this term in 3:1.

11.anchor: Only King Og of Bashan was left of the remaining Rephaites. (It is noteworthy
11.note: tn Heb “Behold” (הִנֵּה, hinneh).

11.anchor: that his sarcophagus
11.note: tn The Hebrew term עֶרֶשׂ (’eres), traditionally translated “bed” (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT) is likely a basaltic (volcanic) stone sarcophagus of suitable size to contain the coffin of the giant Rephaite king. Its iron-like color and texture caused it to be described as an iron container. See A. Millard, “King Og’s Iron Bed: Fact or Fancy?” BR 6 (1990): 16-21, 44; cf. also NEB “his sarcophagus of basalt”; TEV, CEV “his coffin.”

11.anchor: was made of iron.
11.note: tn Or “of iron-colored basalt.” See note on the word “sarcophagus” earlier in this verse.

11.anchor: Does it not, indeed, still remain in Rabbath
11.note: sn Rabbath. This place name (usually occurring as Rabbah; 2 Sam 11:11; 12:27; Jer 49:3) refers to the ancient capital of the Ammonite kingdom, now the modern city of Amman, Jordan. The word means “great [one],” probably because of its political importance. The fact that the sarcophagus “still remain[ed]” there suggests this part of the verse is post-Mosaic, having been added as a matter of explanation for the existence of the artifact and also to verify the claim as to its size.

11.anchor: of the Ammonites? It is thirteen and a half feet
11.note: tn Heb “nine cubits.” Assuming a length of 18 in (45 cm) for the standard cubit, this would be 13.5 ft (4.1 m) long.

11.anchor: long and six feet
11.note: tn Heb “four cubits.” This would be 6 ft (1.8 m) wide.

11.anchor: wide according to standard measure.)
11.note: tn Heb “by the cubit of man.” This probably refers to the “short” or “regular” cubit of approximately 18 in (45 cm).

12.anchor: This is the land we brought under our control at that time: The territory extending from Aroer
12.note: tn The words “the territory extending” are not in the Hebrew text; they are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.sn Aroer. See note on this term in Deut 2:36.

12.anchor: by the Wadi Arnon and half the Gilead hill country with its cities I gave to the Reubenites and Gadites.
12.note: sn Reubenites and Gadites. By the time of Moses’ address the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh had already been granted permission to settle in the Transjordan, provided they helped the other tribes subdue the occupants of Canaan (cf. Num 32:28-42).

13.anchor: The rest of Gilead and all of Bashan, the kingdom of Og, I gave to half the tribe of Manasseh.
13.note: sn Half the tribe of Manasseh. The tribe of Manasseh split into clans, with half opting to settle in Bashan and the other half in Canaan (cf. Num 32:39-42; Josh 17:1-13).

13.anchor: (All the region of Argob,
13.note: sn Argob. See note on this term in v. 4.

14.anchor: Jair, son of Manasseh, took all the Argob region as far as the border with the Geshurites
14.note: sn Geshurites. Geshur was a city and its surrounding area somewhere northeast of Bashan (cf. Josh 12:5 ; 13:11, 13). One of David’s wives was Maacah, the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur and mother of Absalom (cf. 2 Sam 13:37; 15:8; 1 Chr 3:2).

14.anchor: and Maacathites
14.note: sn Maacathites. These were the people of a territory southwest of Mount Hermon on the Jordan River. The name probably has nothing to do with David’s wife from Geshur (see note on “Geshurites” earlier in this verse).

14.anchor: (namely Bashan) and called it by his name, Havvoth-Jair,
14.note: sn Havvoth-Jair. The Hebrew name means “villages of Jair,” the latter being named after a son (i.e., descendant) of Manasseh who took the area by conquest.

15.anchor: I gave Gilead to Machir.
15.note: sn Machir was the name of another descendant of Manasseh (cf. Num 32:41; 1 Chr 7:14-19). Eastern Manasseh was thus divided between the Jairites and the Machirites.

17.anchor: The Arabah and the Jordan River
17.note: tn The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity (also in vv. 20, 25).

17.anchor: were also a border, from the sea of Chinnereth
17.note: tn Heb “from Chinnereth.” The words “the sea of” have been supplied in the translation as a clarification.sn Chinnereth. This is another name for the Sea of Galilee, so called because its shape is that of a harp (the Hebrew term for “harp” is כִּנּוֹר, kinnor).

17.anchor: to the sea of the Arabah (that is, the Salt Sea),
17.note: sn The Salt Sea is another name for the Dead Sea (cf. Gen 14:3; Josh 3:16).

17.anchor: beneath the watershed
17.note: tn The meaning of the Hebrew term אַשְׁדֹּת (’ashdot) is unclear. It is usually translated either “slopes” (ASV, NAB, NIV) or “watershed” (NEB).

17.anchor: of Pisgah
17.note: sn Pisgah. This appears to refer to a small range of mountains, the most prominent peak of which is Mount Nebo (Num 21:20; 23:14; Deut 3:27; cf. 34:1).

18.anchor: your God has given you this land for your possession. You warriors are to cross over before your fellow Israelites
18.note: tn Heb “your brothers, the sons of Israel.”

20.anchor: You must fight
20.note: tn The words “you must fight” are not present in the Hebrew text, but are supplied in the translation for clarity.

20.anchor: gives your countrymen victory
20.note: tn Heb “gives your brothers rest.”

21.anchor: your God did to these two kings; he
21.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The translation uses the pronoun (“he”) for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy.

21.anchor: will do the same to all the kingdoms where you are going.
21.note: tn Heb “which you are crossing over there.”

24.note: tn Heb “Lord Lord.” The phrase אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה (’adonay yÿhvih) is customarily rendered by Jewish tradition as “Lord God.” Cf. NIV, TEV, NLT “Sovereign Lord.”

24.anchor: you have begun to show me
24.note: tn Heb “your servant.” The pronoun is used in the translation to clarify that Moses is speaking of himself, since in contemporary English one does not usually refer to oneself in third person.

24.anchor: your greatness and strength.
24.note: tn Heb “your strong hand” (so NIV), a symbol of God’s activity.

25.anchor: Let me please cross over to see the good land on the other side of the Jordan River – this good hill country and the Lebanon!”
25.note: tn The article is retained in the translation (“the Lebanon,” cf. also NAB, NRSV) to indicate that a region (rather than the modern country of Lebanon) is referred to here. Other recent English versions accomplish this by supplying “mountains” after “Lebanon” (TEV, CEV, NLT).

26.anchor: was angry at me because of you and would not listen to me. Instead, he
26.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” For stylistic reasons the pronoun (“he”) has been used in the translation here.

26.anchor: said to me, “Enough of that!
26.note: tn Heb “much to you” (an idiom).

27.anchor: Go up to the top of Pisgah and take a good look to the west, north, south, and east,
27.note: tn Heb “lift your eyes to the west, north, south, and east and see with your eyes.” The translation omits the repetition of “your eyes” for stylistic reasons.

28.note: tn Heb “command”; KJV, NASB, NRSV “charge Joshua.”

29.anchor: So we settled down in the valley opposite Beth Peor.
29.note: sn Beth Peor. This is probably the spot near Pisgah where Balaam attempted to curse the nation Israel (Num 23:28). The Moabites also worshiped Baal there by the name “Baal [of] Peor” (Num 25:1-5).

1.anchor: Listen, Israel: Today you are about to cross the Jordan so you can dispossess the nations there, people greater and stronger than you who live in large cities with extremely high fortifications.
1.note: tn Heb “fortified to the heavens” (so NRSV); NLT “cities with walls that reach to the sky.” This is hyperbole.

2.anchor: They include the Anakites,
2.note: sn Anakites. See note on this term in Deut 1:28.

2.anchor: a numerous
2.note: tn Heb “great and tall.” Many English versions understand this to refer to physical size or strength rather than numbers (cf. “strong,” NIV, NCV, NRSV, NLT).

3.anchor: your God who goes before you is a devouring fire; he will defeat and subdue them before you. You will dispossess and destroy them quickly just as he
3.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The pronoun has been used in the translation in keeping with contemporary English style to avoid redundancy.

5.anchor: It is not because of your righteousness, or even your inner uprightness,
5.note: tn Heb “uprightness of your heart” (so NASB, NRSV). The Hebrew word צְדָקָה (tsÿdaqah, “righteousness”), though essentially synonymous here with יֹשֶׁר (yosher, “uprightness”), carries the idea of conformity to an objective standard. The term יֹשֶׁר has more to do with an inner, moral quality (cf. NAB, NIV “integrity”). Neither, however, was grounds for the Lord’s favor. As he states in both vv. 4-5, the main reason he allowed Israel to take this land was the sinfulness of the Canaanites who lived there (cf. Gen 15:16).

5.anchor: your God is driving them out ahead of you in order to confirm the promise he
5.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 9:3.

5.anchor: made on oath to your ancestors,
5.note: tn Heb “fathers.”

6.anchor: your God is about to give you this good land as a possession, for you are a stubborn
6.note: tn Heb “stiff-necked” (so KJV, NAB, NIV).sn The Hebrew word translated stubborn means “stiff-necked.” The image is that of a draft animal that is unsubmissive to the rein or yoke and refuses to bend its neck to draw the load. This is an apt description of OT Israel (Exod 32:9; 33:3, 5; 34:9; Deut 9:13).

7.note: tn By juxtaposing the positive זְכֹר (zekhor, “remember”) with the negative אַל־תִּשְׁכַּח (’al-tishÿkakh, “do not forget”), Moses makes a most emphatic plea.

7.anchor: your God in the desert; from the time you left the land of Egypt until you came to this place you were constantly rebelling against him.
7.note: tn Heb “the Lord” (likewise in the following verse with both “him” and “he”). See note on “he” in 9:3.

9.anchor: made with you, I remained there
9.note: tn Heb “in the mountain.” The demonstrative pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons.

10.anchor: gave me the two stone tablets, written by the very finger
10.note: sn The very finger of God. This is a double figure of speech (1) in which God is ascribed human features (anthropomorphism) and (2) in which a part stands for the whole (synecdoche). That is, God, as Spirit, has no literal finger nor, if he had, would he write with his finger. Rather, the sense is that God himself – not Moses in any way – was responsible for the composition of the Ten Commandments (cf. Exod 31:18; 32:16; 34:1).

10.anchor: of God, and on them was everything
10.note: tn Heb “according to all the words.”

10.anchor: he
10.note: tn Heb “the Lord” (likewise at the beginning of vv. 12, 13). See note on “he” in 9:3.

12.anchor: And he said to me, “Get up, go down at once from here because your people whom you brought out of Egypt have sinned! They have quickly turned from the way I commanded them and have made for themselves a cast metal image.”
12.note: tc Heb “a casting.” The MT reads מַסֵּכָה (massekhah, “a cast thing”) but some mss and Smr add עֵגֶל (’egel, “calf”), “a molten calf” or the like (Exod 32:8). Perhaps Moses here omits reference to the calf out of contempt for it.

13.anchor: Moreover, he said to me, “I have taken note of these people; they are a stubborn
13.note: tn Heb “stiff-necked.” See note on the word “stubborn” in 9:6.

14.anchor: Stand aside
14.note: tn Heb “leave me alone.”

14.anchor: and I will destroy them, obliterating their very name from memory,
14.note: tn Heb “from under heaven.”

15.anchor: So I turned and went down the mountain while it
15.note: tn Heb “the mountain.” The translation uses a pronoun for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

16.anchor: your God and had cast for yourselves a metal calf;
16.note: tn On the phrase “metal calf,” see note on the term “metal image” in v. 12.

16.anchor: you had quickly turned aside from the way he
16.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 9:3.

17.anchor: I grabbed the two tablets, threw them down,
17.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “from upon my two hands,” but as this seems somewhat obvious and redundant, it has been left untranslated for stylistic reasons.

19.anchor: ’s intense anger
19.note: tn Heb “the anger and the wrath.” Although many English versions translate as two terms, this construction is a hendiadys which serves to intensify the emotion (cf. NAB, TEV “fierce anger”).

19.anchor: that threatened to destroy you. But he
19.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 9:3.

20.anchor: was also angry enough at Aaron to kill him, but at that time I prayed for him
20.note: tn Heb “Aaron.” The pronoun is used in the translation to avoid redundancy.

21.anchor: As for your sinful thing
21.note: tn Heb “your sin.” This is a metonymy in which the effect (sin) stands for the cause (the metal calf).

21.anchor: that you had made, the calf, I took it, melted it down,
21.note: tn Heb “burned it with fire.”

22.anchor: at Taberah,
22.note: sn Taberah. By popular etymology this derives from the Hebrew verb בָעַר (ba’ar, “to burn”), thus, here, “burning.” The reference is to the Lord’s fiery wrath against Israel because of their constant complaints against him (Num 11:1-3).

22.anchor: Massah,
22.note: sn Massah. See note on this term in Deut 6:16.

22.anchor: and Kibroth-Hattaavah.
22.note: sn Kibroth-Hattaavah. This place name means in Hebrew “burial places of appetite,” that is, graves that resulted from overindulgence. The reference is to the Israelites stuffing themselves with the quail God had provided and doing so with thanklessness (Num 11:31-35).

23.anchor: And when he
23.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 9:3.

23.anchor: your God
23.note: tn Heb “the mouth of the Lord your God,” that is, against the commandment that he had spoken.

24.anchor: You have been rebelling against him
24.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 9:3.

25.anchor: for forty days and nights,
25.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “when I prostrated myself.” Since this is redundant, it has been left untranslated.

25.anchor: for he
25.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 9:3.

26.anchor: I prayed to him:
26.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 9:3.

tn Heb “Lord Lord” (אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה, ’adonay yÿhvih). The phrase is customarily rendered by Jewish tradition as “Lord God” (אֲדֹנָי אֱלֹהִים, ’adonay ’elohim). See also the note on the phrase “Lord God” in Deut 3:24.

26.anchor: do not destroy your people, your valued property
26.note: tn Heb “your inheritance”; NLT “your special (very own NRSV) possession.” Israel is compared to landed property that one would inherit from his ancestors and pass on to his descendants.

26.anchor: that you have powerfully redeemed,
26.note: tn Heb “you have redeemed in your greatness.”

26.anchor: whom you brought out of Egypt by your strength.
26.note: tn Heb “by your strong hand.”

28.anchor: Otherwise the people of the land
28.note: tc The MT reads only “the land.” Smr supplies עַם (’am, “people”) and LXX and its dependents supply “the inhabitants of the land.” The truncated form found in the MT is adequate to communicate the intended meaning; the words “the people of” are supplied in the translation for clarity.

28.anchor: was unable to bring them to the land he promised them, and because of his hatred for them he has brought them out to kill them in the desert.”
28.note: tn Or “wilderness” (so KJV, NASB, NRSV, NLT).

29.anchor: They are your people, your valued property,
29.note: tn Heb “your inheritance.” See note at v. 26.

29.anchor: whom you brought out with great strength and power.

1.anchor: Then we turned and set out toward the desert land on the way to the Red Sea
1.note: tn Heb “Reed Sea.” See note on the term “Red Sea” in Deut 1:40.

4.anchor: Instruct
4.note: tn Heb “command” (so KJV, NASB); NRSV “charge the people as follows.”

4.anchor: these people as follows: ‘You are about to cross the border of your relatives
4.note: tn Heb “brothers”; NAB “your kinsmen.”

4.anchor: the descendants of Esau,
4.note: sn The descendants of Esau (Heb “sons of Esau”; the phrase also occurs in 2:8, 12, 22, 29). These are the inhabitants of the land otherwise known as Edom, south and east of the Dead Sea. Jacob’s brother Esau had settled there after his bitter strife with Jacob (Gen 36:1-8). “Edom” means “reddish,” probably because of the red sandstone of the region, but also by popular etymology because Esau, at birth, was reddish (Gen 25:25).

5.anchor: Do not be hostile toward them, because I am not giving you any of their land, not even a footprint, for I have given Mount Seir
5.note: sn Mount Seir is synonymous with Edom.

6.anchor: You may purchase
6.note: tn Heb includes “with silver.”

7.note: tn The Hebrew text does not have the first person pronoun; it has been supplied for purposes of English style (the Lord is speaking here).
tn Heb “all the work of your hands.”
tn Heb “he has.” This has been converted to first person in the translation in keeping with English style.
tn Heb “known” (so ASV, NASB); NAB “been concerned about.”

7.anchor: your travels through this great wasteland. These forty years I have
7.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God has.” This has been replaced in the translation by the first person pronoun (“I”) in keeping with English style.

8.anchor: So we turned away from our relatives
8.note: tn Or “brothers”; NRSV “our kin.”

8.anchor: the descendants of Esau, the inhabitants of Seir, turning from the desert route,
8.note: tn Heb “the way of the Arabah” (so ASV); NASB, NIV “the Arabah road.”

8.anchor: from Elat
8.note: sn Elat was a port city at the head of the eastern arm of the Red Sea, that is, the Gulf of Aqaba (or Gulf of Eilat). Solomon (1 Kgs 9:28), Uzziah (2 Kgs 14:22), and Ahaz (2 Kgs 16:5-6) used it as a port but eventually it became permanently part of Edom. It may be what is known today as Tell el-Kheleifeh. Modern Eilat is located further west along the northern coast. See G. Pratico, “Nelson Glueck’s 1938-1940 Excavations at Tell el-Kheleifeh: A Reappraisal,” BASOR 259 (1985): 1-32.

8.anchor: and Ezion Geber,
8.note: sn Ezion Geber. A place near the Gulf of Aqaba, Ezion-geber must be distinguished from Elat (cf. 1 Kgs 9:26-28; 2 Chr 8:17-18). It was, however, also a port city (1 Kgs 22:48-49). It may be the same as the modern site Gezirat al-Fauran, 15 mi (24 km) south-southwest from Tell el-Kheleifah.

9.anchor: said to me, “Do not harass Moab and provoke them to war, for I will not give you any of their land as your territory. This is because I have given Ar
9.note: sn Ar was a Moabite city on the Arnon River east of the Dead Sea. It is mentioned elsewhere in the “Book of the Wars of Yahweh” (Num 21:15; cf. 21:28; Isa 15:1). Here it is synonymous with the whole land of Moab.

9.anchor: to the descendants of Lot
9.note: sn The descendants of Lot. Following the destruction of the cities of the plain, Sodom and Gomorrah, as God’s judgment, Lot fathered two sons by his two daughters, namely, Moab and Ammon (Gen 19:30-38). Thus, these descendants of Lot in and around Ar were the Moabites.

10.anchor: (The Emites
10.note: sn Emites. These giant people, like the Anakites (Deut 1:28), were also known as Rephaites (v. 11). They appear elsewhere in the narrative of the invasion of the kings of the east where they are said to have lived around Shaveh Kiriathaim, perhaps 9 to 11 mi (15 to 18 km) east of the north end of the Dead Sea (Gen 14:5).

11.anchor: These people, as well as the Anakites, are also considered Rephaites;
11.note: sn Rephaites. The earliest reference to this infamous giant race is, again, in the story of the invasion of the eastern kings (Gen 14:5). They lived around Ashteroth Karnaim, probably modern Tell Ashtarah (cf. Deut 1:4), in the Bashan plateau east of the Sea of Galilee. Og, king of Bashan, was a Rephaite (Deut 3:11; Josh 12:4; 13:12). Other texts speak of them or their kinfolk in both Transjordan (Deut 2:20; 3:13) and Canaan (Josh 11:21-22; 14:12, 15; 15:13-14; Judg 1:20; 1 Sam 17:4; 1 Chr 20:4-8). They also appear in extra-biblical literature, especially in connection with the city state of Ugarit. See C. L’Heureux, “Ugaritic and Biblical Rephaim,” HTR 67 (1974): 265-74.

12.anchor: Previously the Horites
12.note: sn Horites. Most likely these are the same as the well-known people of ancient Near Eastern texts described as Hurrians. They were geographically widespread and probably non-Semitic. Genesis speaks of them as the indigenous peoples of Edom that Esau expelled (Gen 36:8-19, 31-43) and also as among those who confronted the kings of the east (Gen 14:6).

12.anchor: gave them.)
12.note: tn Most modern English versions, beginning with the ASV (1901), regard vv. 10-12 as parenthetical to the narrative.

13.anchor: Now, get up and cross the Wadi Zered.”
13.note: sn Wadi Zered. Now known as Wadi el-H£esa, this valley marked the boundary between Moab to the north and Edom to the south.

13.anchor: So we did so.
13.note: tn Heb “we crossed the Wadi Zered.” This has been translated as “we did so” for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy.

15.anchor: that eliminated them from within
15.note: tn Heb “from the middle of.” Although many recent English versions leave this expression untranslated, the point seems to be that these soldiers did not die in battle but “within the camp.”

16.anchor: So it was that after all the military men had been eliminated from the community,
16.note: tn Heb “and it was when they were eliminated, all the men of war, to die from the midst of the people.”

18.anchor: “Today you are going to cross the border of Moab, that is, of Ar.
18.note: sn Ar. See note on this word in Deut 2:9.

19.anchor: But when you come close to the Ammonites, do not harass or provoke them because I am not giving you any of the Ammonites’ land as your possession; I have already given it to Lot’s descendants
19.note: sn Lot’s descendants. See note on this phrase in Deut 2:9.

20.anchor: (That also is considered to be a land of the Rephaites.
20.note: sn Rephaites. See note on this word in Deut 2:11.

20.anchor: The Rephaites lived there originally; the Ammonites call them Zamzummites.
20.note: sn Zamzummites. Just as the Moabites called Rephaites by the name Emites, the Ammonites called them Zamzummites (or Zazites; Gen 14:5).

21.anchor: destroyed the Rephaites
21.note: tn Heb “them”; the referent (the Rephaites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

21.anchor: in advance of the Ammonites,
21.note: tn Heb “them”; the referent (the Ammonites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

23.anchor: As for the Avvites
23.note: sn Avvites. Otherwise unknown, these people were probably also Anakite (or Rephaite) giants who lived in the lower Mediterranean coastal plain until they were expelled by the Caphtorites.

23.anchor: who lived in settlements as far west as Gaza, Caphtorites
23.note: sn Caphtorites. These peoples are familiar from both the OT (Gen 10:14; 1 Chr 1:12; Jer 47:4; Amos 9:7) and ancient Near Eastern texts (Miriam Lichtheim, Ancient Egyptian Literature, 2:37-38; ANET 138). They originated in Crete (OT “Caphtor”) and are identified as the ancestors of the Philistines (Gen 10:14; Jer 47:4).

23.anchor: who came from Crete
23.note: tn Heb “Caphtor”; the modern name of the island of Crete is used in the translation for clarity (cf. NCV, TEV, NLT).

24.anchor: Get up, make your way across Wadi Arnon. Look! I have already delivered over to you Sihon the Amorite, king of Heshbon,
24.note: sn Heshbon is the name of a prominent site (now Tell Hesba„n, about 7.5 mi [12 km] south southwest of Amman, Jordan). Sihon made it his capital after having driven Moab from the area and forced them south to the Arnon (Num 21:26-30). Heshbon is also mentioned in Deut 1:4.

25.anchor: This very day I will begin to fill all the people of the earth
25.note: tn Heb “under heaven” (so NIV, NRSV).

25.anchor: with dread and to terrify them when they hear about you. They will shiver and shake in anticipation of your approach.”
25.note: tn Heb “from before you.”

26.anchor: Then I sent messengers from the Kedemoth
26.note: sn Kedemoth. This is probably Aleiyan, about 8 mi (13 km) north of the Arnon and between Dibon and Mattanah.

27.anchor: “Let me pass through your land; I will keep strictly to the roadway.
27.note: tn Heb “in the way in the way” (בַּדֶּרֶךְ בַּדֶּרֶךְ, baderekh baderekh). The repetition lays great stress on the idea of resolute determination to stick to the path. IBHS 116 §7.2.3c.

28.anchor: so that I can eat and sell me water to drink.
28.note: tn Heb “and water for silver give to me so that I may drink.”

30.note: tc The translation follows the LXX in reading the first person pronoun. The MT, followed by many English versions, has a second person masculine singular pronoun, “your.”
tn Heb “hardened his spirit” (so KJV, NASB, NRSV); NIV “made his spirit stubborn.”
tn Heb “made his heart obstinate” (so KJV, NASB); NRSV “made his heart defiant.”

30.anchor: so that he might deliver him over to you

32.anchor: When Sihon and all his troops
emerged to encounter us in battle at Jahaz,
32.note: sn Jahaz. This is probably Khirbet el-Medeiyineh. See J. Dearman, “The Levitical Cities of Reuben and Moabite Toponymy,” BASOR 276 (1984): 55-57.

33.anchor: our God delivered him over to us and we struck him down, along with his sons
33.note: tc The translation follows the Qere or marginal reading; the Kethib (consonantal text) has the singular, “his son.”

34.anchor: At that time we seized all his cities and put every one of them
34.note: tn Heb “every city of men.” This apparently identifies the cities as inhabited.

tn Heb “under the ban” (נַחֲרֵם, nakharem). The verb employed is חָרַם (kharam, usually in the Hiphil) and the associated noun is חֵרֶם (kherem). See J. Naudé, NIDOTTE, 2:276-77, and, for a more thorough discussion, Susan Niditch, War in the Hebrew Bible, 28-77.sn Divine judgment refers to God’s designation of certain persons, places, and things as objects of his special wrath and judgment because, in his omniscience, he knows them to be impure and hopelessly unrepentant.

36.note: sn Aroer. Now known as àAraáir on the northern edge of the Arnon river, Aroer marked the southern limit of Moab and, later, of the allotment of the tribe of Reuben (Josh 13:9, 16).

36.anchor: which is at the edge of Wadi Arnon (it is the city in the wadi),
36.note: tn Heb “the city in the wadi.” This enigmatic reference may refer to Ar or, more likely, to Aroer itself. Epexegetically the text might read, “From Aroer…, that is, the city in the wadi.” See D. L. Christensen, Deuteronomy 1–11 (WBC), 49.

37.anchor: However, you did not approach the land of the Ammonites, the Wadi Jabbok,
37.note: sn Wadi Jabbok. Now known as the Zerqa River, this is a major tributary of the Jordan that normally served as a boundary between Ammon and Gad (Deut 3:16).

1.anchor: Then Moses called all the people of Israel together and said to them:
1.note: tn Heb “and Moses called to all Israel and he said to them”; NAB, NASB, NIV “Moses summoned (convened NRSV) all Israel.”

3.anchor: He
3.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

3.anchor: did not make this covenant with our ancestors
3.note: tn Heb “fathers.”

5.anchor: and you at that time to reveal to you the message
5.note: tn Or “word” (so KJV, NASB, NIV); NRSV “words.”

7.anchor: You must not have any other gods
7.note: tn Heb “there must not be for you other gods.” The expression “for you” indicates possession.

tn Heb “upon my face,” or “before me” (עַל־פָּנָיַ, ’al-panaya). Some understand this in a locative sense: “in my sight.” The translation assumes that the phrase indicates exclusion. The idea is that of placing any other god before the Lord in the sense of taking his place. Contrary to the view of some, this does not leave the door open for a henotheistic system where the Lord is the primary god among others. In its literary context the statement must be taken in a monotheistic sense. See, e.g., 4:39; 6:13-15.

8.anchor: You must not make for yourself an image
8.note: tn Heb “an image, any likeness.”

8.anchor: of anything in heaven above, on earth below, or in the waters beneath.
8.note: tn Heb “under the earth” (so ASV, NASB, NRSV); NCV “below the land.”

9.anchor: your God, am a jealous God. I punish
9.note: tn In the Hebrew text the form is a participle, which is subordinated to what precedes. For the sake of English style, the translation divides this lengthy verse into two sentences.

9.anchor: the sons, grandsons, and great-grandsons for the sin of the fathers who reject
9.note: tn Heb “who hate” (so NAB, NIV, NLT). Just as “to love” (אָהַב, ’ahav) means in a covenant context “to choose, obey,” so “to hate” (שָׂנֵא, sane’) means “to reject, disobey” (cf. the note on the word “loved” in Deut 4:37; see also 5:10).

9.anchor: me,
9.note: tn Heb “visiting the sin of fathers upon sons and upon a third (generation) and upon a fourth (generation) of those who hate me.” God sometimes punishes children for the sins of a father (cf. Num 16:27, 32; Josh 7:24-25; 2 Sam 21:1-9). On the principle of corporate solidarity and responsibility in OT thought see J. Kaminsky, Corporate Responsibility in the Hebrew Bible (JSOTSup). In the idiom of the text, the father is the first generation and the “sons” the second generation, making grandsons the third and great-grandsons the fourth. The reference to a third and fourth generation is a way of emphasizing that the sinner’s punishment would last throughout his lifetime. In this culture, where men married and fathered children at a relatively young age, it would not be unusual for one to see his great-grandsons. In an Aramaic tomb inscription from Nerab dating to the seventh century b.c., Agbar observes that he was surrounded by “children of the fourth generation” as he lay on his death bed (see ANET 661). The language of the text differs from Exod 34:7, the sons are the first generation, the grandsons (literally, “sons of the sons”) the second, great-grandsons the third, and great-great-grandsons the fourth. One could argue that formulation in Deut 5:9 (see also Exod 20:50) is elliptical/abbreviated or that it suffers from textual corruption (the repetition of the words “sons” would invite accidental omission).

10.anchor: but I show covenant faithfulness
10.note: tn This theologically rich term (חֶסֶד, khesed) describes God’s loyalty to those who keep covenant with him. Sometimes it is used synonymously with בְּרִית (bÿrit, “covenant”; Deut 7:9), and sometimes interchangeably with it (Deut 7:12). See H.-J. Zobel, TDOT 5:44-64.

10.anchor: to the thousands
10.note: tc By a slight emendation (לַאֲלּוּפִים [la’allufim] for לַאֲלָפִים [la’alafim]) “clans” could be read in place of the MT reading “thousands.” However, no ms or versional evidence exists to support this emendation.tn Another option is to understand this as referring to “thousands (of generations) of those who love me” (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT). See Deut 7:9.

10.anchor: who choose
10.note: tn Heb “love.” See note on the word “reject” in v. 9.

11.anchor: your God for worthless purposes,
11.note: tn Heb “take up the name of the Lord your God to emptiness”; KJV “take the name of the Lord thy God in vain.” The idea here is not cursing or profanity in the modern sense of these terms but rather the use of the divine Name for unholy, mundane purposes, that is, for meaningless (the Hebrew term is שָׁוְא) and empty ends. In ancient Israel this would include using the Lord’s name as a witness in vows one did not intend to keep.

11.anchor: will not exonerate anyone who abuses his name that way.
11.note: tn Heb “who takes up his name to emptiness.”

12.anchor: Be careful to observe
12.note: tn Heb “to make holy,” that is, to put to special use, in this case, to sacred purposes (cf. vv. 13-15).

14.anchor: but the seventh day is the Sabbath
14.note: tn There is some degree of paronomasia (wordplay) here: “the seventh (הַשְּׁבִיעִי, hashÿvi’i) day is the Sabbath (שַׁבָּת, shabbat).” Otherwise, the words have nothing in common, since “Sabbath” is derived from the verb שָׁבַת (shavat, “to cease”).

14.anchor: your God. On that day you must not do any work, you, your son, your daughter, your male slave, your female slave, your ox, your donkey, any other animal, or the foreigner who lives with you,
14.note: tn Heb “in your gates”; NRSV, CEV “in your towns”; TEV “in your country.”

15.anchor: your God brought you out of there by strength and power.
15.note: tn Heb “by a strong hand and an outstretched arm,” the hand and arm symbolizing divine activity and strength. Cf. NLT “with amazing power and mighty deeds.”

15.anchor: your God has commanded you to observe
15.note: tn Or “keep” (so KJV, NRSV).

16.anchor: Honor
16.note: tn The imperative here means, literally, “regard as heavy” (כַּבֵּד, kabbed). The meaning is that great importance must be ascribed to parents by their children.

16.anchor: your God has commanded you to do, so that your days may be extended and that it may go well with you in the land that he
16.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “He” in 5:3.

17.anchor: You must not murder.
17.note: tn Traditionally “kill” (so KJV, ASV, RSV, NAB). The verb here (רָצַח, ratsakh) is generic for homicide but in the OT both killing in war and capital punishment were permitted and even commanded (Deut 13:5, 9; 20:13, 16-17), so the technical meaning here is “murder.”

20.anchor: You must not offer false testimony against another.
20.note: tn Heb “your neighbor.” Clearly this is intended generically, however, and not to be limited only to those persons who live nearby (frequently the way “neighbor” is understood in contemporary contexts). So also in v. 20.

21.anchor: You must not desire
21.note: tn The Hebrew verb used here (חָמַד, khamad) is different from the one translated “crave” (אָוַה, ’avah) in the next line. The former has sexual overtones (“lust” or the like; cf. Song of Sol 2:3) whereas the latter has more the idea of a desire or craving for material things.

21.anchor: another man’s
21.note: tn Heb “your neighbor’s.” See note on the term “fellow man” in v. 19.

21.anchor: wife, nor should you crave his
21.note: tn Heb “your neighbor’s.” The pronoun is used in the translation for stylistic reasons.

21.anchor: house, his field, his male and female servants, his ox, his donkey, or anything else he owns.”
21.note: tn Heb “or anything that is your neighbor’s.”

22.anchor: said these things to your entire assembly at the mountain from the middle of the fire, the cloud, and the darkness with a loud voice, and that was all he said.
22.note: tn Heb “and he added no more” (so KJV, NASB, NRSV); NLT “This was all he said at that time.”

22.anchor: Then he inscribed the words
22.note: tn Heb “them”; the referent (the words spoken by the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

24.anchor: our God has shown us his great glory
24.note: tn Heb “his glory and his greatness.”

24.anchor: and we have heard him speak from the middle of the fire. It is now clear to us
24.note: tn Heb “this day we have seen.”

26.anchor: Who is there from the entire human race
26.note: tn Heb “who is there of all flesh.”

27.anchor: our God is saying and then you can tell us whatever he
27.note: tn Heb “the Lord our God.” See note on “He” in 5:3.

28.anchor: heard you speaking to me, he
28.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “He” in 5:3.

29.anchor: If only it would really be their desire to fear me and obey
29.note: tn Heb “keep” (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV).

31.anchor: But as for you, remain here with me so I can declare to you all the commandments,
31.note: tn Heb “commandment.” The MT actually has the singular (הַמִּצְוָה, hammitsvah), suggesting perhaps that the following terms (חֻקִּים [khuqqim] and מִשְׁפָּטִים [mishpatim]) are in epexegetical apposition to “commandment.” That is, the phrase could be translated “the entire command, namely, the statutes and ordinances.” This would essentially make מִצְוָה (mitsvah) synonymous with תּוֹרָה (torah), the usual term for the whole collection of law.

31.anchor: statutes, and ordinances that you are to teach them, so that they can carry them out in the land I am about to give them.”
31.note: tn Heb “to possess it” (so KJV, ASV); NLT “as their inheritance.”

33.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

33.anchor: has commanded you so that you may live, that it may go well with you, and that you may live long
33.note: tn Heb “may prolong your days”; NAB “may have long life”; TEV “will continue to live.”

1.anchor: your God brings you to the land that you are going to occupy and forces out many nations before you – Hittites,
1.note: sn Hittites. The center of Hittite power was in Anatolia (central modern Turkey). In the Late Bronze Age (1550-1200 b.c.) they were at their zenith, establishing outposts and colonies near and far. Some elements were obviously in Canaan at the time of the Conquest (1400-1350 b.c.).

sn Girgashites. These cannot be ethnically identified and are unknown outside the OT. They usually appear in such lists only when the intention is to have seven groups in all (see also the note on the word “seven” later in this verse).
sn Amorites. Originally from the upper Euphrates region (Amurru), the Amorites appear to have migrated into Canaan beginning in 2200 b.c. or thereabouts.

sn Canaanites. These were the indigenous peoples of the land, going back to the beginning of recorded history (ca. 3000 b.c.). The OT identifies them as descendants of Ham (Gen 10:6), the only Hamites to have settled north and east of Egypt.
sn Perizzites. This is probably a subgroup of Canaanites (Gen 13:7; 34:30).

sn Hivites. These are usually thought to be the same as the Hurrians, a people well-known in ancient Near Eastern texts. They are likely identical to the Horites (see note on the term “Horites” in Deut 2:12).
sn Jebusites. These inhabited the hill country, particularly in and about Jerusalem (cf. Num 13:29; Josh 15:8; 2 Sam 5:6; 24:16).

sn Seven. This is an ideal number in the OT, one symbolizing fullness or completeness. Therefore, the intent of the text here is not to be precise and list all of Israel’s enemies but simply to state that Israel will have a full complement of foes to deal with. For other lists of Canaanites, some with fewer than seven peoples, see Exod 3:8; 13:5; 23:23, 28; 33:2; 34:11; Deut 20:17; Josh 3:10; 9:1; 24:11. Moreover, the “Table of Nations” (Gen 10:15-19) suggests that all of these (possibly excepting the Perizzites) were offspring of Canaan and therefore Canaanites.

2.anchor: and he
2.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

2.anchor: delivers them over to you and you attack them, you must utterly annihilate
2.note: tn In the Hebrew text the infinitive absolute before the finite verb emphasizes the statement. The imperfect has an obligatory nuance here. Cf. ASV “shalt (must NRSV) utterly destroy them”; CEV “must destroy them without mercy.”

2.anchor: them. Make no treaty
2.note: tn Heb “covenant” (so NASB, NRSV); TEV “alliance.”

5.anchor: Instead, this is what you must do to them: You must tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars,
5.note: sn Sacred pillars. The Hebrew word (מַצֵּבֹת, matsevot) denotes a standing pillar, usually made of stone. Its purpose was to mark the presence of a shrine or altar thought to have been visited by deity. Though sometimes associated with pure worship of the Lord (Gen 28:18, 22; 31:13; 35:14; Exod 24:4), these pillars were usually associated with pagan cults and rituals (Exod 23:24; 34:13; Deut 12:3; 1 Kgs 14:23; 2 Kgs 17:10; Hos 3:4; 10:1; Jer 43:13).

5.anchor: cut down their sacred Asherah poles,
5.note: sn Sacred Asherah poles. A leading deity of the Canaanite pantheon was Asherah, wife/sister of El and goddess of fertility. She was commonly worshiped at shrines in or near groves of evergreen trees, or, failing that, at places marked by wooden poles (Hebrew אֲשֵׁרִים [’asherim], as here). They were to be burned or cut down (Deut 12:3; 16:21; Judg 6:25, 28, 30; 2 Kgs 18:4).

6.anchor: For you are a people holy
6.note: tn That is, “set apart.”

6.anchor: your God. He
6.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

6.anchor: has chosen you to be his people, prized
6.note: tn Or “treasured” (so NIV, NRSV); NLT “his own special treasure.” The Hebrew term סְגֻלָּה (sÿgullah) describes Israel as God’s choice people, those whom he elected and who are most precious to him (cf. Exod 19:4-6; Deut 14:2; 26:18; 1 Chr 29:3; Ps 135:4; Eccl 2:8 Mal 3:17). See E. Carpenter, NIDOTTE 3:224.

8.anchor: Rather it is because of his
8.note: tn Heb “the Lord’s.” See note on “He” in 7:6.

8.anchor: love
8.note: tn For the verb אָהַב (’ahav, “to love”) as a term of choice or election, see note on the word “loved” in Deut 4:37.

8.anchor: for you and his faithfulness to the promise
8.note: tn Heb “oath.” This is a reference to the promises of the so-called “Abrahamic Covenant” (cf. Gen 15:13-16).

8.anchor: he solemnly vowed
8.note: tn Heb “swore on oath.”

8.anchor: to your ancestors
8.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 12, 13).

8.anchor: brought you out with great power,
8.note: tn Heb “by a strong hand” (NAB similar); NLT “with such amazing power.”

8.anchor: redeeming
8.note: sn Redeeming you from the place of slavery. The Hebrew verb translated “redeeming” (from the root פָּדָה, padah) has the idea of redemption by the payment of a ransom. The initial symbol of this was the Passover lamb, offered by Israel to the Lord as ransom in exchange for deliverance from bondage and death (Exod 12:1-14). Later, the firstborn sons of Israel, represented by the Levites, became the ransom (Num 3:11-13). These were all types of the redemption effected by the death of Christ who described his atoning work as “a ransom for many” (Matt 20:28; cf. 1 Pet 1:18).

8.anchor: you from the place of slavery, from the power
8.note: tn Heb “hand” (so KJV, NRSV), a metaphor for power or domination.

9.anchor: your God is the true God,
9.note: tn Heb “the God.” The article here expresses uniqueness; cf. TEV “is the only God”; NLT “is indeed God.”

9.anchor: the faithful God who keeps covenant faithfully
9.note: tn Heb “who keeps covenant and loyalty.” The syndetic construction of בְּרִית (bÿrit) and חֶסֶד (khesed) should be understood not as “covenant” plus “loyalty” but as an adverbial construction in which חֶסֶד (“loyalty”) modifies the verb שָׁמַר (shamar, “keeps”).

10.anchor: but who pays back those who hate
10.note: tn For the term “hate” as synonymous with rejection or disobedience see note on the word “reject” in Deut 5:9 (cf. NRSV “reject”).

10.anchor: him as they deserve and destroys them. He will not ignore
10.note: tn Heb “he will not hesitate concerning.”

12.anchor: your God will faithfully keep covenant with you
12.note: tn Heb “will keep with you the covenant and loyalty.” On the construction used here, see v. 9.

12.anchor: as he promised
12.note: tn Heb “which he swore on oath.” The relative pronoun modifies “covenant,” so one could translate “will keep faithfully the covenant (or promise) he made on oath to your ancestors.”

13.anchor: He will love and bless you, and make you numerous. He will bless you with many children,
13.note: tn Heb “will bless the fruit of your womb” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV).

14.anchor: You will be blessed beyond all peoples; there will be no barrenness
14.note: sn One of the ironies about the promises to the patriarchs concerning offspring was the characteristic barrenness of the wives of the men to whom these pledges were made (cf. Gen 11:30; 25:21; 29:31). Their affliction is in each case described by the very Hebrew word used here (עֲקָרָה, ’aqarah), an affliction that will no longer prevail in Canaan.

16.anchor: You must destroy
16.note: tn Heb “devour” (so NRSV); KJV, NAB, NASB “consume.” The verbal form (a perfect with vav consecutive) is understood here as having an imperatival or obligatory nuance (cf. the instructions and commands that follow). Another option is to take the statement as a continuation of the preceding conditional promises and translate “and you will destroy.”

16.anchor: your God is about to deliver over to you; you must not pity them or worship
16.note: tn Or “serve” (so KJV, NIV, NRSV).

18.anchor: you must not fear them. You must carefully recall
18.note: tn Heb “recalling, you must recall.” The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute before the finite verb for emphasis. Cf. KJV, ASV “shalt well remember.”

19.anchor: the great judgments
19.note: tn Heb “testings” (so NAB), a reference to the plagues. See note at 4:34.

19.anchor: you saw, the signs and wonders, the strength and power
19.note: tn Heb “the strong hand and outstretched arm.” See 4:34.

19.anchor: by which he
19.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

20.anchor: your God will release hornets
20.note: tn The meaning of the term translated “hornets” (צִרְעָה, tsir’ah) is debated. Various suggestions are “discouragement” (HALOT 1056-57 s.v.; cf. NEB, TEV, CEV “panic”; NCV “terror”) and “leprosy” (J. H. Tigay, Deuteronomy [JPSTC], 360, n. 33; cf. NRSV “the pestilence”), as well as “hornet” (BDB 864 s.v.; cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NLT). The latter seems most suitable to the verb שָׁלַח (shalakh, “send”; cf. Exod 23:28; Josh 24:12).

20.anchor: among them until the very last ones who hide from you
20.note: tn Heb “the remnant and those who hide themselves.”

22.anchor: He,
22.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 7:19.

23.anchor: your God will give them over to you; he will throw them into a great panic
23.note: tn Heb “he will confuse them (with) great confusion.” The verb used here means “shake, stir up” (see Ruth 1:19; 1 Sam 4:5; 1 Kgs 1:45; Ps 55:2); the accompanying cognate noun refers to confusion, unrest, havoc, or panic (1 Sam 5:9, 11; 14:20; 2 Chr 15:5; Prov 15:16; Isa 22:5; Ezek 7:7; 22:5; Amos 3:9; Zech 14:13).

24.anchor: He will hand over their kings to you and you will erase their very names from memory.
24.note: tn Heb “you will destroy their name from under heaven” (cf. KJV); NRSV “blot out their name from under heaven.”

25.anchor: You must burn the images of their gods, but do not covet the silver and gold that covers them so much that you take it for yourself and thus become ensnared by it; for it is abhorrent
25.note: tn The Hebrew word תּוֹעֵבָה (to’evah, “abhorrent; detestable”) describes anything detestable to the Lord because of its innate evil or inconsistency with his own nature and character. Frequently such things (or even persons) must be condemned to annihilation (חֵרֶם, kherem) lest they become a means of polluting or contaminating others (cf. Deut 13:17; 20:17-18). See M. Grisanti, NIDOTTE 4:315.

26.anchor: You must not bring any abhorrent thing into your house and thereby become an object of divine wrath
26.note: tn Heb “come under the ban” (so NASB); NRSV “be set apart for destruction.” The same phrase occurs again at the end of this verse.sn The Hebrew word translated an object of divine wrath (חֵרֶם, kherem) refers to persons or things placed under God’s judgment, usually to the extent of their complete destruction. See note on the phrase “divine judgment” in Deut 2:34.

26.anchor: along with it.
26.note: tn Or “like it is.”

26.anchor: You must absolutely detest
26.note: tn This Hebrew verb (שָׁקַץ, shaqats) is essentially synonymous with the next verb (תָעַב, ta’av; cf. תּוֹעֵבָה, to’evah; see note on the word “abhorrent” in v. 25), though its field of meaning is more limited to cultic abomination (cf. Lev 11:11, 13; Ps 22:25).

26.anchor: and abhor it,
26.note: tn Heb “detesting you must detest and abhorring you must abhor.” Both verbs are preceded by a cognate infinitive absolute indicating emphasis.

1.note: tn Heb “These are the words.”

1.anchor: Moses said to the assembly of Israel
1.note: tn Heb “to all Israel.”

tn Heb “on the other side of the Jordan.” This would appear to favor authorship by someone living on the west side of the Jordan, that is, in Canaan, whereas the biblical tradition locates Moses on the east side (cf. v. 5). However the Hebrew phrase בְּעֵבֶר הַיּרְדֵּן (bÿ’ever hayyrÿden) is a frozen form meaning “Transjordan,” a name appropriate from any geographical vantage point. To this day, one standing east of the Jordan can describe himself as being in Transjordan.
tn The Hebrew term מוֹל (mol) may also mean “in front of” or “near” (cf. NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT).
sn This place is otherwise unattested and its location is unknown. Perhaps it is Khirbet Sufah, 4 mi (6 km) SSE of Madaba, Jordan.
tn The Hebrew term בֵּין (ben) may suggest “in the area of.”
sn Paran is the well-known desert area between Mount Sinai and Kadesh Barnea (cf. Num 10:12; 12:16).
sn Tophel refers possibly to et£-T£afîleh, 15 mi (25 km) SE of the Dead Sea, or to Da‚bîlu, another name for Paran. See H. Cazelles, “Tophel (Deut. 1:1),” VT 9 (1959): 412-15.
sn Laban. Perhaps this refers to Libnah (Num 33:20).
sn Hazeroth. This probably refers to àAin Khadra. See Y. Aharoni, The Land of the Bible, 199-200.
sn Di Zahab. Perhaps this refers to Mina al-Dhahab on the eastern Sinai coast.

2.anchor: Now it is ordinarily an eleven-day journey
2.note: sn An eleven-day journey was about 140 mi (233 km).

2.anchor: from Horeb
2.note: sn Horeb is another name for Sinai. “Horeb” occurs 9 times in the Book of Deuteronomy and “Sinai” only once (33:2). “Sinai” occurs 13 times in the Book of Exodus and “Horeb” only 3 times.

2.anchor: to Kadesh Barnea
2.note: sn Kadesh Barnea. Possibly this refers to àAin Qudeis, about 50 mi (80 km) southwest of Beer Sheba, but more likely to àAin Qudeirat, 5 mi (8 km) NW of àAin Qudeis. See R. Cohen, “Did I Excavate Kadesh-Barnea?” BAR 7 (1981): 20-33.

2.anchor: by way of Mount Seir.
2.note: sn Mount Seir is synonymous with Edom. “By way of Mount Seir” refers to the route from Horeb that ended up in Edom Cf. CEV “by way of the Mount Seir Road”; TEV “by way of the hill country of Edom.”

3.anchor: However, it was not until
3.note: tn Heb “in” or “on.” Here there is a contrast between the ordinary time of eleven days (v. 2) and the actual time of forty years, so “not until” brings out that vast disparity.

3.anchor: the first day of the eleventh month
3.note: sn The eleventh month is Shebat in the Hebrew calendar, January/February in the modern (Gregorian) calendar.

3.anchor: of the fortieth year
3.note: sn The fortieth year would be 1406 b.c. according to the “early” date of the exodus. See E. H. Merrill, Kingdom of Priests, 66-75.

3.anchor: that Moses addressed the Israelites just as
3.note: tn Heb “according to all which.”

4.anchor: This took place after the defeat
4.note: tn Heb “when he struck [or “smote”].”

4.anchor: of King Sihon
4.note: sn See Deut 2:26–3:22.

4.anchor: of the Amorites, whose capital was
4.note: tn Heb “who lived.”

4.anchor: in Heshbon,
4.note: sn Heshbon is probably modern Tell Hesban, about 7.5 mi (12 km) south southwest of Amman, Jordan.

4.anchor: and King Og of Bashan, whose capital was
4.note: tn Heb “who lived.”

4.anchor: in Ashtaroth,
4.note: sn Ashtaroth is probably Tell àAshtarah, about 22 mi (35 km) due east of the Sea of Galilee.

4.anchor: specifically in Edrei.
4.note: sn Edrei is probably modern Deràa, 60 mi (95 km) south of Damascus (see Num 21:33; Josh 12:4; 13:12, 31).

5.anchor: So it was in the Transjordan, in Moab, that Moses began to deliver these words:
5.note: tn Heb “this instruction”; KJV, NIV, NRSV “this law”; TEV “God’s laws and teachings.” The Hebrew noun תוֹרָה (torah) is derived from the verb יָרָה (yarah, “to teach”) and here it refers to the Book of Deuteronomy, not the Pentateuch as a whole.

6.anchor: our God spoke to us at Horeb and said, “You have stayed
6.note: tn Heb “lived”; “dwelled.”

7.note: tn Heb “turn”; NAB “Leave here”; NIV, TEV “Break camp.”
tn Heb “go (to).”

7.anchor: the Amorite hill country, to all its areas
7.note: tn Heb “its dwelling places.”
tn Heb “the Arabah” (so ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV).
tn Heb “lowlands” (so TEV) or “steppes”; NIV, CEV, NLT “the western foothills.”sn The Shephelah is the geographical region between the Mediterranean coastal plain and the Judean hill country.

sn The Hebrew term Negev means literally “desert” or “south” (so KJV, ASV). It refers to the area south of Beer Sheba and generally west of the Arabah Valley between the Dead Sea and the Gulf of Aqaba.

8.anchor: Look! I have already given the land to you.
8.note: tn Heb “I have placed before you the land.”

8.anchor: Go, occupy the territory that I,
8.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” Since the Lord is speaking, it is preferable for clarity to supply the first person pronoun in the translation.

8.anchor: , promised
8.note: tn Heb “swore” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT). This refers to God’s promise, made by solemn oath, to give the patriarchs the land.

8.anchor: to give to your ancestors
8.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 11, 21, 35).

8.anchor: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and to their descendants.”
8.note: tn Heb “their seed after them.”

10.anchor: your God has increased your population
10.note: tn Heb “multiplied you.”

10.anchor: to the point that you are now as numerous as the very stars of the sky.
10.note: tn Or “heavens.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.

11.anchor: , the God of your ancestors, make you a thousand times more numerous than you are now, blessing you
11.note: tn Heb “may he bless you.”

13.anchor: Select wise and practical
13.note: tn The Hebrew verb נְבֹנִים (nÿvonim, from בִּין [bin]) is a Niphal referring to skill or intelligence (see T. Fretheim, NIDOTTE 1:652-53).

15.anchor: So I chose
15.note: tn Or “selected”; Heb “took.”

16.anchor: I furthermore admonished your judges at that time that they
16.note: tn Or “you.” A number of English versions treat the remainder of this verse and v. 17 as direct discourse rather than indirect discourse (cf. KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

16.anchor: should pay attention to issues among your fellow citizens
16.note: tn Heb “brothers.” The term “brothers” could, in English, be understood to refer to siblings, so “fellow citizens” has been used in the translation.

16.anchor: and judge fairly,
16.note: tn The Hebrew word צֶדֶק (tsedeq, “fairly”) carries the basic idea of conformity to a norm of expected behavior or character, one established by God himself. Fair judgment adheres strictly to that norm or standard (see D. Reimer, NIDOTTE 3:750).

16.anchor: whether between one citizen and another
16.note: tn Heb “between a man and his brother.”

16.anchor: or a citizen and a resident foreigner.
16.note: tn Heb “his stranger” or “his sojourner”; NAB, NIV “an alien”; NRSV “resident alien.” The Hebrew word גֵּר (ger) commonly means “foreigner.”

17.anchor: They
17.note: tn Heb “you,” and throughout the verse (cf. NASB, NRSV).

17.anchor: must not discriminate in judgment, but hear the lowly
17.note: tn Heb “the small,” but referring to social status, not physical stature.

20.anchor: our God is about to give
20.note: tn The Hebrew participle has an imminent future sense here, although many English versions treat it as a present tense (“is giving us,” NAB, NIV, NRSV) or a predictive future (“will give us,” NCV).

21.anchor: Look, he
21.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun (“he”) has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons, to avoid repetition.

21.anchor: has placed the land in front of you!
21.note: tn Or “has given you the land” (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV).

23.anchor: I thought this was a good idea,
23.note: tn Heb “the thing was good in my eyes.”

23.anchor: so I sent
23.note: tn Or “selected” (so NIV, NRSV, TEV); Heb “took.”

24.anchor: They left and went up to the hill country, coming to the Eshcol Valley,
24.note: tn Or “the Wadi Eshcol” (so NAB).sn The Eshcol Valley is a verdant valley near Hebron, still famous for its viticulture (cf. Num 13:22-23). The Hebrew name “Eshcol” means “trestle,” that is, the frame on which grape vines grow.

25.anchor: Then they took
25.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “in their hand,” which is unnecessary and somewhat redundant in English style.

26.anchor: your God.
26.note: tn Heb “the mouth of the Lord your God.” To include “the mouth” would make for odd English style. The mouth stands by metonymy for the Lord’s command, which in turn represents the Lord himself.

27.note: tn Heb “in your tents,” that is, privately.

28.anchor: What is going to happen to us? Our brothers have drained away our courage
28.note: tn Heb “have caused our hearts to melt.”

28.anchor: by describing people who are more numerous
28.note: tn Heb “greater.” Many English versions understand this to refer to physical size or strength rather than numbers (cf. “stronger,” NAB, NIV, NRSV; “bigger,” NASB).

28.anchor: and taller than we are, and great cities whose defenses appear to be as high as heaven
28.note: tn Or “as the sky.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.

28.anchor: itself! Moreover, they said they saw

28.note: tn Heb “the sons of the Anakim.”sn Anakites were giant people (Num 13:33; Deut 2:10, 21; 9:2) descended from a certain Anak whose own forefather Arba founded the city of Kiriath Arba, i.e., Hebron (Josh 21:11).

29.anchor: So I responded to you, “Do not be terrified
29.note: tn Heb “do not tremble and do not be afraid.” Two synonymous commands are combined for emphasis.

30.anchor: your God is about to go
30.note: tn The Hebrew participle indicates imminent future action here, though some English versions treat it as a predictive future (“will go ahead of you,” NCV; cf. also TEV, CEV).

30.anchor: ahead of you; he will fight for you, just as you saw him do in Egypt
30.note: tn Heb “according to all which he did for you in Egypt before your eyes.”

31.anchor: and in the desert, where you saw him
31.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun (“him”) has been employed in the translation for stylistic reasons.

34.anchor: heard you, he became angry and made this vow:
34.note: tn Heb “and swore,” i.e., made an oath or vow.

35.note: tn Heb “Not a man among these men.”

36.anchor: The exception is Caleb son of Jephunneh;
36.note: sn Caleb had, with Joshua, brought back to Israel a minority report from Canaan urging a conquest of the land, for he was confident of the Lord’s power (Num 13:6, 8, 16, 30; 14:30, 38).

36.anchor: he will see it and I will give him and his descendants the territory on which he has walked, because he has wholeheartedly followed me.”
36.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The pronoun (“me”) has been employed in the translation, since it sounds strange to an English reader for the Lord to speak about himself in third person.

38.anchor: However, Joshua son of Nun, your assistant,
38.note: tn Heb “the one who stands before you”; NAB “your aide”; TEV “your helper.”

38.anchor: will go. Encourage him, because he will enable Israel to inherit the land.
38.note: tn Heb “it”; the referent (the land) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

39.anchor: Also, your infants, who you thought would die on the way,
and your children, who as yet do not know good from bad,
39.note: sn Do not know good from bad. This is a figure of speech called a merism (suggesting a whole by referring to its extreme opposites). Other examples are the tree of “the knowledge of good and evil” (Gen 2:9), the boy who knows enough “to reject the wrong and choose the right” (Isa 7:16; 8:4), and those who “cannot tell their right hand from their left” (Jonah 4:11). A young child is characterized by lack of knowledge.

40.note: tn The Hebrew pronoun is plural, as are the following verbs, indicating that Moses and the people are addressed (note v. 41).

40.anchor: turn back and head for the desert by the way to the Red Sea.”
40.note: tn Heb “the Reed Sea.” “Reed” is a better translation of the Hebrew סוּף (suf), traditionally rendered “red.” The name “Red Sea” is based on the LXX which referred to it as ἐρυθρᾶς θαλάσσης (eruqra" qalassh", “red sea”). Nevertheless, because the body of water in question is known in modern times as the Red Sea, this term was used in the translation. The part of the Red Sea in view here is not the one crossed in the exodus but its eastern arm, now known as the Gulf of Eilat or Gulf of Aqaba.

43.note: tn Heb “the mouth of the Lord.” See note at 1:26.

44.anchor: The Amorite inhabitants of that area
44.note: tn Heb “in that hill country,” repeating the end of v. 43.

44.anchor: you and chased you like a swarm of bees, striking you down from Seir as far as Hormah.
44.note: sn Hormah is probably Khirbet el-Meshash, 5.5 mi (9 km) west of Arad and 7.5 mi (12 km) SE of Beer Sheba. Its name is a derivative of the verb חָרָם (kharam, “to ban; to exterminate”). See Num 21:3.

45.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The pronoun (“he”) has been employed in the translation here for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy.

45.anchor: paid no attention to you whatsoever.
45.note: tn Heb “did not hear your voice and did not turn an ear to you.”

46.anchor: Therefore, you remained at Kadesh for a long time – indeed, for the full time.
46.note: tn Heb “like the days which you lived.” This refers to the rest of the forty-year period in the desert before Israel arrived in Moab.

1.note: tn Heb “fathers.”

tn Heb “you must be careful to obey in the land the Lord, the God of your fathers, has given you to possess all the days which you live in the land.” This adverbial statement modifies “to obey,” not “to possess,” so the order in the translation has been rearranged to make this clear.

2.anchor: You must by all means destroy
2.note: tn Heb “destroying you must destroy”; KJV “Ye shall utterly (surely ASV) destroy”; NRSV “must demolish completely.” The Hebrew infinitive absolute precedes the verb for emphasis, which is reflected in the translation by the words “by all means.”

2.anchor: all the places where the nations you are about to dispossess worship their gods – on the high mountains and hills and under every leafy tree.
2.note: sn Every leafy tree. This expression refers to evergreens which, because they keep their foliage throughout the year, provided apt symbolism for nature cults such as those practiced in Canaan. The deity particularly in view is Asherah, wife of the great god El, who was considered the goddess of fertility and whose worship frequently took place at shrines near or among clusters (groves) of such trees (see also Deut 7:5). See J. Hadley, NIDOTTE 1:569-70; J. DeMoor, TDOT 1:438-44.

3.anchor: You must tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars,
3.note: sn Sacred pillars. These are the stelae (stone pillars; the Hebrew term is מַצֵּבֹת, matsevot) associated with Baal worship, perhaps to mark a spot hallowed by an alleged visitation of the gods. See also Deut 7:5.

3.anchor: burn up their sacred Asherah poles,
3.note: sn Sacred Asherah poles. The Hebrew term (plural) is אֲשֵׁרִים (’asherim). See note on the word “(leafy) tree” in v. 2, and also Deut 7:5.

5.anchor: But you must seek only the place he
5.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

5.anchor: chooses from all your tribes to establish his name as his place of residence,
5.note: tc Some scholars, on the basis of v. 11, emend the MT reading שִׁכְנוֹ (shikhno, “his residence”) to the infinitive construct לְשָׁכֵן (lÿshakhen, “to make [his name] to dwell”), perhaps with the 3rd person masculine singular sf לְשַׁכְּנוֹ (lÿshakÿno, “to cause it to dwell”). Though the presupposed nounשֵׁכֶן (shekhen) is nowhere else attested, the parallel here with שַׁמָּה (shammah, “there”) favors retaining the MT as it stands.

6.anchor: And there you must take your burnt offerings, your sacrifices, your tithes, the personal offerings you have prepared,
6.note: tn Heb “heave offerings of your hand.”

7.note: tn Heb “and your houses,” referring to entire households. The pronouns “you” and “your” are plural in the Hebrew text.

7.anchor: your God and rejoice in all the output of your labor with which he
7.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 12:5.

8.anchor: You must not do like we are doing here today, with everyone
8.note: tn Heb “a man.”

9.anchor: for you have not yet come to the final stop
9.note: tn Heb “rest.”

10.anchor: When you do go across the Jordan River
10.note: tn The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.

10.anchor: and settle in the land he
10.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 12:5.

10.anchor: is granting you as an inheritance and you find relief from all the enemies who surround you, you will live in safety.
10.note: tn In the Hebrew text vv. 10-11 are one long, complex sentence. For stylistic reasons the translation divides this into two sentences.

11.anchor: your God chooses for his name to reside, bringing
11.note: tn Heb “and it will be (to) the place where the Lord your God chooses to cause his name to dwell you will bring.”

11.anchor: everything I am commanding you – your burnt offerings, sacrifices, tithes, the personal offerings you have prepared,
11.note: tn Heb “heave offerings of your hand.”

11.anchor: and all your choice votive offerings which you devote to him.
11.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 12:5.

12.anchor: your God, along with your sons, daughters, male and female servants, and the Levites in your villages
12.note: tn Heb “within your gates” (so KJV, NASB); NAB “who belongs to your community.”

12.anchor: (since they have no allotment or inheritance with you).
12.note: sn They have no allotment or inheritance with you. See note on the word “inheritance” in Deut 10:9.

14.anchor: for you may do so
14.note: tn Heb “offer burnt offerings.” The expression “do so” has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

14.anchor: chooses in one of your tribal areas – there you may do everything I am commanding you.
14.note: sn This injunction to worship in a single and central sanctuary – one limited and appropriate to the thrice-annual festival celebrations (see Exod 23:14-17; 34:22-24; Lev 23:4-36; Deut 16:16-17) – marks a departure from previous times when worship was carried out at local shrines (cf. Gen 8:20; 12:7; 13:18; 22:9; 26:25; 35:1, 3, 7; Exod 17:15). Apart from the corporate worship of the whole theocratic community, however, worship at local altars would still be permitted as in the past (Deut 16:21; Judg 6:24-27; 13:19-20; 1 Sam 7:17; 10:5, 13; 2 Sam 24:18-25; 1 Kgs 18:30).

15.anchor: your God blesses you
15.note: tn Heb “only in all the desire of your soul you may sacrifice and eat flesh according to the blessing of the Lord your God which he has given to you.”

15.anchor: in all your villages.
15.note: tn Heb “gates” (so KJV, NASB; likewise in vv. 17, 18).

18.anchor: your God may you eat these, in the place he
18.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 12:5.

18.anchor: chooses. This applies to you, your son, your daughter, your male and female servants, and the Levites
18.note: tn See note at Deut 12:12.

18.anchor: your God in all the output of your labor.
18.note: tn Heb “in all the sending forth of your hands.”

20.anchor: your God extends your borders as he said he would do and you say, “I want to eat meat just as I please,”
20.note: tn Heb “for my soul desires to eat meat.”

20.anchor: you may do so as you wish.
20.note: tn Heb “according to all the desire of your soul you may eat meat.”

21.anchor: If the place he
21.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 12:5.

21.anchor: chooses to locate his name is too far for you, you may slaughter any of your herd and flock he
21.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 12:5.

21.anchor: has given you just as I have stipulated; you may eat them in your villages
21.note: tn Heb “gates” (so KJV, NASB); NAB “in your own community.”

23.anchor: However, by no means eat the blood, for the blood is life itself
23.note: sn The blood is life itself. This is a figure of speech (metonymy) in which the cause or means (the blood) stands for the result or effect (life).
