That is, life depends upon the existence and circulation of blood, a truth known empirically but not scientifically tested and proved until the 17th century a.d. (cf. Lev 17:11).

25.anchor: ’s sight.
25.note: tc Heb “in the eyes of the Lord.” The LXX adds “your God” to create the common formula, “the Lord your God.” The MT is preferred precisely because it does not include the stereotyped formula; thus it more likely preserves the original text.

26.anchor: will choose.
26.note: tc Again, to complete a commonly attested wording the LXX adds after “choose” the phrase “to place his name there.” This shows insensitivity to deliberate departures from literary stereotypes. The MT reading is to be preferred.

27.anchor: your God; the blood of your other sacrifices
27.note: sn These other sacrifices would be so-called peace or fellowship offerings whose ritual required a different use of the blood from that of burnt (sin and trespass) offerings (cf. Lev 3; 7:11-14, 19-21).

27.anchor: you must pour out on his
27.note: tn Heb “on the altar of the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

29.anchor: your God eliminates the nations from the place where you are headed and you dispossess them, you will settle down in their land.
29.note: tn Heb “dwell in their land” (so NASB). In the Hebrew text vv. 29-30 are one long sentence. For stylistic reasons the translation divides it into two.

31.anchor: your God the way they do!
31.note: tn Heb “you must not do thus to/for the Lord your God.”
tn See note on this term at Deut 7:25.
tn Heb “every abomination of the Lord.” See note on the word “his” in v. 27.

32.note: sn Beginning with 12:32, the verse numbers through 13:18 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 12:32 ET = 13:1 HT, 13:1 ET = 13:2 HT, 13:2 ET = 13:3 HT, etc., through 13:18 ET = 13:19 HT. With 14:1 the verse numbers in the ET and HT are again the same.

tn This verse highlights a phenomenon found throughout Deuteronomy, but most especially in chap. 12, namely, the alternation of grammatical singular and plural forms of the pronoun (known as Numeruswechsel in German scholarship). Critical scholarship in general resolves the “problem” by suggesting varying literary traditions – one favorable to the singular pronoun and the other to the plural – which appear in the (obviously rough) redacted text at hand. Even the ancient versions were troubled by the lack of harmony of grammatical number and in this verse, for example, offered a number of alternate readings. The MT reads “Everything I am commanding you (plural) you (plural) must be careful to do; you (singular) must not add to it nor should you (singular) subtract form it.” Smr, LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate suggest singular for the first two pronouns but a few Smr mss propose plural for the last two. What both ancient and modern scholars tend to overlook, however, is the covenantal theological tone of the Book of Deuteronomy, one that views Israel as a collective body (singular) made up of many individuals (plural). See M. Weinfeld, Deuteronomy 1–11 (AB), 15-16; J. A. Thompson, Deuteronomy (TOTC), 21-23.

32.anchor: must be careful to do everything I am commanding you. Do not add to it or subtract from it!
32.note: sn Do not add to it or subtract from it. This prohibition makes at least two profound theological points: (1) This work by Moses is of divine origination (i.e., it is inspired) and therefore can tolerate no human alteration; and (2) the work is complete as it stands (i.e., it is canonical).

1.anchor: Now, Israel, pay attention to the statutes and ordinances
1.note: tn These technical Hebrew terms (חֻקִּים [khuqqim] and מִשְׁפָּטִים [mishpatim]) occur repeatedly throughout the Book of Deuteronomy to describe the covenant stipulations to which Israel had been called to subscribe (see, in this chapter alone, vv. 1, 5, 6, 8). The word חֻקִּים derives from the verb חֹק (khoq, “to inscribe; to carve”) and מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim) from שָׁפַט (shafat, “to judge”). They are virtually synonymous and are used interchangeably in Deuteronomy.
tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 31, 37).

2.anchor: your God that I am delivering to
2.note: tn Heb “commanding.”

3.anchor: did at Baal Peor,
3.note: tc The LXX and Syriac read “to Baal Peor,” that is, the god worshiped at that place; see note on the name “Beth Peor” in Deut 3:29.

3.anchor: how he
3.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

3.anchor: eradicated from your midst everyone who followed Baal Peor.
3.note: tn Or “followed the Baal of Peor” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV), referring to the pagan god Baal.

5.anchor: my God told me to do, so that you might carry them out in
5.note: tn Heb “in the midst of” (so ASV).

6.anchor: So be sure to do them, because this will testify of your wise understanding
6.note: tn Heb “it is wisdom and understanding.”

6.anchor: to the people who will learn of all these statutes and say, “Indeed, this great nation is a very wise
6.note: tn Heb “wise and understanding.”

8.anchor: And what other great nation has statutes and ordinances as just
8.note: tn Or “pure”; or “fair”; Heb “righteous.”

8.anchor: as this whole law
8.note: tn The Hebrew phrase הַתּוֹרָה הַזֹּאת (hattorah hazzo’t), in this context, refers specifically to the Book of Deuteronomy. That is, it is the collection of all the חֻקִּים (khuqqim, “statutes,” 4:1) and מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim, “ordinances,” 4:1) to be included in the covenant text. In a full canonical sense, of course, it pertains to the entire Pentateuch or Torah.

8.anchor: that I am about to share with
8.note: tn Heb “place before.”

9.anchor: Again, however, pay very careful attention,
9.note: tn Heb “watch yourself and watch your soul carefully.”

10.anchor: You
10.note: tn The text begins with “(the) day (in) which.” In the Hebrew text v. 10 is subordinate to v. 11, but for stylistic reasons the translation treats v. 10 as an independent clause, necessitating the omission of the subordinating temporal phrase at the beginning of the verse.

10.anchor: your God at Horeb and he
10.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 4:3.

10.anchor: said to me, “Assemble the people before me so that I can tell them my commands.
10.note: tn Heb “my words.” See v. 13; in Hebrew the “ten commandments” are the “ten words.”

11.anchor: You approached and stood at the foot of the mountain, a mountain ablaze to the sky above it
11.note: tn Heb “a mountain burning with fire as far as the heart of the heavens.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.

11.anchor: and yet dark with a thick cloud.
11.note: tn Heb “darkness, cloud, and heavy cloud.”

12.anchor: spoke to you from the middle of the fire; you heard speech but you could not see anything – only a voice was heard.
12.note: tn The words “was heard” are supplied in the translation to avoid the impression that the voice was seen.

13.anchor: And he revealed to you the covenant
13.note: sn This is the first occurrence of the word בְּרִית (bÿrit, “covenant”) in the Book of Deuteronomy but it appears commonly hereafter (4:23, 31; 5:2, 3; 7:9, 12; 8:18; 9:9, 10, 11, 15; 10:2, 4, 5, 8; 17:2; 29:1, 9, 12, 14, 15, 18, 21, 25; 31:9, 16, 20, 25, 26; 33:9). Etymologically, it derives from the notion of linking or yoking together. See M. Weinfeld, TDOT 2:255.

13.anchor: he has commanded you to keep, the ten commandments,
13.note: tn Heb “the ten words.”

14.anchor: commanded me to teach you statutes and ordinances for you to keep in the land which you are about to enter and possess.
14.note: tn Heb “to which you are crossing over to possess it.”

15.anchor: Be very careful,
15.note: tn Heb “give great care to your souls.”

16.anchor: I say this
16.note: tn The words “I say this” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. In the Hebrew text v. 16 is subordinated to “Be careful” in v. 15, but this makes for an unduly long sentence in English.

18.anchor: anything that crawls
18.note: tn Heb “creeping thing.”

18.anchor: on the ground, or any fish in the deep waters of the earth.
18.note: tn Heb “under the earth.”

19.anchor: When you look up
19.note: tn Heb “lest you lift up your eyes.” In the Hebrew text vv. 16-19 are subordinated to “Be careful” in v. 15, but this makes for an unduly long sentence in English.

19.anchor: to the sky
19.note: tn Or “heavens.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.

19.anchor: and see the sun, moon, and stars – the whole heavenly creation
19.note: tn Heb “all the host of heaven.”

19.anchor: – you must not be seduced to worship and serve them,
19.note: tn In the Hebrew text the verbal sequence in v. 19 is “lest you look up…and see…and be seduced…and worship them…and serve them.” However, the first two actions are not prohibited in and of themselves. The prohibition pertains to the final three actions. The first two verbs describe actions that are logically subordinate to the following actions and can be treated as temporal or circumstantial: “lest, looking up…and seeing…, you are seduced.” See Joüon 2:635 §168.h.

19.anchor: your God has assigned
19.note: tn Or “allotted.”

19.anchor: them to all the people
19.note: tn Or “nations.”

19.anchor: of the world.
19.note: tn Heb “under all the heaven.”sn The OT views the heavenly host as God’s council, which surrounds his royal throne ready to do his bidding (see 1 Kgs 22:19). God has given this group, sometimes called the “sons of God” (cf. Job 1:6; 38:7; Ps 89:6), jurisdiction over the nations. See Deut 32:8 (LXX). Some also see this assembly as the addressee in Ps 82. While God delegated his council to rule over the nations, he established a theocratic government over Israel and ruled directly over his chosen people via the Mosaic covenant. See v. 20, as well as Deut 32:9.

20.anchor: has selected and brought from Egypt, that iron-smelting furnace,
20.note: tn A כּוּר (kur) was not a source of heat but a crucible (“iron-smelting furnace”) in which precious metals were melted down and their impurities burned away (see I. Cornelius, NIDOTTE 2:618-19); cf. NAB “that iron foundry, Egypt.” The term is a metaphor for intense heat. Here it refers to the oppression and suffering Israel endured in Egypt. Since a crucible was used to burn away impurities, it is possible that the metaphor views Egypt as a place of refinement to bring Israel to a place of submission to divine sovereignty.

20.anchor: to be his special people
20.note: tn Heb “to be his people of inheritance.” The Lord compares his people to valued property inherited from one’s ancestors and passed on to one’s descendants.

21.anchor: became angry with me because of you and vowed that I would never cross the Jordan nor enter the good land that he
21.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 4:3.

21.anchor: is about to give you.
21.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “(as) an inheritance,” or “(as) a possession.”

22.anchor: So I must die here in this land; I will not cross the Jordan. But you are going over and will possess that
22.note: tn Heb “this.” The translation uses “that” to avoid confusion; earlier in the verse Moses refers to Transjordan as “this land.”

23.anchor: your God that he has made with you, and that you do not make an image of any kind, just as he
23.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 4:3.

23.anchor: has forbidden
23.note: tn Heb “commanded.”

24.anchor: your God is a consuming fire; he is a jealous God.
24.note: tn The juxtaposition of the Hebrew terms אֵשׁ (’esh, “fire”) and קַנָּא (qanna’, “jealous”) is interesting in light of Deut 6:15 where the Lord is seen as a jealous God whose anger bursts into a destructive fire. For God to be “jealous” means that his holiness and uniqueness cannot tolerate pretended or imaginary rivals. It is not petty envy but response to an act of insubordination that must be severely judged (see H. Peels, NIDOTTE 3:937-40).

25.anchor: After you have produced children and grandchildren and have been in the land a long time,
25.note: tn Heb “have grown old in the land,” i.e., been there for a long time.

25.anchor: if you become corrupt and make an image of any kind
25.note: tn Heb “a form of anything.” Cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV, TEV “an idol.”

25.anchor: your God that enrage him,
25.note: tn The infinitive construct is understood here as indicating the result, not the intention, of their actions.

26.anchor: I invoke heaven and earth as witnesses against you
26.note: sn I invoke heaven and earth as witnesses against you. This stock formula introduces what is known form-critically as a רִיב (riv) or controversy pattern. It is commonly used in the ancient Near Eastern world in legal contexts and in the OT as a forensic or judicial device to draw attention to Israel’s violation of the Lord’s covenant with them (see Deut 30:19; Isa 1:2; 3:13; Jer 2:9). Since court proceedings required the testimony of witnesses, the Lord here summons heaven and earth (that is, all creation) to testify to his faithfulness, Israel’s disobedience, and the threat of judgment.

26.anchor: today that you will surely and swiftly be removed
26.note: tn Or “be destroyed”; KJV “utterly perish”; NLT “will quickly disappear”; CEV “you won’t have long to live.”

26.anchor: from the very land you are about to cross the Jordan to possess. You will not last long there because you will surely be
26.note: tn Or “be completely” (so NCV, TEV). It is not certain here if the infinitive absolute indicates the certainty of the following action (cf. NIV) or its degree.

27.anchor: will scatter you among the peoples and there will be very few of you
27.note: tn Heb “you will be left men (i.e., few) of number.”

29.anchor: your God from there, you will find him, if, indeed, you seek him with all your heart and soul.
29.note: tn Or “mind and being.” See Deut 6:5.

30.anchor: In your distress when all these things happen to you in the latter days,
30.note: sn The phrase is not used here in a technical sense for the eschaton, but rather refers to a future time when Israel will be punished for its sin and experience exile. See Deut 31:29.
tn Heb “hear his voice.” The expression is an idiom meaning “obey,” occurring in Deut 8:20; 9:23; 13:18; 21:18, 20; 26:14, 17; 27:10; 28:1-2, 15, 45, 62; 30:2, 8, 10, 20.

31.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 4:3.

31.anchor: is a merciful God), he will not let you down
31.note: tn Heb “he will not drop you,” i.e., “will not abandon you” (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

31.anchor: or destroy you, for he cannot
31.note: tn Or “will not.” The translation understands the imperfect verbal form to have an added nuance of capability here.

32.anchor: Indeed, ask about the distant past, starting from the day God created humankind
32.note: tn The Hebrew term אָדָם (’adam) may refer either to Adam or, more likely, to “man” in the sense of the human race (“mankind,” “humankind”). The idea here seems more universal in scope than reference to Adam alone would suggest.

tn The verb is not present in the Hebrew text but has been supplied in the translation for clarification. The challenge has both temporal and geographical dimensions. The people are challenged to (1) inquire about the entire scope of past history and (2) conduct their investigation on a worldwide scale.

34.note: tn The translation assumes the reference is to Israel’s God in which case the point is this: God’s intervention in Israel’s experience is unique in the sense that he has never intervened in such power for any other people on earth. The focus is on the uniqueness of Israel’s experience. Some understand the divine name here in a generic sense, “a god,” or “any god.” In this case God’s incomparability is the focus (cf. v. 35, where this theme is expressed).
tn Heb “tried to go to take for himself.”

34.anchor: a nation from the middle of another nation, accompanied by judgments,
34.note: tn Heb “by testings.” The reference here is the judgments upon Pharaoh in the form of plagues. See Deut 7:19 (cf. v. 18) and 29:3 (cf. v. 2).
tn Heb “by strong hand and by outstretched arm.”

36.anchor: From heaven he spoke to you in order to teach you, and on earth he showed you his great fire from which you also heard his words.
36.note: tn Heb “and his words you heard from the midst of the fire.”

37.note: tn The concept of love here is not primarily that of emotional affection but of commitment or devotion. This verse suggests that God chose Israel to be his special people because he loved the patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob) and had promised to bless their descendants. See as well Deut 7:7-9.

tc The LXX, Smr, Syriac, Targum, and Vulgate read a third person masculine plural suffix for the MT’s 3rd person masculine singular, “his descendants.” Cf. Deut 10:15. Quite likely the MT should be emended in this instance.

38.anchor: to dispossess nations greater and stronger than you and brought you here this day to give you their land as your property.
38.note: tn Heb “(as) an inheritance,” that is, landed property that one can pass on to one’s descendants.

40.anchor: Keep his statutes and commandments that I am setting forth

42.note: tn Heb “the slayer who slew his neighbor without knowledge.”

42.anchor: without hating him at the time of the accident
42.note: tn Heb “yesterday and a third (day).” The point is that there was no animosity between the two parties at the time of the accident and therefore no motive for the killing.

44.anchor: This is the law that Moses set before the Israelites.
44.note: tn Heb “the sons of Israel” (likewise in the following verse).

48.note: tn The words “their territory extended” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. In the Hebrew text vv. 47-49 are all one sentence, but for the sake of English style and readability the translation divides the text into two sentences.

48.anchor: from Aroer at the edge of the Arnon valley as far as Mount Siyon
48.note: sn Mount Siyon (the Hebrew name is שִׂיאֹן [si’on], not to be confused with Zion [צִיּוֹן, tsiyyon]) is another name for Mount Hermon, also called Sirion and Senir (cf. Deut 3:9).

49.anchor: including all the Arabah of the Transjordan in the east to the sea of the Arabah,
49.note: sn The sea of the Arabah refers to the Dead Sea, also known as the Salt Sea in OT times (cf. Deut 3:17).
tn The meaning of the Hebrew term אַשְׁדֹּת (’ashdot) is unclear. It is usually translated either “slopes” (ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT) or “watershed” (NEB).

1.anchor: Suppose a prophet or one who foretells by dreams
1.note: tn Heb “or a dreamer of dreams” (so KJV, ASV, NASB). The difference between a prophet (נָבִיא, navi’) and one who foretells by dreams (חֹלֵם אוֹ, ’o kholem) was not so much one of office – for both received revelation by dreams (cf. Num 12:6) – as it was of function or emphasis. The prophet was more a proclaimer and interpreter of revelation whereas the one who foretold by dreams was a receiver of revelation. In later times the role of the one who foretold by dreams was abused and thus denigrated as compared to that of the prophet (cf. Jer 23:28).

1.anchor: should appear among you and show you a sign or wonder,
1.note: tn The expression אוֹת אוֹ מוֹפֵת (’ot ’o mofet) became a formulaic way of speaking of ways of authenticating prophetic messages or other works of God (cf. Deut 28:46; Isa 20:3). The NT equivalent is the Greek term σημεῖον (shmeion), a sign performed (used frequently in the Gospel of John, cf. 2:11, 18; 20:30-31). They could, however, be counterfeited or (as here) permitted to false prophets by the Lord as a means of testing his people.

3.anchor: You must not listen to the words of that prophet or dreamer,
3.note: tn Heb “or dreamer of dreams.” See note on this expression in v. 1.

3.anchor: your God will be testing you to see if you love him
3.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

3.anchor: with all your mind and being.
3.note: tn Heb “all your heart and soul” (so NRSV, CEV, NLT); or “heart and being” (NCV “your whole being”). See note on the word “being” in Deut 6:5.

5.anchor: As for that prophet or dreamer,
5.note: tn Heb “or dreamer of dreams.” See note on this expression in v. 1.

5.anchor: your God has commanded you to go. In this way you must purge out evil from within.
5.note: tn Heb “your midst” (so NAB, NRSV). The severity of the judgment here (i.e., capital punishment) is because of the severity of the sin, namely, high treason against the Great King. Idolatry is a violation of the first two commandments (Deut 5:6-10) as well as the spirit and intent of the Shema (Deut 6:4-5).

6.anchor: Suppose your own full brother,
6.note: tn Heb “your brother, the son of your mother.” In a polygamous society it was not rare to have half brothers and sisters by way of a common father and different mothers.

6.anchor: your son, your daughter, your beloved wife, or your closest friend should seduce you secretly and encourage you to go and serve other gods
6.note: tn In the Hebrew text these words are in the form of a brief quotation: “entice you secretly saying, ‘Let us go and serve other gods.’”

6.anchor: that neither you nor your ancestors
6.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in v. 17).

6.anchor: have previously known,
6.note: tn Heb “which you have not known, you or your fathers.” (cf. KJV, ASV; on “fathers” cf. v. 18).

7.anchor: the gods of the surrounding people (whether near you or far from you, from one end of the earth
7.note: tn Or “land” (so NIV, NCV); the same Hebrew word can be translated “land” or “earth.”

9.anchor: Instead, you must kill him without fail!
9.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with the words “without fail” (cf. NIV “you must certainly put him to death”).

9.anchor: Your own hand must be the first to strike him,
9.note: tn Heb “to put him to death,” but this is misleading in English for such an action would leave nothing for the others to do.

10.anchor: You must stone him to death
10.note: sn Execution by means of pelting the offender with stones afforded a mechanism whereby the whole community could share in it. In a very real sense it could be done not only in the name of the community and on its behalf but by its members (cf. Lev 24:14; Num 15:35; Deut 21:21; Josh 7:25).

11.anchor: Thus all Israel will hear and be afraid; no longer will they continue to do evil like this among you.
11.note: sn Some see in this statement an argument for the deterrent effect of capital punishment (Deut 17:13; 19:20; 21:21).

13.anchor: some evil people
13.note: tn Heb “men, sons of Belial.” The Hebrew term בְּלִיַּעַל (bÿliyya’al) has the idea of worthlessness, without morals or scruples (HALOT 133-34 s.v.). Cf. NAB, NRSV “scoundrels”; TEV, CEV “worthless people”; NLT “worthless rabble.”

13.anchor: have departed from among you to entice the inhabitants of their cities,
13.note: tc The LXX and Tg read “your” for the MT’s “their.”

13.anchor: saying, “Let’s go and serve other gods” (whom you have not known before).
13.note: tn The translation understands the relative clause as a statement by Moses, not as part of the quotation from the evildoers. See also v. 2.

14.anchor: You must investigate thoroughly and inquire carefully. If it is indeed true that such a disgraceful thing is being done among you,
14.note: tc Theodotian adds “in Israel,” perhaps to broaden the matter beyond the local village.

15.anchor: you must by all means
15.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, indicated in the translation by the words “by all means.” Cf. KJV, NASB “surely”; NIV “certainly.”

15.anchor: slaughter the inhabitants of that city with the sword; annihilate
15.note: tn Or “put under divine judgment. The Hebrew word (חֵרֶם, kherem) refers to placing persons or things under God’s judgment, usually to the extent of their complete destruction.Though primarily applied against the heathen, this severe judgment could also fall upon unrepentant Israelites (cf. the story of Achan in Josh 7). See also the note on the phrase “divine judgment” in Deut 2:34.

16.anchor: You must gather all of its plunder into the middle of the plaza
16.note: tn Heb “street.”

16.anchor: your God. It will be an abandoned ruin
16.note: tn Heb “mound”; NAB “a heap of ruins.” The Hebrew word תֵּל (tel) refers to this day to a ruin represented especially by a built-up mound of dirt or debris (cf. Tel Aviv, “mound of grain”).

17.anchor: You must not take for yourself anything that has been placed under judgment.
17.note: tn Or “anything that has been put under the divine curse”; Heb “anything of the ban” (cf. NASB). See note on the phrase “divine judgment” in Deut 2:34.

18.anchor: your God, keeping all his commandments that I am giving
18.note: tn Heb “commanding” (so NASB, NRSV).

18.anchor: you today and doing what is right
18.note: tc The LXX and Smr add “and good” to bring the phrase in line with a familiar cliché (cf. Deut 6:18; Josh 9:25; 2 Kgs 10:3; 2 Chr 14:1; etc.). This is an unnecessary and improper attempt to force a text into a preconceived mold.

18.anchor: before him.
18.note: tn Heb “in the eyes of the Lord your God.” See note on the word “him” in v. 3.

1.note: tn Heb “sons” (so NASB); TEV, NLT “people.”

1.anchor: your God. Do not cut yourselves or shave your forehead bald
1.note: sn Do not cut yourselves or shave your forehead bald. These were pagan practices associated with mourning the dead; they were not be imitated by God’s people (though they frequently were; cf. 1 Kgs 18:28; Jer 16:6; 41:5; 47:5; Hos 7:14 [LXX]; Mic 5:1). For other warnings against such practices see Lev 21:5; Jer 16:5.

2.anchor: For you are a people holy
2.note: tn Or “set apart.”

2.anchor: your God. He
2.note: tn Heb “The Lord.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

2.anchor: has chosen you to be his people, prized
2.note: tn Or “treasured.” The Hebrew term סְגֻלָּה (sÿgullah) describes Israel as God’s choice people, those whom he elected and who are most precious to him (cf. Exod 19:4-6; Deut 14:2; 26:18; 1 Chr 29:3; Ps 135:4; Eccl 2:8 Mal 3:17). See E. Carpenter, NIDOTTE 3:224.sn The Hebrew term translated “select” (and the whole verse) is reminiscent of the classic covenant text (Exod 19:4-6) which describes Israel’s entry into covenant relationship with the Lord. Israel must resist paganism and its trappings precisely because she is a holy people elected by the Lord from among the nations to be his instrument of world redemption (cf. Deut 7:6; 26:18; Ps 135:4; Mal 3:17; Titus 2:14; 1 Pet 2:9).

3.anchor: You must not eat any forbidden
3.note: tn The Hebrew word תּוֹעֵבָה (to’evah, “forbidden; abhorrent”) describes anything detestable to the Lord because of its innate evil or inconsistency with his own nature and character. See note on the word “abhorrent” in Deut 7:25. Cf. KJV “abominable”; NIV “detestable”; NRSV “abhorrent.”

5.anchor: the ibex,
5.note: tn The Hebrew term אַיָּל (’ayyal) may refer to a type of deer (cf. Arabic ’ayyal). Cf. NAB “the red deer.”

5.anchor: the gazelle,
5.note: tn The Hebrew term צְבִי (tsÿvi) is sometimes rendered “roebuck” (so KJV).

5.anchor: the deer,
5.note: tn The Hebrew term יַחְמוּר (yakhmur) may refer to a “fallow deer”; cf. Arabic yahmur (“deer”). Cf. NAB, NIV, NCV “roe deer”; NEB, NRSV, NLT “roebuck.”

5.anchor: the wild goat, the antelope,
5.note: tn The Hebrew term דִּישֹׁן (dishon) is a hapax legomenon. Its referent is uncertain but the animal is likely a variety of antelope (cf. NEB “white-rumped deer”; NIV, NRSV, NLT “ibex”).

5.anchor: the wild oryx,
5.note: tn The Hebrew term תְּאוֹ (tÿ’o; a variant is תּוֹא, to’) could also refer to another species of antelope. Cf. NEB “long-horned antelope”; NIV, NRSV “antelope.”

5.anchor: and the mountain sheep.
5.note: tn The Hebrew term זֶמֶר (zemer) is another hapax legomenon with the possible meaning “wild sheep.” Cf. KJV, ASV “chamois”; NEB “rock-goat”; NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT “mountain sheep.”

6.anchor: You may eat any animal that has hooves divided into two parts and that chews the cud.
6.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “among the animals.” This has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.

7.anchor: However, you may not eat the following animals among those that chew the cud or those that have divided hooves: the camel, the hare, and the rock badger.
7.note: tn The Hebrew term שָׁפָן (shafan) may refer to the “coney” (cf. KJV, NIV) or hyrax (“rock badger,” cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV, NLT).

8.anchor: Also the pig is ritually impure to you; though it has divided hooves,
8.note: tc The MT lacks (probably by haplography) the phrase וְשֹׁסַע שֶׁסַע פַּרְסָה (vÿshosa’ shesa’ parsah, “and is clovenfooted,” i.e., “has parted hooves”), a phrase found in the otherwise exact parallel in Lev 11:7. The LXX and Smr attest the longer reading here. The meaning is, however, clear without it.

12.anchor: These are the ones you may not eat: the eagle,
12.note: tn NEB “the griffon-vulture.”

12.anchor: the vulture,
12.note: tn The Hebrew term פֶּרֶס (peres) describes a large vulture otherwise known as the ossifrage (cf. KJV). This largest of the vultures takes its name from its habit of dropping skeletal remains from a great height so as to break the bones apart.

12.anchor: the black vulture,
12.note: tn The Hebrew term עָזְנִיָּה (’ozniyyah) may describe the black vulture (so NIV) or it may refer to the osprey (so NAB, NRSV, NLT), an eagle-like bird subsisting mainly on fish.

13.anchor: the kite, the black kite, the dayyah
13.note: tn The Hebrew term is דַּיָּה (dayyah). This, with the previous two terms (רָאָה [ra’ah] and אַיָּה [’ayyah]), is probably a kite of some species but otherwise impossible to specify.

15.anchor: the ostrich,
15.note: tn Or “owl.” The Hebrew term בַּת הַיַּעֲנָה (bat hayya’anah) is sometimes taken as “ostrich” (so ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV, NLT), but may refer instead to some species of owl (cf. KJV “owl”; NEB “desert-owl”; NIV “horned owl”).

15.anchor: the owl,
15.note: tn The Hebrew term תַּחְמָס (takhmas) is either a type of owl (cf. NEB “short-eared owl”; NIV “screech owl”) or possibly the nighthawk (so NRSV, NLT).

15.anchor: the seagull, the falcon
15.note: tn The Hebrew term נֵץ (nets) may refer to the falcon or perhaps the hawk (so NEB, NIV).

16.anchor: the little owl, the long-eared owl, the white owl,
16.note: tn The Hebrew term תִּנְשֶׁמֶת (tinshemet) may refer to a species of owl (cf. ASV “horned owl”; NASB, NIV, NLT “white owl”) or perhaps even to the swan (so KJV); cf. NRSV “water hen.”

17.anchor: the jackdaw,
17.note: tn The Hebrew term קָאַת (qa’at) may also refer to a type of owl (NAB, NIV, NRSV “desert owl”) or perhaps the pelican (so KJV, NASB, NLT).

19.anchor: and any winged thing on the ground are impure to you – they may not be eaten.
19.note: tc The MT reads the Niphal (passive) for expected Qal (“you [plural] must not eat”); cf. Smr, LXX. However, the harder reading should stand.

21.anchor: You may not eat any corpse, though you may give it to the resident foreigner who is living in your villages
21.note: tn Heb “gates” (also in vv. 27, 28, 29).

21.anchor: your God. Do not boil a young goat in its mother’s milk.
21.note: sn Do not boil a young goat in its mother’s milk. This strange prohibition – one whose rationale is unclear but probably related to pagan ritual – may seem out of place here but actually is not for the following reasons: (1) the passage as a whole opens with a prohibition against heathen mourning rites (i.e., death, vv. 1-2) and closes with what appear to be birth and infancy rites. (2) In the other two places where the stipulation occurs (Exod 23:19 and Exod 34:26) it similarly concludes major sections. (3) Whatever the practice signified it clearly was abhorrent to the Lord and fittingly concludes the topic of various breaches of purity and holiness as represented by the ingestion of unclean animals (vv. 3-21). See C. M. Carmichael, “On Separating Life and Death: An Explanation of Some Biblical Laws,” HTR 69 (1976): 1-7; J. Milgrom, “You Shall Not Boil a Kid In Its Mother’s Milk,” BRev 1 (1985): 48-55; R. J. Ratner and B. Zuckerman, “In Rereading the ‘Kid in Milk’ Inscriptions,” BRev 1 (1985): 56-58; and M. Haran, “Seething a Kid in its Mother’s Milk,” JJS 30 (1979): 23-35.

22.anchor: You must be certain to tithe
22.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, indicated in the translation by the words “be certain.”

23.anchor: your God you must eat from the tithe of your grain, your new wine,
23.note: tn This refers to wine in the early stages of fermentation. In its later stages it becomes wine (יַיִן, yayin) in its mature sense.

24.anchor: When he
24.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “He” in 14:2.

24.anchor: blesses you, if the
24.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “way is so far from you that you are unable to carry it because the.” These words have not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons, because they are redundant.

25.anchor: you may convert the tithe into money, secure the money,
25.note: tn Heb “bind the silver in your hand.”

1.anchor: You must not sacrifice to him
1.note: tn Heb “to the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 16:1.

1.anchor: a bull or sheep that has a blemish or any other defect, because that is considered offensive
1.note: tn The Hebrew word תּוֹעֵבָה (to’evah, “an abomination”; cf. NAB) describes persons, things, or practices offensive to ritual or moral order. See M. Grisanti, NIDOTTE 4:314-18; see also the note on the word “abhorrent” in Deut 7:25.

2.anchor: Suppose a man or woman is discovered among you – in one of your villages
2.note: tn Heb “gates.”

2.anchor: your God is giving you – who sins before the Lord your God
2.note: tn Heb “does the evil in the eyes of the Lord your God.”

3.anchor: by serving other gods and worshiping them – the sun,
3.note: tc The MT reads “and to the sun,” thus including the sun, the moon, and other heavenly spheres among the gods. However, Theodotion and Lucian read “or to the sun,” suggesting perhaps that the sun and the other heavenly bodies are not in the category of actual deities.

3.anchor: moon, or any other heavenly bodies which I have not permitted you to worship.
3.note: tn Heb “which I have not commanded you.” The words “to worship” are supplied in the translation for clarification.

4.anchor: When it is reported to you and you hear about it, you must investigate carefully. If it is indeed true that such a disgraceful thing
4.note: tn Heb “an abomination” (תּוֹעֵבָה); see note on the word “offensive” in v. 1.

5.anchor: you must bring to your city gates
5.note: tn Heb “gates.”

5.anchor: that man or woman who has done this wicked thing – that very man or woman – and you must stone that person to death.
5.note: tn Heb “stone them with stones so that they die” (KJV similar); NCV “throw stones at that person until he dies.”

7.note: tn Heb “the hand of the witnesses.” This means the two or three witnesses are to throw the first stones (cf. NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT).

7.anchor: must be first to begin the execution, and then all the people
7.note: tn Heb “the hand of all the people.”

8.anchor: If a matter is too difficult for you to judge – bloodshed,
8.note: tn Heb “between blood and blood.”

8.anchor: legal claim,
8.note: tn Heb “between claim and claim.”

8.anchor: or assault
8.note: tn Heb “between blow and blow.”

8.anchor: – matters of controversy in your villages
8.note: tn Heb “gates.”

8.anchor: your God chooses.
8.note: tc Several Greek recensions add “to place his name there,” thus completing the usual formula to describe the central sanctuary (cf. Deut 12:5, 11, 14, 18; 16:6). However, the context suggests that the local Levitical towns, and not the central sanctuary, are in mind.

12.anchor: The person who pays no attention
12.note: tn Heb “who acts presumptuously not to listen” (cf. NASB).

15.anchor: you must select without fail
15.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, indicated in the translation by the words “without fail.”

15.anchor: your God chooses. From among your fellow citizens
15.note: tn Heb “your brothers,” but not referring to siblings (cf. NIV “your brother Israelites”; NLT “a fellow Israelite”). The same phrase also occurs in v. 20.

15.anchor: you must appoint a king – you may not designate a foreigner who is not one of your fellow Israelites.
15.note: tn Heb “your brothers.” See the preceding note on “fellow citizens.”

16.anchor: Moreover, he must not accumulate horses for himself or allow the people to return to Egypt to do so,
16.note: tn Heb “in order to multiply horses.” The translation uses “do so” in place of “multiply horses” to avoid redundancy (cf. NAB, NIV).

17.anchor: Furthermore, he must not marry many
17.note: tn Heb “must not multiply” (cf. KJV, NASB); NLT “must not take many.”

18.anchor: When he sits on his royal throne he must make a copy of this law
18.note: tn Or “instruction.” The LXX reads here τὸ δευτερονόμιον τοῦτο (to deuteronomion touto, “this second law”). From this Greek phrase the present name of the book, “Deuteronomy” or “second law” (i.e., the second giving of the law), is derived. However, the MT’s expression מִשְׁנֶה הַתּוֹרָה הַזֹּאת (mishneh hattorah hazzo’t) is better rendered “copy of this law.” Here the term תּוֹרָה (torah) probably refers only to the book of Deuteronomy and not to the whole Pentateuch.

18.anchor: on a scroll
18.note: tn The Hebrew term סֵפֶר (sefer) means a “writing” or “document” and could be translated “book” (so KJV, ASV, TEV). However, since “book” carries the connotation of a modern bound book with pages (an obvious anachronism) it is preferable to render the Hebrew term “scroll” here and elsewhere.

20.anchor: Then he will not exalt himself above his fellow citizens or turn from the commandments to the right or left, and he and his descendants will enjoy many years ruling over his kingdom
20.note: tc Heb “upon his kingship.” Smr supplies כִּסֵא (kise’, “throne”) so as to read “upon the throne of his kingship.” This overliteralizes what is a clearly understood figure of speech.

1.anchor: At the end of every seven years you must declare a cancellation
1.note: tn The Hebrew term שְׁמִטָּת (shÿmittat), a derivative of the verb שָׁמַט (shamat, “to release; to relinquish”), refers to the cancellation of the debt and even pledges for the debt of a borrower by his creditor. This could be a full and final remission or, more likely, one for the seventh year only. See R. Wakely, NIDOTTE 4:155-60. Here the words “of debts” are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied. Cf. NAB “a relaxation of debts”; NASB, NRSV “a remission of debts.”

2.anchor: This is the nature of the cancellation: Every creditor must remit what he has loaned to another person;
2.note: tn Heb “his neighbor,” used idiomatically to refer to another person.

2.anchor: he must not force payment from his fellow Israelite,
2.note: tn Heb “his neighbor and his brother.” The words “his brother” may be a scribal gloss identifying “his neighbor” (on this idiom, see the preceding note) as a fellow Israelite (cf. v. 3). In this case the conjunction before “his brother” does not introduce a second category, but rather has the force of “that is.”

3.anchor: You may exact payment from a foreigner, but whatever your fellow Israelite
3.note: tn Heb “your brother.”

4.anchor: Lord
4.note: tc After the phrase “the Lord” many mss and versions add “your God” to complete the usual full epithet.

4.anchor: will surely bless
4.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “surely.” Note however, that the use is rhetorical, for the next verse attaches a condition.

4.anchor: you in the land that he
4.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

4.anchor: is giving you as an inheritance,
4.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “to possess.”

5.anchor: if you carefully obey
5.note: tn Heb “if listening you listen to the voice of.” The infinitive absolute is used for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “carefully.” The idiom “listen to the voice” means “obey.”

5.anchor: him
5.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 15:4.

5.anchor: by keeping
5.note: tn Heb “by being careful to do.”

5.anchor: all these commandments that I am giving
5.note: tn Heb “commanding” (so NASB); NAB “which I enjoin you today.”

7.note: tn Heb “one of your brothers” (so NASB); NAB “one of your kinsmen”; NRSV “a member of your community.” See the note at v. 2.
tn Heb “gates.”

7.anchor: your God is giving you should be poor, you must not harden your heart or be insensitive
7.note: tn Heb “withdraw your hand.” Cf. NIV “hardhearted or tightfisted” (NRSV and NLT similar).
tn Heb “from your needy brother.”

8.anchor: Instead, you must be sure to open your hand to him and generously lend
8.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute before both verbs. The translation indicates the emphasis with the words “be sure to” and “generously,” respectively.

8.anchor: him whatever he needs.
8.note: tn Heb “whatever his need that he needs for himself.” This redundant expression has been simplified in the translation for stylistic reasons.

9.anchor: Be careful lest you entertain the wicked thought that the seventh year, the year of cancellation of debts, has almost arrived, and your attitude
9.note: tn Heb “your eye.”

9.anchor: be wrong toward your impoverished fellow Israelite
9.note: tn Heb “your needy brother.”

9.anchor: and you do not lend
9.note: tn Heb “give” (likewise in v. 10).

9.anchor: against you and you will be regarded as having sinned.
9.note: tn Heb “it will be a sin to you.”

10.anchor: You must by all means lend
10.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “by all means.”

10.anchor: to him and not be upset by doing it,
10.note: tc Heb “your heart must not be grieved in giving to him.” The LXX and Orig add, “you shall surely lend to him sufficient for his need,” a suggestion based on the same basic idea in v. 8. Such slavish adherence to stock phrases is without warrant in most cases, and certainly here.

11.anchor: There will never cease to be some poor people in the land; therefore, I am commanding you to make sure you open
11.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “make sure.”

11.anchor: your hand to your fellow Israelites
11.note: tn Heb “your brother.”

12.anchor: If your fellow Hebrew
12.note: sn Elsewhere in the OT, the Israelites are called “Hebrews” (עִבְרִי, ’ivriy) by outsiders, rarely by themselves (cf. Gen 14:13; 39:14, 17; 41:12; Exod 1:15, 16, 19; 2:6, 7, 11, 13; 1 Sam 4:6; Jonah 1:9). Thus, here and in the parallel passage in Exod 21:2-6 the term עִבְרִי may designate non-Israelites, specifically a people well-known throughout the ancient Near East as ’apiru or habiru. They lived a rather vagabond lifestyle, frequently hiring themselves out as laborers or mercenary soldiers. While accounting nicely for the surprising use of the term here in an Israelite law code, the suggestion has against it the unlikelihood that a set of laws would address such a marginal people so specifically (as opposed to simply calling them aliens or the like). More likely עִבְרִי is chosen as a term to remind Israel that when they were “Hebrews,” that is, when they were in Egypt, they were slaves. Now that they are free they must not keep their fellow Israelites in economic bondage. See v. 15.

12.anchor: – whether male or female
12.note: tn Heb “your brother, a Hebrew (male) or Hebrew (female).”

12.anchor: – is sold to you and serves you for six years, then in the seventh year you must let that servant
12.note: tn Heb “him.” The singular pronoun occurs throughout the passage.

12.anchor: go free.
12.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “from you.”

14.anchor: You must supply them generously
14.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “generously.”

16.anchor: However, if the servant
16.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the indentured servant introduced in v. 12) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

16.anchor: says to you, “I do not want to leave
16.note: tn Heb “go out from.” The imperfect verbal form indicates the desire of the subject here.

17.anchor: you shall take an awl and pierce a hole through his ear to the door.
17.note: sn When the bondslave’s ear was drilled through to the door, the door in question was that of the master’s house. In effect, the bondslave is declaring his undying and lifelong loyalty to his creditor. The scar (or even hole) in the earlobe would testify to the community that the slave had surrendered independence and personal rights. This may be what Paul had in mind when he said “I bear on my body the marks of Jesus” (Gal 6:17).

18.anchor: You should not consider it difficult to let him go free, for he will have served you for six years, twice
18.note: tn The Hebrew term מִשְׁנֶה (mishneh, “twice”) could mean “equivalent to” (cf. NRSV) or, more likely, “double” (cf. NAB, NIV, NLT). The idea is that a hired worker would put in only so many hours per day whereas a bondslave was available around the clock.

19.anchor: You must set apart
19.note: tn Heb “sanctify” (תַּקְדִּישׁ, taqdish), that is, put to use on behalf of the Lord.

20.anchor: your God in the place he
20.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The translation uses a pronoun for stylistic reasons. See note on “he” in 15:4.

21.anchor: If they have any kind of blemish – lameness, blindness, or anything else
21.note: tn Heb “any evil blemish”; NASB “any (+ other NAB, TEV) serious defect.”

22.anchor: You may eat it in your villages,
22.note: tn Heb “in your gates.”

22.anchor: whether you are ritually impure or clean,
22.note: tc The LXX adds ἐν σοί (en soi, “among you”) to make clear that the antecedent is the people and not the animals. That is, the people, whether ritually purified or not, may eat such defective animals.

1.note: sn The month Abib, later called Nisan (Neh 2:1; Esth 3:7), corresponds to March-April in the modern calendar.

1.anchor: your God, for in that month
1.note: tn Heb “in the month Abib.” The demonstrative “that” has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons.
tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

2.anchor: You must sacrifice the Passover animal
2.note: tn Heb “sacrifice the Passover” (so NASB). The word “animal” has been supplied in the translation for clarity.

2.anchor: your God in the place where he
2.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in the previous verse.

4.anchor: There must not be a scrap of yeast within your land
4.note: tn Heb “leaven must not be seen among you in all your border.”

4.anchor: for seven days, nor can any of the meat you sacrifice on the evening of the first day remain until the next morning.
4.note: tn Heb “remain all night until the morning” (so KJV, ASV). This has been simplified in the translation for stylistic reasons.

5.anchor: You may not sacrifice the Passover in just any of your villages
5.note: tn Heb “gates.”

6.anchor: but you must sacrifice it
6.note: tn Heb “the Passover.” The translation uses a pronoun to avoid redundancy in English.

6.anchor: in the evening in
6.note: tc The MT reading אֶל (’el, “unto”) before “the place” should, following Smr, Syriac, Targums, and Vulgate, be omitted in favor of ב (bet; בַּמָּקוֹם, bammaqom), “in the place.”

6.anchor: the place where he
6.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 16:1.

7.note: tn The rules that governed the Passover meal are found in Exod 12:1-51, and Deut 16:1-8. The word translated “cook” (בָּשַׁל, bashal) here is translated “boil” in other places (e.g. Exod 23:19, 1 Sam 2:13-15). This would seem to contradict Exod 12:9 where the Israelites are told not to eat the Passover sacrifice raw or boiled. However, 2 Chr 35:13 recounts the celebration of a Passover feast during the reign of Josiah, and explains that the people “cooked (בָּשַׁל, bashal) the Passover sacrifices over the open fire.” The use of בָּשַׁל (bashal) with “fire” (אֵשׁ, ’esh) suggests that the word could be used to speak of boiling or roasting.

8.anchor: your God; you must not do any work on that day.
8.note: tn The words “on that day” are not in the Hebrew text; they are supplied in the translation for clarification (cf. TEV, NLT).

9.anchor: You must count seven weeks; you must begin to count them
9.note: tn Heb “the seven weeks.” The translation uses a pronoun to avoid redundancy in English.

10.anchor: Then you are to celebrate the Festival of Weeks
10.note: tn The Hebrew phrase חַג שָׁבֻעוֹת (khag shavu’ot) is otherwise known in the OT (Exod 23:16) as קָצִיר (qatsir, “harvest”) and in the NT as πεντηχοστή (penthcosth, “Pentecost”).

10.anchor: your God with the voluntary offering
10.note: tn Heb “the sufficiency of the offering of your hand.”

10.anchor: that you will bring, in proportion to how he
10.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 16:1.

11.anchor: You shall rejoice before him
11.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 16:1.

11.anchor: – you, your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, the Levites in your villages,
11.note: tn Heb “gates.”

13.anchor: You must celebrate the Festival of Temporary Shelters
13.note: tn The Hebrew phrase חַג הַסֻּכֹּת (khag hassukot, “festival of huts” or “festival of shelters”) is traditionally known as the Feast of Tabernacles. The rendering “booths” (cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV) is now preferable to the traditional “tabernacles” (KJV, ASV, NIV) in light of the meaning of the term סֻכָּה (sukkah, “hut; booth”), but “booths” are frequently associated with trade shows and craft fairs in contemporary American English. Clearer is the English term “shelters” (so NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT), but this does not reflect the temporary nature of the living arrangement. This feast was a commemoration of the wanderings of the Israelites after they left Egypt, suggesting that a translation like “temporary shelters” is more appropriate.

13.anchor: for seven days, at the time of the grain and grape harvest.
13.note: tn Heb “when you gather in your threshing-floor and winepress.”

14.anchor: You are to rejoice in your festival, you, your son, your daughter, your male and female slaves, the Levites, the resident foreigners, the orphans, and the widows who are in your villages.
14.note: tn Heb “in your gates.”

15.anchor: your God in the place he
15.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 16:1.

15.anchor: chooses, for he
15.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 16:1.

15.anchor: will bless you in all your productivity and in whatever you do;
15.note: tn Heb “in all the work of your hands” (so NASB, NIV); NAB, NRSV “in all your undertakings.”

16.anchor: your God in the place he chooses for the Festival of Unleavened Bread, the Festival of Weeks, and the Festival of Temporary Shelters; and they must not appear before him
16.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 16:1.

17.anchor: Every one of you must give as you are able,
17.note: tn Heb “a man must give according to the gift of his hand.” This has been translated as second person for stylistic reasons, in keeping with the second half of the verse, which is second person rather than third.

18.anchor: You must appoint judges and civil servants
18.note: tn The Hebrew term וְשֹׁטְרִים (vÿshoterim), usually translated “officers” (KJV, NCV) or “officials” (NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT), derives from the verb שֹׁטֵר (shoter, “to write”). The noun became generic for all types of public officials. Here, however, it may be appositionally epexegetical to “judges,” thus resulting in the phrase, “judges, that is, civil officers,” etc. Whoever the שֹׁטְרִים are, their task here consists of rendering judgments and administering justice.

18.anchor: for each tribe in all your villages
18.note: tn Heb “gates.”

18.anchor: your God is giving you, and they must judge the people fairly.
18.note: tn Heb “with judgment of righteousness”; ASV, NASB “with righteous judgment.”

19.anchor: You must not pervert justice or show favor. Do not take a bribe, for bribes blind the eyes of the wise and distort
19.note: tn Heb “twist, overturn”; NRSV “subverts the cause.”

19.anchor: the words of the righteous.
19.note: tn Or “innocent”; NRSV “those who are in the right”; NLT “the godly.”

20.anchor: You must pursue justice alone
20.note: tn Heb “justice, justice.” The repetition is emphatic; one might translate as “pure justice” or “unadulterated justice” (cf. NLT “true justice”).

21.anchor: You must not plant any kind of tree as a sacred Asherah pole
21.note: tn Heb “an Asherah, any tree.”sn Sacred Asherah pole. This refers to a tree (or wooden pole) dedicated to the worship of Asherah, wife/sister of El and goddess of fertility. See also Deut 7:5.

22.anchor: You must not erect a sacred pillar,
22.note: sn Sacred pillar. This refers to the stelae (stone pillars; the Hebrew term is מַצֵּבֹת, matsevot) associated with Baal worship, perhaps to mark a spot hallowed by an alleged visitation of the gods. See also Deut 7:5.

1.note: tn The MT places the terms “priests” and “Levites” in apposition, thus creating an epexegetical construction in which the second term qualifies the first, i.e., “Levitical priests.” This is a way of asserting their legitimacy as true priests. The Syriac renders “to the priest and to the Levite,” making a distinction between the two, but one that is out of place here.

sn Of his inheritance. This is a figurative way of speaking of the produce of the land the Lord will give to his people. It is the Lord’s inheritance, but the Levites are allowed to eat it since they themselves have no inheritance among the other tribes of Israel.

2.anchor: They
2.note: tn Heb “he” (and throughout the verse).

2.anchor: will have no inheritance in the midst of their fellow Israelites;
2.note: tn Heb “brothers,” but not referring to actual siblings. Cf. NASB “their countrymen”; NRSV “the other members of the community.”

3.anchor: This shall be the priests’ fair allotment
3.note: tn Heb “judgment”; KJV, NASB, NRSV “the priest’s due.”

4.anchor: You must give them the best of your
4.note: tn Heb “the firstfruits of your…” (so NIV).

5.anchor: your God has chosen them and their sons from all your tribes to stand
5.note: tc Smr and some Greek texts add “before the Lord your God” to bring the language into line with a formula found elsewhere (Deut 10:8; 2 Chr 29:11). This reading is not likely to be original, however.

5.anchor: and serve in his name
5.note: tn Heb “the name of the Lord.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

6.anchor: Suppose a Levite comes by his own free will
6.note: tn Heb “according to all the desire of his soul.”

6.anchor: from one of your villages, from any part of Israel where he is living,
6.note: tn Or “sojourning.” The verb used here refers to living temporarily in a place, not settling down.

8.anchor: He must eat the same share they do, despite any profits he may gain from the sale of his family’s inheritance.
8.note: tn Presumably this would not refer to a land inheritance, since that was forbidden to the descendants of Levi (v. 1). More likely it referred to some family possessions (cf. NIV, NCV, NRSV, CEV) or other private property (cf. NLT “a private source of income”), or even support sent by relatives (cf. TEV “whatever his family sends him”).

10.anchor: There must never be found among you anyone who sacrifices his son or daughter in the fire,
10.note: tn Heb “who passes his son or his daughter through the fire.” The expression “pass…through the fire” is probably a euphemism for human sacrifice (cf. NAB, NIV, TEV, NLT). See also Deut 12:31.

10.anchor: anyone who practices divination,
10.note: tn Heb “a diviner of divination” (קֹסֵם קְסָמִים, qosem qÿsamim). This was a means employed to determine the future or the outcome of events by observation of various omens and signs (cf. Num 22:7; 23:23; Josh 13:22; 1 Sam 6:2; 15:23; 28:8; etc.). See M. Horsnell, NIDOTTE 3:945-51.

10.anchor: an omen reader,
10.note: tn Heb “one who causes to appear” (מְעוֹנֵן, mÿ’onen). Such a practitioner was thought to be able to conjure up spirits or apparitions (cf. Lev 19:26; Judg 9:37; 2 Kgs 21:6; Isa 2:6; 57:3; Jer 27:9; Mic 5:11).

10.anchor: a soothsayer,
10.note: tn Heb “a seeker of omens” (מְנַחֵשׁ, mÿnakhesh). This is a subset of divination, one illustrated by the use of a “divining cup” in the story of Joseph (Gen 44:5).

10.anchor: a sorcerer,
10.note: tn Heb “a doer of sorcery” (מְכַשֵּׁף, mikhashef). This has to do with magic or the casting of spells in order to manipulate the gods or the powers of nature (cf. Lev 19:26-31; 2 Kgs 17:15b-17; 21:1-7; Isa 57:3, 5; etc.). See M. Horsnell, NIDOTTE 2:735-38.

11.anchor: one who casts spells,
11.note: tn Heb “a binder of binding” (חֹבֵר חָבֶר, khover khaver). The connotation is that of immobilizing (“binding”) someone or something by the use of magical words (cf. Ps 58:6; Isa 47:9, 12).

11.anchor: one who conjures up spirits,
11.note: tn Heb “asker of a [dead] spirit” (שֹׁאֵל אוֹב, sho’el ’ov). This is a form of necromancy (cf. Lev 19:31; 20:6; 1 Sam 28:8, 9; Isa 8:19; 19:3; 29:4).

11.anchor: a practitioner of the occult,
11.note: tn Heb “a knowing [or “familiar”] [spirit]” (יִדְּעֹנִי, yiddÿ’oniy), i.e., one who is expert in mantic arts (cf. Lev 19:31; 20:6, 27; 1 Sam 28:3, 9; 2 Kgs 21:6; Isa 8:19; 19:3).

11.anchor: or a necromancer.
11.note: tn Heb “a seeker of the dead.” This is much the same as “one who conjures up spirits” (cf. 1 Sam 28:6-7).

12.anchor: and because of these detestable things
12.note: tn Heb “these abhorrent things.” The repetition is emphatic. For stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy, the same term used earlier in the verse has been translated “detestable” here.

12.anchor: your God is about to drive them out
12.note: tn The translation understands the Hebrew participial form as having an imminent future sense here.

15.anchor: your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you – from your fellow Israelites;
15.note: tc The MT expands here on the usual formula by adding “from among you” (cf. Deut 17:15; 18:18; Smr; a number of Greek texts). The expansion seems to be for the purpose of emphasis, i.e., the prophet to come must be not just from Israel but an Israelite by blood.tn “from your brothers,” but not referring to actual siblings. Cf. NAB “from among your own kinsmen”; NASB “from your countrymen”; NRSV “from among your own people.” A similar phrase occurs in v. 17.

16.anchor: our
16.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the collective singular in this verse: “my God…lest I die.”

19.anchor: I will personally hold responsible
19.note: tn Heb “will seek from him”; NAB “I myself will make him answer for it”; NRSV “will hold accountable.”

19.anchor: anyone who then pays no attention to the words that prophet
19.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the prophet mentioned in v. 18) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

20.anchor: “But if any prophet presumes to speak anything in my name that I have not authorized
20.note: tn Or “commanded” (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV).

21.anchor: Now if you say to yourselves,
21.note: tn Heb “in your heart.”
tn Heb “know the word which the Lord has not spoken.” The issue here is not understanding the meaning of the message, but distinguishing a genuine prophetic word from a false one.

22.anchor: whenever a prophet speaks in my
22.note: tn Heb “the Lord’s.” See note on the word “his” in v. 5.

22.anchor: name and the prediction
22.note: tn Heb “the word,” but a predictive word is in view here. Cf. NAB “his oracle.”

22.anchor: is not fulfilled,
22.note: tn Heb “does not happen or come to pass.”

22.anchor: then I have
22.note: tn Heb “the Lord has.” See note on the word “his” in v. 5.

22.anchor: not spoken it;
22.note: tn Heb “that is the word which the Lord has not spoken.”

1.anchor: When you go to war against your enemies and see chariotry
1.note: tn Heb “horse and chariot.”
tn Heb “people.”

2.anchor: As you move forward for battle, the priest
2.note: sn The reference to the priest suggests also the presence of the ark of the covenant, the visible sign of God’s presence. The whole setting is clearly that of “holy war” or “Yahweh war,” in which God himself takes initiative as the true commander of the forces of Israel (cf. Exod 14:14-18; 15:3-10; Deut 3:22; 7:18-24; 31:6, 8).

2.anchor: will approach and say to the soldiers,
2.note: tn Heb “and he will say to the people.” Cf. NIV, NCV, CEV “the army”; NRSV, NLT “the troops.”

4.anchor: your God goes with you to fight on your behalf against your enemies to give you victory.”
4.note: tn Or “to save you” (so KJV, NASB, NCV); or “to deliver you.”

5.anchor: Moreover, the officers are to say to the troops,
5.note: tn Heb “people” (also in vv. 8, 9).

5.anchor: “Who among you
5.note: tn Heb “Who [is] the man” (also in vv. 6, 7, 8).

5.anchor: has built a new house and not dedicated
5.note: tn The Hebrew term חָנַךְ (khanakh) occurs elsewhere only with respect to the dedication of Solomon’s temple (1 Kgs 8:63 = 2 Chr 7:5). There it has a religious connotation which, indeed, may be the case here as well. The noun form (חָנֻכָּה, khanukah) is associated with the consecration of the great temple altar (2 Chr 7:9) and of the postexilic wall of Jerusalem (Neh 12:27). In Maccabean times the festival of Hanukkah was introduced to celebrate the rededication of the temple following its desecration by Antiochus IV Epiphanes (1 Macc 4:36-61).

5.anchor: it? He may go home, lest he die in battle and someone else
5.note: tn Heb “another man.”

7.note: tn Heb “Who [is] the man.”

8.anchor: In addition, the officers are to say to the troops, “Who among you is afraid and fainthearted? He may go home so that he will not make his fellow soldier’s
8.note: tn Heb “his brother’s.”

8.anchor: heart as fearful
8.note: tn Heb “melted.”

9.anchor: Then, when the officers have finished speaking,
9.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “to the people,” but this phrase has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.

9.anchor: they must appoint unit commanders
9.note: tn Heb “princes of hosts.”

11.anchor: If it accepts your terms
11.note: tn Heb “if it answers you peace.”

11.anchor: and submits to you, all the people found in it will become your slaves.
11.note: tn Heb “become as a vassal and will serve you.” The Hebrew term translated slaves (מַס, mas) refers either to Israelites who were pressed into civil service, especially under Solomon (1 Kgs 5:27; 9:15, 21; 12:18), or (as here) to foreigners forced as prisoners of war to become slaves to Israel. The Gibeonites exemplify this type of servitude (Josh 9:3-27; cf. Josh 16:10; 17:13; Judg 1:28, 30-35; Isa 31:8; Lam 1:1).

13.anchor: your God will deliver it over to you
13.note: tn Heb “to your hands.”

16.anchor: As for the cities of these peoples that
16.note: tn The antecedent of the relative pronoun is “cities.”

16.anchor: your God is going to give you as an inheritance, you must not allow a single living thing
16.note: tn Heb “any breath.”

17.anchor: Instead you must utterly annihilate them
17.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation seeks to reflect with “utterly.” Cf. CEV “completely wipe out.”sn The Hebrew verb refers to placing persons or things so evil and/or impure as to be irredeemable under God’s judgment, usually to the extent of their complete destruction. See also the note on the phrase “the divine judgment” in Deut 2:34.

17.anchor: – the Hittites,
17.note: sn Hittite. The center of Hittite power was in Anatolia (central modern Turkey). In the Late Bronze Age (1550-1200 b.c.) they were at their zenith, establishing outposts and colonies near and far. Some elements were obviously in Canaan at the time of the Conquest (1400-1350 b.c.).

17.anchor: Amorites,
17.note: sn Amorite. Originally from the upper Euphrates region (Amurru), the Amorites appear to have migrated into Canaan beginning in 2200 b.c. or thereabouts.

17.anchor: Canaanites,
17.note: sn Canaanite. These were the indigenous peoples of the land of Palestine, going back to the beginning of recorded history (ca. 3000 b.c.). The OT identifies them as descendants of Ham (Gen 10:6), the only Hamites to have settled north and east of Egypt.

17.anchor: Perizzites,
17.note: sn Perizzite. This probably refers to a subgroup of Canaanites (Gen 13:7; 34:30).

17.anchor: Hivites,
17.note: sn Hivite. These are usually thought to be the same as the Hurrians, a people well-known in ancient Near Eastern texts. They are likely identical to the Horites (see note on “Horites” in Deut 2:12).

17.anchor: and Jebusites
17.note: tc The LXX adds “Girgashites” here at the end of the list in order to list the full (and usual) complement of seven (see note on “seven” in Deut 7:1).sn Jebusite. These people inhabited the hill country, particularly in and about Jerusalem (cf. Num 13:29; Josh 15:8; 2 Sam 5:6; 24:16).

18.anchor: so that they cannot teach you all the abhorrent ways they worship
18.note: tn Heb “to do according to all their abominations which they do for their gods.”

19.anchor: If you besiege a city for a long time while attempting to capture it,
19.note: tn Heb “to fight against it to capture it.”

19.anchor: you must not chop down its trees,
19.note: tn Heb “you must not destroy its trees by chopping them with an iron” (i.e., an ax).

19.anchor: for you may eat fruit
19.note: tn Heb “you may eat from them.” The direct object is not expressed; the word “fruit” is supplied in the translation for clarity.

19.anchor: from them and should not cut them down. A tree in the field is not human that you should besiege it!
19.note: tn Heb “to go before you in siege.”

20.anchor: However, you may chop down any tree you know is not suitable for food,
20.note: tn Heb “however, a tree which you know is not a tree for food you may destroy and cut down.”

20.anchor: and you may use it to build siege works
20.note: tn Heb “[an] enclosure.” The term מָצוֹר (matsor) may refer to encircling ditches or to surrounding stagings. See R. de Vaux, Ancient Israel, 238.

1.note: tn Heb “slain [one].” The term חָלָל (khalal) suggests something other than a natural death (cf. Num 19:16; 23:24; Jer 51:52; Ezek 26:15; 30:24; 31:17-18).
tn The Hebrew text includes “to possess it,” but this has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.

1.anchor: and no one knows who killed
1.note: tn Heb “struck,” but in context a fatal blow is meant; cf. NLT “who committed the murder.”

2.anchor: your elders and judges must go out and measure how far it is to the cities in the vicinity of the corpse.
2.note: tn Heb “surrounding the slain [one].”

3.anchor: Then the elders of the city nearest to the corpse
3.note: tn Heb “slain [one].”

4.anchor: and bring the heifer down to a wadi with flowing water,
4.note: tn The combination “a wadi with flowing water” is necessary because a wadi (נַחַל, nakhal) was ordinarily a dry stream or riverbed. For this ritual, however, a perennial stream must be chosen so that there would be fresh, rushing water.

4.anchor: to a valley that is neither plowed nor sown.
4.note: sn The unworked heifer, fresh stream, and uncultivated valley speak of ritual purity – of freedom from human contamination.

5.anchor: Then the Levitical priests
5.note: tn Heb “the priests, the sons of Levi.”

5.anchor: your God has chosen them to serve him and to pronounce blessings in his name,
5.note: tn Heb “in the name of the Lord.” See note on Deut 10:8. The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

5.anchor: and to decide
5.note: tn Heb “by their mouth.”

5.anchor: every judicial verdict
5.note: tn Heb “every controversy and every blow.”

6.anchor: and all the elders of that city nearest the corpse
6.note: tn Heb “slain [one].”

6.anchor: must wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the valley.
6.note: tn Heb “wadi,” a seasonal watercourse through a valley.

7.anchor: Then they must proclaim, “Our hands have not spilled this blood, nor have we
7.note: tn Heb “our eyes.” This is a figure of speech known as synecdoche in which the part (the eyes) is put for the whole (the entire person).
tn Heb “seen”; the implied object (the crime committed) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

8.anchor: Do not blame
8.note: tn Heb “Atone for.”

8.anchor: , and do not hold them accountable for the bloodshed of an innocent person.”
8.note: tn Heb “and do not place innocent blood in the midst of your people Israel.”

9.anchor: In this manner you will purge out the guilt of innocent blood from among you, for you must do what is right before
9.note: tn Heb “in the eyes of” (so ASV, NASB, NIV).

10.anchor: your God allows you to prevail
10.note: tn Heb “gives him into your hands.”

11.anchor: if you should see among them
11.note: tn Heb “the prisoners.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy.

12.anchor: you may bring her back to your house. She must shave her head,
12.note: sn This requirement for the woman to shave her head may symbolize the putting away of the old life and customs in preparation for being numbered among the people of the Lord. The same is true for the two following requirements.

13.anchor: discard the clothing she was wearing when captured,
13.note: tn Heb “she is to…remove the clothing of her captivity” (cf. NASB); NRSV “discard her captive’s garb.”

13.anchor: and stay
13.note: tn Heb “sit”; KJV, NASB, NRSV “remain.”

13.anchor: in your house, lamenting for her father and mother for a full month. After that you may have sexual relations
13.note: tn Heb “go unto,” a common Hebrew euphemism for sexual relations.

14.anchor: If you are not pleased with her, then you must let her go
14.note: sn Heb “send her off.” The Hebrew term שִׁלַּחְתָּה (shillakhtah) is a somewhat euphemistic way of referring to divorce, the matter clearly in view here (cf. Deut 22:19, 29; 24:1, 3; Jer 3:1; Mal 2:16). This passage does not have the matter of divorce as its principal objective, so it should not be understood as endorsing divorce generally. It merely makes the point that if grounds for divorce exist (see Deut 24:1-4), and then divorce ensues, the husband could in no way gain profit from it.

14.anchor: where she pleases. You cannot in any case sell
14.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates by the words “in any case.”

14.anchor: her;
14.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “for money.” This phrase has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.

14.anchor: you must not take advantage of
14.note: tn Or perhaps “must not enslave her” (cf. ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); Heb “[must not] be tyrannical over.”

14.anchor: her, since you have already humiliated
14.note: sn You have humiliated her. Since divorce was considered rejection, the wife subjected to it would “lose face” in addition to the already humiliating event of having become a wife by force (21:11-13). Furthermore, the Hebrew verb translated “humiliated” here (עָנָה, ’anah), commonly used to speak of rape (cf. Gen 34:2; 2 Sam 13:12, 14, 22, 32; Judg 19:24), likely has sexual overtones as well. The woman may not be enslaved or abused after the divorce because it would be double humiliation (see also E. H. Merrill, Deuteronomy [NAC], 291).

15.anchor: Suppose a man has two wives, one whom he loves more than the other,
15.note: tn Heb “one whom he loves and one whom he hates.” For the idea of שָׂנֵא (sane’, “hate”) meaning to be rejected or loved less (cf. NRSV “disliked”), see Gen 29:31, 33; Mal 1:2-3. Cf. A. Konkel, NIDOTTE 3:1256-60.

15.anchor: and they both
15.note: tn Heb “both the one whom he loves and the one whom he hates.” On the meaning of the phrase “one whom he loves and one whom he hates” see the note on the word “other” earlier in this verse. The translation has been simplified for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy.

16.anchor: In the day he divides his inheritance
16.note: tn Heb “when he causes his sons to inherit what is his.”

16.anchor: he must not appoint as firstborn the son of the favorite wife in place of the other
16.note: tn Heb “the hated.”

17.anchor: Rather, he must acknowledge the son of the less loved
17.note: tn See note on the word “other” in v. 15.

17.anchor: wife as firstborn and give him the double portion
17.note: tn Heb “measure of two.” The Hebrew expression פִּי שְׁנַיִם (piy shÿnayim) suggests a two-thirds split; that is, the elder gets two parts and the younger one part. Cf. 2 Kgs 2:9; Zech 13:8. The practice is implicit in Isaac’s blessing of Jacob (Gen 25:31-34) and Jacob’s blessing of Ephraim (Gen 48:8-22).

17.anchor: of all he has, for that son is the beginning of his father’s procreative power
17.note: tn Heb “his generative power” (אוֹן, ’on; cf. HALOT 22 s.v.). Cf. NAB “the first fruits of his manhood”; NRSV “the first issue of his virility.”

18.anchor: If a person has a stubborn, rebellious son who pays no attention to his father or mother, and they discipline him to no avail,
18.note: tn Heb “and he does not listen to them.”

20.anchor: They must declare to the elders
20.note: tc The LXX and Smr read “to the men,” probably to conform to this phrase in v. 21. However, since judicial cases were the responsibility of the elders in such instances (cf. Deut 19:12; 21:3, 6; 25:7-8) the reading of the MT is likely original and correct here.

21.anchor: Then all the men of his city must stone him to death. In this way you will purge out
21.note: tn The Hebrew term בִּעַרְתָּה (bi’artah), here and elsewhere in such contexts (cf. Deut 13:5; 17:7, 12; 19:19; 21:9), suggests God’s anger which consumes like fire (thus בָעַר, ba’ar, “to burn”). See H. Ringgren, TDOT 2:203-4.

21.anchor: wickedness from among you, and all Israel
21.note: tc Some LXX traditions read הַנִּשְׁאָרִים (hannish’arim, “those who remain”) for the MT’s יִשְׂרָאֵל (yisra’el, “Israel”), understandable in light of Deut 19:20. However, the more difficult reading found in the MT is more likely original.

22.anchor: If a person commits a sin punishable by death and is executed, and you hang the corpse
22.note: tn Heb “him.”

23.anchor: his body must not remain all night on the tree; instead you must make certain you bury
23.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates by “make certain.”

23.anchor: him that same day, for the one who is left exposed
23.note: tn Heb “hung,” but this could convey the wrong image in English (hanging with a rope as a means of execution). Cf. NCV “anyone whose body is displayed on a tree.”

23.anchor: on a tree is cursed by God.
23.note: sn The idea behind the phrase cursed by God seems to be not that the person was impaled because he was cursed but that to leave him exposed there was to invite the curse of God upon the whole land. Why this would be so is not clear, though the rabbinic idea that even a criminal is created in the image of God may give some clue (thus J. H. Tigay, Deuteronomy [JPSTC], 198). Paul cites this text (see Gal 3:13) to make the point that Christ, suspended from a cross, thereby took upon himself the curse associated with such a display of divine wrath and judgment (T. George, Galatians [NAC], 238-39).

1.anchor: your God and do what he requires; keep his statutes, ordinances, and commandments
1.note: tn This collocation of technical terms for elements of the covenant text lends support to its importance and also signals a new section of paraenesis in which Moses will exhort Israel to covenant obedience. The Hebrew term מִשְׁמָרוֹת (mishmarot, “obligations”) sums up the three terms that follow – חֻקֹּת (khuqot), מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishppatim), and מִצְוֹת (mitsot).

2.anchor: Bear in mind today that I am not speaking
2.note: tn Heb “that not.” The words “I am speaking” have been supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.

2.anchor: to your children who have not personally experienced the judgments
2.note: tn Heb “who have not known and who have not seen the discipline of the Lord.” The collocation of the verbs “know” and “see” indicates that personal experience (knowing by seeing) is in view. The term translated “discipline” (KJV, ASV “chastisement”) may also be rendered “instruction,” but vv. 2b-6 indicate that the referent of the term is the various acts of divine judgment the Israelites had witnessed.

2.anchor: your God, which revealed
2.note: tn The words “which revealed” have been supplied in the translation to show the logical relationship between the terms that follow and the divine judgments. In the Hebrew text the former are in apposition to the latter.

2.anchor: his greatness, strength, and power.
2.note: tn Heb “his strong hand and his stretched-out arm.”

3.anchor: They did not see
3.note: tn In the Hebrew text vv. 2-7 are one long sentence. For stylistic reasons the English translation divides the passage into three sentences. To facilitate this stylistic decision the words “They did not see” are supplied at the beginning of both v. 3 and v. 5, and “I am speaking” at the beginning of v. 7.

3.anchor: the awesome deeds he performed
3.note: tn Heb “his signs and his deeds which he did” (NRSV similar). The collocation of “signs” and “deeds” indicates that these acts were intended to make an impression on observers and reveal something about God’s power (cf. v. 2b). The word “awesome” has been employed to bring out the force of the word “signs” in this context.

4.anchor: or what he did to the army of Egypt, including their horses and chariots, when he made the waters of the Red Sea
4.note: tn Heb “Reed Sea.” “Reed Sea” (or “Sea of Reeds”) is a more accurate rendering of the Hebrew expression יָם סוּף (yam suf), traditionally translated “Red Sea.” See note on the term “Red Sea” in Exod 13:18.

4.anchor: overwhelm them while they were pursuing you and he
4.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

4.anchor: annihilated them.
4.note: tn Heb “and the Lord destroyed them to this day” (cf. NRSV); NLT “he has kept them devastated to this very day.” The translation uses the verb “annihilated” to indicate the permanency of the action.

5.anchor: They did not see
5.note: tn See note on these same words in v. 3.

6.anchor: or what he did to Dathan and Abiram,
6.note: sn Dathan and Abiram. These two (along with others) had challenged Moses’ leadership in the desert with the result that the earth beneath them opened up and they and their families disappeared (Num 16:1-3, 31-35).

6.anchor: sons of Eliab the Reubenite,
6.note: tn Or “the descendant of Reuben”; Heb “son of Reuben.”

6.anchor: when the earth opened its mouth in the middle of the Israelite camp
