6.note: tn Heb “in the midst of all Israel” (so KJV, ASV, NRSV); NASB “among all Israel.” In the Hebrew text these words appear at the end of the verse, but they are logically connected with the verbs. To make this clear the translation places the phrase after the first verb.

6.anchor: and swallowed them, their families,
6.note: tn Heb “their houses,” referring to all who lived in their household. Cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT “households.”

6.anchor: their tents, and all the property they brought with them.
6.note: tn Heb “and all the substance which was at their feet.”

7.note: tn On the addition of these words in the translation see note on “They did not see” in v. 3.

8.anchor: Now pay attention to all the commandments
8.note: tn Heb “the commandment.” The singular מִצְוָה (mitsvah, “commandment”) speaks here as elsewhere of the whole corpus of covenant stipulations in Deuteronomy (cf. 6:1, 25; 7:11; 8:1).

8.anchor: I am giving
8.note: tn Heb “commanding” (so NASB, NRSV). For stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy, “giving” has been used in the translation (likewise in vv. 13, 27).

8.anchor: you today, so that you may be strong enough to enter and possess the land where you are headed,
8.note: tn Heb “which you are crossing over there to possess it.”

9.anchor: promised to give to your ancestors
9.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in v. 21).

10.anchor: For the land where you are headed
10.note: tn Heb “you are going there to possess it”; NASB “into which you are about to cross to possess it”; NRSV “that you are crossing over to occupy.”

10.anchor: is not like the land of Egypt from which you came, a land where you planted seed and which you irrigated by hand
10.note: tn Heb “with your foot” (so NASB, NLT). There is a two-fold significance to this phrase. First, Egypt had no rain so water supply depended on human efforts at irrigation. Second, the Nile was the source of irrigation waters but those waters sometimes had to be pumped into fields and gardens by foot-power, perhaps the kind of machinery (Arabic shaduf) still used by Egyptian farmers (see C. Aldred, The Egyptians, 181). Nevertheless, the translation uses “by hand,” since that expression is the more common English idiom for an activity performed by manual labor.

11.anchor: Instead, the land you are crossing the Jordan to occupy
11.note: tn Heb “which you are crossing over there to possess it.”

11.anchor: is one of hills and valleys, a land that drinks in water from the rains,
11.note: tn Heb “rain of heaven.”

12.anchor: your God looks after.
12.note: tn Heb “seeks.” The statement reflects the ancient belief that God (Baal in Canaanite thinking) directly controlled storms and rainfall.

12.anchor: He is constantly attentive to it
12.note: tn Heb “the eyes of the Lord your God are continually on it” (so NIV); NASB, NRSV “always on it.” sn Constantly attentive to it. This attention to the land by the Lord is understandable in light of the centrality of the land in the Abrahamic covenant (cf. Gen 12:1, 7; 13:15; 15:7, 16, 18; 17:8; 26:3).

12.anchor: from the beginning to the end of the year.
12.note: sn From the beginning to the end of the year. This refers to the agricultural year that was marked by the onset of the heavy rains, thus the autumn. See note on the phrase “the former and the latter rains” in v. 14.

13.anchor: Now, if you pay close attention
13.note: tn Heb “if hearing, you will hear.” The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute to emphasize the verbal idea. The translation renders this emphasis with the word “close.”

13.anchor: to my commandments that I am giving you today and love
13.note: tn Again, the Hebrew term אָהַב (’ahav) draws attention to the reciprocation of divine love as a condition or sign of covenant loyalty (cf. Deut 6:5).

13.anchor: your God and serve him with all your mind and being,
13.note: tn Heb “heart and soul” or “heart and being.” See note on the word “being” in Deut 6:5.

14.anchor: then he promises,
14.note: tn The words “he promises” do not appear in the Hebrew text but are needed in the translation to facilitate the transition from the condition (v. 13) to the promise and make it clear that the Lord is speaking the words of vv. 14-15.

14.anchor: “I will send rain for your land
14.note: tn Heb “the rain of your land.” In this case the genitive (modifying term) indicates the recipient of the rain.

14.anchor: in its season, the autumn and the spring rains,
14.note: sn The autumn and the spring rains. The “former” (יוֹרֶה, yoreh) and “latter” (מַלְקוֹשׁ, malqosh) rains come in abundance respectively in September/October and March/April. Planting of most crops takes place before the former rains fall and the harvests follow the latter rains.

15.anchor: I will provide pasture
15.note: tn Heb “grass in your field.”

16.anchor: Make sure you do not turn away to serve and worship other gods!
16.note: tn Heb “Watch yourselves lest your heart turns and you turn aside and serve other gods and bow down to them.”

17.anchor: will erupt
17.note: tn Heb “will become hot”; KJV, NASB, NRSV “will be kindled”; NAB “will flare up”; NIV, NLT “will burn.”

17.anchor: against you and he will close up the sky
17.note: tn Or “heavens.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.

17.anchor: so that it does not rain. The land will not yield its produce, and you will soon be removed
17.note: tn Or “be destroyed”; NAB, NIV “will soon perish.”

17.anchor: from the good land that the Lord
17.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 11:4.

18.anchor: Fix these words of mine into your mind and being,
18.note: tn Heb “heart and soul” or “heart and being.” See note on the word “being” in Deut 6:5.

18.anchor: and tie them as a reminder on your hands and let them be symbols
18.note: tn On the Hebrew term טוֹטָפֹת (totafot, “reminders”), cf. Deut 6:4-9.

19.anchor: Teach them to your children and speak of them as you sit in your house, as you walk along the road,
19.note: tn Or “as you are away on a journey” (cf. NRSV, TEV, NLT); NAB “at home and abroad.”

21.anchor: promised to give to your ancestors, like the days of heaven itself.
21.note: tn Heb “like the days of the heavens upon the earth,” that is, forever.

22.anchor: For if you carefully observe all of these commandments
22.note: tn Heb “this commandment.” See note at Deut 5:30.

22.anchor: I am giving you
22.note: tn Heb “commanding you to do it.” For stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy, “giving” has been used in the translation and “to do it” has been left untranslated.

22.anchor: your God, live according to his standards,
22.note: tn Heb “walk in all his ways” (so KJV, NIV); TEV “do everything he commands.”

23.anchor: then he
23.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

24.anchor: Every place you set your foot
24.note: tn Heb “the sole of your foot walks.” The placing of the foot symbolizes conquest and dominion, especially on land or on the necks of enemies (cf. Deut 1:36; Ps 7:13; Isa 63:3 Hab 3:19; Zech 9:13). See E. H. Merrill, NIDOTTE 1:992.

24.anchor: will be yours; your border will extend from the desert to Lebanon and from the River (that is, the Euphrates) as far as the Mediterranean Sea.
24.note: tn Heb “the after sea,” that is, the sea behind one when one is facing east, which is the normal OT orientation. Cf. ASV “the hinder sea.”

26.anchor: Take note – I am setting before you today a blessing and a curse:
26.note: sn A blessing and a curse. Every extant treaty text of the late Bronze Age attests to a section known as the “blessings and curses,” the former for covenant loyalty and the latter for covenant breach. Blessings were promised rewards for obedience; curses were threatened judgments for disobedience. In the Book of Deuteronomy these are fully developed in 27:1–28:68. Here Moses adumbrates the whole by way of anticipation.

27.anchor: the blessing if you take to heart
27.note: tn Heb “listen to,” that is, obey.

28.anchor: and the curse if you pay no attention
28.note: tn Heb “do not listen to,” that is, do not obey.
tn Heb “the commandments of the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

28.anchor: commandments and turn from the way I am setting before

28.note: tn Heb “walk after”; NIV “by following”; NLT “by worshiping.” This is a violation of the first commandment, the most serious of the covenant violations (Deut 5:6-7).

29.anchor: your God brings you into the land you are to possess, you must pronounce the blessing on Mount Gerizim and the curse on Mount Ebal.
29.note: sn Mount Gerizim…Mount Ebal. These two mountains are near the ancient site of Shechem and the modern city of Nablus. The valley between them is like a great amphitheater with the mountain slopes as seating sections. The place was sacred because it was there that Abraham pitched his camp and built his first altar after coming to Canaan (Gen 12:6). Jacob also settled at Shechem for a time and dug a well from which Jesus once requested a drink of water (Gen 33:18-20; John 4:5-7). When Joshua and the Israelites finally brought Canaan under control they assembled at Shechem as Moses commanded and undertook a ritual of covenant reaffirmation (Josh 8:30-35; 24:1, 25). Half the tribes stood on Mt. Gerizim and half on Mt. Ebal and in antiphonal chorus pledged their loyalty to the Lord before Joshua and the Levites who stood in the valley below (Josh 8:33; cf. Deut 27:11-13).

30.anchor: Are they not across the Jordan River,
30.note: tn The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.

30.anchor: toward the west, in the land of the Canaanites who live in the Arabah opposite Gilgal
30.note: sn Gilgal. From a Hebrew verb root גָלַל (galal, “to roll”) this place name means “circle” or “rolling,” a name given because God had “rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you” (Josh 5:9). It is perhaps to be identified with Khirbet el-Metjir, 1.2 mi (2 km) northeast of OT Jericho.

tc The MT plural “oaks” (אֵלוֹנֵי, ’eloney) should probably be altered (with many Greek texts) to the singular “oak” (אֵלוֹן, ’elon; cf. NRSV) in line with the only other occurrence of the phrase (Gen 12:6). The Syriac, Tg. Ps.-J. read mmrá, confusing this place with the “oaks of Mamre” near Hebron (Gen 13:18). Smr also appears to confuse “Moreh” with “Mamre” (reading mwr’, a combined form), adding the clarification mwl shkm (“near Shechem”) apparently to distinguish it from Mamre near Hebron.

1.note: tn Heb “you must not see,” but, if translated literally into English, the statement is misleading.
tn Heb “brother’s” (also later in this verse). In this context it is not limited to one’s siblings, however; cf. NAB “your kinsman’s.”

1.anchor: ox or sheep going astray, do not ignore it;
1.note: tn Heb “hide yourself.”

1.anchor: you must return it without fail
1.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with the words “without fail.”

2.anchor: If the owner
2.note: tn Heb “your brother” (also later in this verse).

2.anchor: does not live
2.note: tn Heb “is not.” The idea of “residing” is implied.

2.anchor: near you or you do not know who the owner is,
2.note: tn Heb “and you do not know him.”

2.anchor: then you must corral the animal
2.note: tn Heb “it”; the referent (the ox or sheep mentioned in v. 1) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

3.anchor: You shall do the same to his donkey, his clothes, or anything else your neighbor
3.note: tn Heb “your brother” (also in v. 4).

3.anchor: has lost and you have found; you must not refuse to get involved.
3.note: tn Heb “you must not hide yourself.”

4.anchor: When you see
4.note: tn Heb “you must not see.” See note at 22:1.

4.anchor: your neighbor’s donkey or ox fallen along the road, do not ignore it;
4.note: tn Heb “and (must not) hide yourself from them.”

4.anchor: instead, you must be sure
4.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “be sure.”

4.anchor: to help him get the animal on its feet again.
4.note: tn Heb “help him to lift them up.” In keeping with English style the singular is used in the translation, and the referent (“the animal”) has been specified for clarity.

5.anchor: A woman must not wear men’s clothing,
5.note: tn Heb “a man’s clothing.”

5.anchor: nor should a man dress up in women’s clothing, for anyone who does this is offensive
5.note: tn The Hebrew term תּוֹעֵבָה (to’evah, “offense”) speaks of anything that runs counter to ritual or moral order, especially (in the OT) to divine standards. Cross-dressing in this covenant context may suggest homosexuality, fertility cult ritual, or some other forbidden practice.

6.anchor: If you happen to notice a bird’s nest along the road, whether in a tree or on the ground, and there are chicks or eggs with the mother bird sitting on them,
6.note: tn Heb “and the mother sitting upon the chicks or the eggs.”

6.anchor: you must not take the mother from the young.
6.note: tn Heb “sons,” used here in a generic sense for offspring.

7.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation seeks to reflect with “be sure.”

8.anchor: If you build a new house, you must construct a guard rail
8.note: tn Or “a parapet” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV); KJV “a battlement”; NLT “a barrier.”

8.anchor: around your roof to avoid being culpable
8.note: tn Heb “that you not place bloodshed in your house.”

9.anchor: You must not plant your vineyard with two kinds of seed; otherwise the entire yield, both of the seed you plant and the produce of the vineyard, will be defiled.
9.note: tn Heb “set apart.” The verb קָדַשׁ (qadash) in the Qal verbal stem (as here) has the idea of being holy or being treated with special care. Some take the meaning as “be off-limits, forfeited,” i.e., the total produce of the vineyard, both crops and grapes, have to be forfeited to the sanctuary (cf. Exod 29:37; 30:29; Lev 6:18, 27; Num 16:37-38; Hag 2:12).

11.anchor: You must not wear clothing made with wool and linen meshed together.
11.note: tn The Hebrew term שַׁעַטְנֵז (sha’atnez) occurs only here and in Lev 19:19. HALOT 1610-11 s.v. takes it to be a contraction of words (שַׁשׁ [shash, “headdress”] + עַטְנַז [’atnaz, “strong”]). BDB 1043 s.v. שַׁעַטְנֵז offers the translation “mixed stuff” (cf. NEB “woven with two kinds of yarn”; NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT “woven together”). The general meaning is clear even if the etymology is not.

12.anchor: You shall make yourselves tassels
12.note: tn Heb “twisted threads” (גְּדִלִים, gÿdilim) appears to be synonymous with צִיצִת (tsitsit) which, in Num 15:38, occurs in a passage instructing Israel to remember the covenant. Perhaps that is the purpose of the tassels here as well. Cf. KJV, ASV “fringes”; NAB “twisted cords.”

13.anchor: Suppose a man marries a woman, has sexual relations with her,
13.note: tn Heb “goes to her,” a Hebrew euphemistic idiom for sexual relations.

13.anchor: and then rejects
13.note: tn Heb “hate.” See note on the word “other” in Deut 21:15. Cf. NAB “comes to dislike”; NASB “turns against”; TEV “decides he doesn’t want.”

14.anchor: accusing her of impropriety
14.note: tn Heb “deeds of things”; NRSV “makes up charges against her”; NIV “slanders her.”

14.anchor: and defaming her reputation
14.note: tn Heb “brings against her a bad name”; NIV “gives her a bad name.”

14.anchor: by saying, “I married this woman but when I had sexual relations
14.note: tn Heb “drew near to her.” This is another Hebrew euphemism for having sexual relations.

15.anchor: Then the father and mother of the young woman must produce the evidence of virginity
15.note: sn In light of v. 17 this would evidently be blood-stained sheets indicative of the first instance of intercourse. See E. H. Merrill, Deuteronomy (NAC), 302-3.

16.anchor: The young woman’s father must say to the elders, “I gave my daughter to this man and he has rejected
16.note: tn Heb “hated.” See note on the word “other” in Deut 21:15.

17.anchor: Moreover, he has raised accusations of impropriety by saying, ‘I discovered your daughter was not a virgin,’ but this is the evidence of my daughter’s virginity!” The cloth must then be spread out
17.note: tn Heb “they will spread the garment.”

18.anchor: The elders of that city must then seize the man and punish
18.note: tn Heb “discipline.”

19.anchor: They will fine him one hundred shekels of silver and give them to the young woman’s father, for the man who made the accusation
19.note: tn Heb “for he”; the referent (the man who made the accusation) has been specified in the translation to avoid confusion with the young woman’s father, the last-mentioned male.

19.anchor: ruined the reputation
19.note: tn Heb “brought forth a bad name.”

21.anchor: the men of her city must bring the young woman to the door of her father’s house and stone her to death, for she has done a disgraceful thing
21.note: tn The Hebrew term נְבָלָה (nÿvalah) means more than just something stupid. It refers to a moral lapse so serious as to jeopardize the whole covenant community (cf. Gen 34:7; Judg 19:23; 20:6, 10; Jer 29:23). See C. Pan, NIDOTTE 3:11-13. Cf. NAB “she committed a crime against Israel.”

21.anchor: in Israel by behaving like a prostitute while living in her father’s house. In this way you will purge
21.note: tn Heb “burn.” See note on Deut 21:21.

22.anchor: If a man is caught having sexual relations with
22.note: tn Heb “lying with” (so KJV, NASB), a Hebrew idiom for sexual relations.

22.anchor: a married woman
22.note: tn Heb “a woman married to a husband.”

22.anchor: both the man who had relations with the woman and the woman herself must die; in this way you will purge
22.note: tn Heb “burn.” See note on the phrase “purge out” in Deut 21:21.

23.anchor: If a virgin is engaged to a man and another man meets
23.note: tn Heb “finds.”

23.anchor: her in the city and has sexual relations with
23.note: tn Heb “lies with.”

24.anchor: you must bring the two of them to the gate of that city and stone them to death, the young woman because she did not cry out though in the city and the man because he violated
24.note: tn Heb “humbled.”

24.anchor: his neighbor’s fiancÃ©e;
24.note: tn Heb “wife.”

24.anchor: in this way you will purge
24.note: tn Heb “burn.” See note on the phrase “purge out” in Deut 21:21.

25.anchor: But if the man came across
25.note: tn Heb “found,” also in vv. 27, 28.

25.anchor: the engaged woman in the field and overpowered her and raped
25.note: tn Heb “lay with” here refers to a forced sexual relationship, as the accompanying verb “seized” (חָזַק, khazaq) makes clear.

25.anchor: her, then only the rapist
25.note: tn Heb “the man who lay with her, only him.”

26.anchor: You must not do anything to the young woman – she has done nothing deserving of death. This case is the same as when someone attacks another person
26.note: tn Heb “his neighbor.”

27.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the man who attacked the woman) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

28.anchor: Suppose a man comes across a virgin who is not engaged and overpowers and rapes

30.note: sn Beginning with 22:30, the verse numbers through 23:25 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 22:30 ET = 23:1 HT, 23:1 ET = 23:2 HT, 23:2 ET = 23:3 HT, etc., through 23:25 ET = 23:26 HT. With 24:1 the verse numbers in the ET and HT are again the same.
tn Heb “take.” In context this refers to marriage, as in the older English expression “take a wife.”
sn This presupposes either the death of the father or their divorce since it would be impossible for one to marry his stepmother while his father was still married to her.

30.anchor: wife and in this way dishonor his father.
30.note: tn Heb “uncover his father’s skirt” (so ASV, NASB). This appears to be a circumlocution for describing the dishonor that would come to a father by having his own son share his wife’s sexuality (cf. NAB, NIV “dishonor his father’s bed”).

1.note: tn Heb “bruised by crushing,” which many English versions take to refer to crushed testicles (NAB, NRSV, NLT); TEV “who has been castrated.”

tn Heb “cut off with respect to the penis”; KJV, ASV “hath his privy member cut off”; English versions vary in their degree of euphemism here; cf. NAB, NRSV, TEV, NLT “penis”; NASB “male organ”; NCV “sex organ”; CEV “private parts”; NIV “emasculated by crushing or cutting.”

sn The Hebrew term translated “assembly” (קָהָל, qahal) does not refer here to the nation as such but to the formal services of the tabernacle or temple. Since emasculated or other sexually abnormal persons were commonly associated with pagan temple personnel, the thrust here may be primarily polemical in intent. One should not read into this anything having to do with the mentally and physically handicapped as fit to participate in the life and ministry of the church.

2.anchor: A person of illegitimate birth
2.note: tn Or “a person born of an illegitimate marriage.”

2.anchor: ; to the tenth generation no one related to him may do so.
2.note: tn Heb “enter the assembly of the Lord.” The phrase “do so” has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

3.anchor: An Ammonite or Moabite
3.note: sn An Ammonite or Moabite. These descendants of Lot by his two daughters (cf. Gen 19:30-38) were thereby the products of incest and therefore excluded from the worshiping community. However, these two nations also failed to show proper hospitality to Israel on their way to Canaan (v. 4).

3.anchor: ; to the tenth generation none of their descendants shall ever
3.note: tn The Hebrew term translated “ever” (עַד־עוֹלָם, ’ad-’olam) suggests that “tenth generation” (vv. 2, 3) also means “forever.” However, in the OT sense “forever” means not “for eternity” but for an indeterminate future time. See A. Tomasino, NIDOTTE 3:346.

3.anchor: do so,
3.note: tn Heb “enter the assembly of the Lord.” The phrase “do so” has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

4.anchor: for they did not meet you with food and water on the way as you came from Egypt, and furthermore, they hired
4.note: tn Heb “hired against you.”

5.anchor: your God refused to listen to Balaam and changed
5.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God changed.” The phrase “the Lord your God” has not been included in the translation here for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy. Moreover, use of the pronoun “he” could create confusion regarding the referent (the Lord or Balaam).

5.anchor: your God loves
5.note: tn The verb אָהַב (’ahav, “love”) here and commonly elsewhere in the Book of Deuteronomy speaks of God’s elective grace toward Israel. See note on the word “loved” in Deut 4:37.

7.anchor: You must not hate an Edomite, for he is your relative;
7.note: tn Heb “brother.”

7.anchor: you must not hate an Egyptian, for you lived as a foreigner
7.note: tn Heb “sojourner.”

8.anchor: Children of the third generation born to them
8.note: sn Concessions were made to the Edomites and Egyptians (as compared to the others listed in vv. 1-6) because the Edomites (i.e., Esauites) were full “brothers” of Israel and the Egyptians had provided security and sustenance for Israel for more than four centuries.

9.anchor: When you go out as an army against your enemies, guard yourselves against anything impure.
9.note: tn Heb “evil.” The context makes clear that this is a matter of ritual impurity, not moral impurity, so it is “evil” in the sense that it disbars one from certain religious activity.

10.anchor: If there is someone among you who is impure because of some nocturnal emission,
10.note: tn Heb “nocturnal happening.” The Hebrew term קָרֶה (qareh) merely means “to happen” so the phrase here is euphemistic (a “night happening”) for some kind of bodily emission such as excrement or semen. Such otherwise normal physical functions rendered one ritually unclean whether accidental or not. See Lev 15:16-18; 22:4.

12.anchor: You are to have a place outside the camp to serve as a latrine.
12.note: tn Heb “so that one may go outside there.” This expression is euphemistic.

13.anchor: You must have a spade among your other equipment and when you relieve yourself
13.note: tn Heb “sit.” This expression is euphemistic.

13.anchor: outside you must dig a hole with the spade
13.note: tn Heb “with it”; the referent (the spade mentioned at the beginning of the verse) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

13.anchor: and then turn and cover your excrement.
13.note: tn Heb “what comes from you,” a euphemism.

14.anchor: your God walks about in the middle of your camp to deliver you and defeat
14.note: tn Heb “give [over] your enemies.”

14.anchor: your enemies for you. Therefore your camp should be holy, so that he does not see anything indecent
14.note: tn Heb “nakedness of a thing”; NLT “any shameful thing.” The expression עֶרְוַת דָּבָר (’ervat davar) refers specifically to sexual organs and, by extension, to any function associated with them. There are some aspects of human life that are so personal and private that they ought not be publicly paraded. Cultically speaking, even God is offended by such impropriety (cf. Gen 9:22-23; Lev 18:6-12, 16-19; 20:11, 17-21). See B. Seevers, NIDOTTE 3:528-30.

15.anchor: You must not return an escaped slave to his master when he has run away to you.
15.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “from his master,” but this would be redundant in English style.

16.anchor: Indeed, he may live among you in any place he chooses, in whichever of your villages
16.note: tn Heb “gates.”

17.anchor: There must never be a sacred prostitute
17.note: tn The Hebrew term translated “sacred prostitute” here (קְדֵשָׁה [qÿdeshah], from קַדֵשׁ [qadesh, “holy”]; cf. NIV “shrine prostitute”; NASB “cult prostitute”; NRSV, TEV, NLT “temple prostitute”) refers to the pagan fertility cults that employed female and male prostitutes in various rituals designed to evoke agricultural and even human fecundity (cf. Gen 38:21-22; 1 Kgs 14:24; 15:12; 22:47; 2 Kgs 23:7; Hos 4:14). The Hebrew term for a regular, noncultic (i.e., “secular”) female prostitute is זוֹנָה (zonah).

17.anchor: among the young women
17.note: tn Heb “daughters.”

17.anchor: of Israel nor a sacred male prostitute
17.note: tn The male cultic prostitute was called קָדֵשׁ (qadesh; see note on the phrase “sacred prostitute” earlier in this verse). The colloquial Hebrew term for a “secular” male prostitute (i.e., a sodomite) is the disparaging epithet כֶּלֶב (kelev, “dog”) which occurs in the following verse (cf. KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB).

17.anchor: among the young men
17.note: tn Heb “sons.”

18.anchor: You must never bring the pay of a female prostitute
18.note: tn Here the Hebrew term זוֹנָה (zonah) refers to a noncultic (i.e., “secular”) female prostitute; see note on the phrase “sacred prostitute” in v. 17.

18.anchor: or the wage of a male prostitute
18.note: tn Heb “of a dog.” This is the common Hebrew term for a noncultic (i.e., “secular”) male prostitute. See note on the phrase “sacred male prostitute” in v. 17.

19.anchor: You must not charge interest on a loan to your fellow Israelite,
19.note: tn Heb “to your brother” (likewise in the following verse). Since this is not limited to actual siblings, “fellow Israelite” is used in the translation (cf. NAB, NASB “countrymen”).

21.anchor: your God you must not delay in fulfilling it, for otherwise he
21.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

21.anchor: will surely
21.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which is reflected in the translation by “surely.”

21.anchor: hold you accountable as a sinner.
21.note: tn Heb “and it will be a sin to you”; NIV, NCV, NLT “be guilty of sin.”

24.anchor: When you enter the vineyard of your neighbor you may eat as many grapes as you please,
24.note: tn Heb “grapes according to your appetite, your fullness.”

24.anchor: but you must not take away any in a container.
24.note: tn Heb “in your container”; NAB, NIV “your basket.”

25.anchor: When you go into the ripe grain fields of your neighbor you may pluck off the kernels with your hand,
25.note: sn For the continuation of these practices into NT times see Matt 12:1-8; Mark 2:23-28; Luke 6:1-5.

1.anchor: Then Moses and the elders of Israel commanded the people: “Pay attention to all the commandments
1.note: tn Heb “the whole commandment.” See note at 5:31.
tn Heb “commanding”; NAB “which I enjoin on you today” (likewise in v. 10).

2.anchor: When you cross the Jordan River
2.note: tn The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.

2.anchor: your God is giving you, you must erect great stones and cover
2.note: tn Heb “plaster” (so KJV, ASV; likewise in v. 4). In the translation “cover” has been used for stylistic reasons.

3.anchor: , the God of your ancestors,
3.note: tn Heb “fathers.”

4.anchor: So when you cross the Jordan you must erect on Mount Ebal
4.note: tc Smr reads “Mount Gerizim” for the MT reading “Mount Ebal” to justify the location of the Samaritan temple there in the postexilic period. This reading is patently self-serving and does not reflect the original. In the NT when the Samaritan woman of Sychar referred to “this mountain” as the place of worship for her community she obviously had Gerizim in mind (cf. John 4:20).

10.anchor: You must obey him
10.note: tn Heb “listen to the voice of the Lord your God.” Here “listen” (NAB “hearken”) means “obey” (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

12.anchor: “The following tribes
12.note: tn The word “tribes” has been supplied here and in the following verse in the translation for clarity.

14.anchor: “The Levites will call out to every Israelite
14.note: tn Heb “Israelite man.”

15.anchor: ‘Cursed is the one
15.note: tn Heb “man,” but in a generic sense here.

15.anchor: who makes a carved or metal image – something abhorrent
15.note: tn The Hebrew term translated here “abhorrent” (תּוֹעֵבָה, to’evah) speaks of attitudes and/or behaviors so vile as to be reprehensible to a holy God. See note on the word “abhorrent” in Deut 7:25.

15.anchor: , the work of the craftsman
15.note: tn Heb “craftsman’s hands.”

15.anchor: – and sets it up in a secret place.’ Then all the people will say, ‘Amen!’
15.note: tn Or “So be it!” The term is an affirmation expressing agreement with the words of the Levites.

16.anchor: ‘Cursed
16.note: tn The Levites speak again at this point; throughout this pericope the Levites pronounce the curse and the people respond with “Amen.”

16.anchor: is the one who disrespects
16.note: tn The Hebrew term קָלָה (qalah) means to treat with disdain or lack of due respect (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV “dishonors”; NLT “despises”). It is the opposite of כָּבֵד (kaved, “to be heavy,” that is, to treat with reverence and proper deference). To treat a parent lightly is to dishonor him or her and thus violate the fifth commandment (Deut 5:16; cf. Exod 21:17).

20.anchor: ‘Cursed is the one who has sexual relations with
20.note: tn Heb “who lies with” (so NASB, NRSV); also in vv. 22, 23. This is a Hebrew idiom for having sexual relations (cf. NIV “who sleeps with”; NLT “who has sexual intercourse with”).

20.anchor: his father’s former wife,
20.note: tn See note at Deut 22:30.

20.anchor: for he dishonors his father.’
20.note: tn Heb “he uncovers his father’s skirt” (NASB similar). See note at Deut 22:30.

21.anchor: ‘Cursed is the one who commits bestiality.’
21.note: tn Heb “lies with any animal” (so NASB, NRSV). “To lie with” is a Hebrew euphemism for having sexual relations with someone (or in this case, some animal).

24.anchor: ‘Cursed is the one who kills
24.note: tn Or “strikes down” (so NRSV).

1.anchor: your God destroys the nations whose land he
1.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

2.anchor: you must set apart for yourselves three cities
2.note: sn These three cities, later designated by Joshua, were Kedesh of Galilee, Shechem, and Hebron (Josh 20:7-9).

3.anchor: You shall build a roadway and divide into thirds the whole extent
3.note: tn Heb “border.”

4.anchor: Now this is the law pertaining to one who flees there in order to live,
4.note: tn Heb “and this is the word pertaining to the one who kills who flees there and lives.”

4.anchor: if he has accidentally killed another
4.note: tn Heb “who strikes his neighbor without knowledge.”

4.anchor: without hating him at the time of the accident.
4.note: tn Heb “yesterday and a third (day)” (likewise in v. 6). The point is that there was no animosity between the two parties at the time of the accident and therefore no motive for the killing. Cf. NAB “had previously borne no malice”; NRSV “had not been at enmity before.”

5.anchor: Suppose he goes with someone else
5.note: tn Heb “his neighbor” (so NAB, NIV); NASB “his friend.”

5.anchor: to the forest to cut wood and when he raises the ax
5.note: tn Heb “and he raises his hand with the iron.”

5.anchor: to cut the tree, the ax head flies loose
5.note: tn Heb “the iron slips off.”

5.anchor: from the handle and strikes
5.note: tn Heb “finds.”

5.anchor: his fellow worker
5.note: tn Heb “his neighbor.”

5.anchor: so hard that he dies. The person responsible
5.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the person responsible for his friend’s death) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

5.anchor: may then flee to one of these cities to save himself.
5.note: tn Heb “and live.”

6.anchor: Otherwise the blood avenger will chase after the killer in the heat of his anger, eventually overtake him,
6.note: tn Heb “and overtake him, for the road is long.”

6.anchor: and kill him,
6.note: tn Heb “smite with respect to life,” that is, fatally.

6.anchor: though this is not a capital case
6.note: tn Heb “no judgment of death.”

8.anchor: your God enlarges your borders as he promised your ancestors
8.note: tn Heb “fathers.”

8.anchor: and gives you all the land he pledged to them,
8.note: tn Heb “he said to give to your ancestors.” The pronoun has been used in the translation instead for stylistic reasons.

9.anchor: and then you are careful to observe all these commandments
9.note: tn Heb “all this commandment.” This refers here to the entire covenant agreement of the Book of Deuteronomy as encapsulated in the Shema (Deut 6:4-5).

9.anchor: I am giving
9.note: tn Heb “commanding”; NAB “which I enjoin on you today.”

9.anchor: your God and to always walk in his ways), then you must add three more cities
9.note: sn You will add three more cities. Since these are alluded to nowhere else and thus were probably never added, this must be a provision for other cities of refuge should they be needed (cf. v. 8). See P. C. Craigie, Deuteronomy (NICOT), 267.

10.anchor: You must not shed innocent blood
10.note: tn Heb “innocent blood must not be shed.” The Hebrew phrase דָּם נָקִי (dam naqiy) means the blood of a person to whom no culpability or responsibility adheres because what he did was without malice aforethought (HALOT 224 s.v דָּם 4.b).

10.anchor: your God is giving you as an inheritance, for that would make you guilty.
10.note: tn Heb “and blood will be upon you” (cf. KJV, ASV); NRSV “thereby bringing bloodguilt upon you.”

11.anchor: However, suppose a person hates someone else
11.note: tn Heb “his neighbor.”

11.anchor: and stalks him, attacks him, kills him,
11.note: tn Heb “rises against him and strikes him fatally.”

12.anchor: The elders of his own city must send for him and remove him from there to deliver him over to the blood avenger
12.note: tn The גֹאֵל הַדָּם (go’el haddam, “avenger of blood”) would ordinarily be a member of the victim’s family who, after due process of law, was invited to initiate the process of execution (cf. Num 35:16-28). See R. Hubbard, NIDOTTE 1:789-94.

13.anchor: You must not pity him, but purge out the blood of the innocent
13.note: sn Purge out the blood of the innocent. Because of the corporate nature of Israel’s community life, the whole community shared in the guilt of unavenged murder unless and until vengeance occurred. Only this would restore spiritual and moral equilibrium (Num 35:33).

14.anchor: You must not encroach on your neighbor’s property,
14.note: tn Heb “border.” Cf. NRSV “You must not move your neighbor’s boundary marker.”

14.anchor: which will have been defined
14.note: tn Heb “which they set off from the beginning.”

14.anchor: your God is giving you.
14.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “to possess it.” This phrase has been left untranslated to avoid redundancy.

15.anchor: A single witness may not testify
15.note: tn Heb “rise up” (likewise in v. 16).

15.anchor: against another person for any trespass or sin that he commits. A matter may be legally established
15.note: tn Heb “may stand.”

16.anchor: If a false
16.note: tn Heb “violent” (חָמָס, khamas). This is a witness whose motivation from the beginning is to do harm to the accused and who, therefore, resorts to calumny and deceit. See I. Swart and C. VanDam, NIDOTTE 2:177-80.

16.anchor: witness testifies against another person and accuses him of a crime,
16.note: tn Or “rebellion.” Rebellion against God’s law is in view (cf. NAB “of a defection from the law”).

17.anchor: , that is, before the priests and judges
17.note: tn The appositional construction (“before the Lord, that is, before the priests and judges”) indicates that these human agents represented the Lord himself, that is, they stood in his place (cf. Deut 16:18-20; 17:8-9).

18.anchor: The judges will thoroughly investigate the matter, and if the witness should prove to be false and to have given false testimony against the accused,
18.note: tn Heb “his brother” (also in the following verse).

19.anchor: you must do to him what he had intended to do to the accused. In this way you will purge
19.note: tn Heb “you will burn out” (בִּעַרְתָּ, bi’arta). Like a cancer, unavenged sin would infect the whole community. It must, therefore, be excised by the purging out of its perpetrators who, presumably, remained unrepentant (cf. Deut 13:6; 17:7, 12; 21:21; 22:21-22, 24; 24:7).

21.anchor: You must not show pity; the principle will be a life for a life, an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth, a hand for a hand, and a foot for a foot.
21.note: sn This kind of justice is commonly called lex talionis or “measure for measure” (cf. Exod 21:23-25; Lev 24:19-20). It is likely that it is the principle that is important and not always a strict application. That is, the punishment should fit the crime and it may do so by the payment of fines or other suitable and equitable compensation (cf. Exod 22:21; Num 35:31). See T. S. Frymer-Kensky, “Tit for Tat: The Principle of Equal Retribution in Near Eastern and Biblical Law,” BA 43 (1980): 230-34.

1.anchor: If a man marries a woman and she does not please him because he has found something offensive
1.note: tn Heb “nakedness of a thing.” The Hebrew phrase עֶרְוַת דָּבָר (’ervat davar) refers here to some gross sexual impropriety (see note on “indecent” in Deut 23:14). Though the term usually has to do only with indecent exposure of the genitals, it can also include such behavior as adultery (cf. Lev 18:6-18; 20:11, 17, 20-21; Ezek 22:10; 23:29; Hos 2:10).

2.anchor: When she has left him
2.note: tn Heb “his house.”

3.anchor: If the second husband rejects
3.note: tn Heb “hates.” See note on the word “other” in Deut 21:15.

3.anchor: her and then divorces her,
3.note: tn Heb “writes her a document of divorce.”

4.anchor: her first husband who divorced her is not permitted to remarry
4.note: tn Heb “to return to take her to be his wife.”
sn The issue here is not divorce and its grounds per se but prohibition of remarriage to a mate whom one has previously divorced.

4.anchor: You must not bring guilt on the land
4.note: tn Heb “cause the land to sin” (so KJV, ASV).

5.anchor: When a man is newly married, he need not go into
5.note: tn Heb “go out with.”

5.anchor: the army nor be obligated in any way; he must be free to stay at home for a full year and bring joy to
5.note: tc For the MT’s reading Piel שִׂמַּח (simmakh, “bring joy to”), the Syriac and others read שָׂמַח (samakh, “enjoy”).

6.anchor: One must not take either lower or upper millstones as security on a loan, for that is like taking a life itself as security.
6.note: sn Taking millstones as security on a loan would amount to taking the owner’s own life in pledge, since the millstones were the owner’s means of earning a living and supporting his family.

7.anchor: If a man is found kidnapping a person from among his fellow Israelites,
7.note: tn Heb “from his brothers, from the sons of Israel.” The terms “brothers” and “sons of Israel” are in apposition; the second defines the first more specifically.

7.anchor: and regards him as mere property
7.note: tn Or “and enslaves him.”
tn Heb “that thief.”

7.anchor: must die. In this way you will purge
7.note: tn Heb “burn.” See note on the word “purge” in Deut 19:19.

8.anchor: Be careful during an outbreak of leprosy to follow precisely
8.note: tn Heb “to watch carefully and to do.”

9.anchor: your God did to Miriam
9.note: sn What the Lord your God did to Miriam. The reference is to Miriam’s having contracted leprosy because of her intemperate challenge to Moses’ leadership (Num 12:1-15). The purpose for the allusion here appears to be the assertion of the theocratic leadership of the priests who, like Moses, should not be despised.

10.anchor: When you make any kind of loan to your neighbor, you may not go into his house to claim what he is offering as security.
10.note: tn Heb “his pledge.” This refers to something offered as pledge of repayment, i.e., as security for the debt.

11.anchor: You must stand outside and the person to whom you are making the loan will bring out to you what he is offering as security.
11.note: tn Heb “his pledge.”

12.anchor: If the person is poor you may not use what he gives you as security for a covering.
12.note: tn Heb “may not lie down in his pledge.” What is in view is the use of clothing as guarantee for the repayment of loans, a matter already addressed elsewhere (Deut 23:19-20; 24:6; cf. Exod 22:25-26; Lev 25:35-37). Cf. NAB “you shall not sleep in the mantle he gives as a pledge”; NRSV “in the garment given you as the pledge.”

13.anchor: You must by all means
13.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation seeks to reflect with “by all means.”

13.anchor: return to him at sunset the item he gave you as security so that he may sleep in his outer garment and bless you for it; it will be considered a just
13.note: tn Or “righteous” (so NIV, NLT).

14.anchor: You must not oppress a lowly and poor servant, whether one from among your fellow Israelites
14.note: tn Heb “your brothers,” but not limited only to actual siblings; cf. NASB “your (+ own NAB) countrymen.”

14.anchor: or from the resident foreigners who are living in your land and villages.
14.note: tn Heb “who are in your land in your gates.” The word “living” is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.

16.anchor: Fathers must not be put to death for what their children
16.note: tn Heb “sons” (so NASB; twice in this verse). Many English versions, including the KJV, read “children” here.

19.anchor: Whenever you reap your harvest in your field and leave some unraked grain there,
19.note: tn Heb “in the field.”

19.anchor: your God may bless all the work you do.
19.note: tn Heb “of your hands.” This law was later applied in the story of Ruth who, as a poor widow, was allowed by generous Boaz to glean in his fields (Ruth 2:1-13).

20.anchor: When you beat your olive tree you must not repeat the procedure;
20.note: tn Heb “knock down after you.”

21.anchor: When you gather the grapes of your vineyard you must not do so a second time;
21.note: tn Heb “glean after you.”

1.note: tn Heb “and it will come to pass that.”

2.anchor: your God is giving you, place it in a basket, and go to the place where he
2.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

2.anchor: chooses to locate his name.
2.note: sn The place where he chooses to locate his name. This is a circumlocution for the central sanctuary, first the tabernacle and later the Jerusalem temple. See Deut 12:1-14 and especially the note on the word “you” in v. 14.

3.anchor: your
3.note: tc For the MT reading “your God,” certain LXX mss have “my God,” a contextually superior rendition followed by some English versions (e.g., NAB, NASB, TEV). Perhaps the text reflects dittography of the kaf (כ) at the end of the word with the following preposition כִּי (ki).

3.anchor: Lord
3.note: tc The Syriac adds “your God” to complete the usual formula.

3.anchor: promised
3.note: tn Heb “swore on oath.”

3.anchor: to our ancestors
3.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 7, 15).

4.anchor: The priest will then take the basket from you
4.note: tn Heb “your hand.”

5.anchor: , “A wandering
5.note: tn Though the Hebrew term אָבַד (’avad) generally means “to perish” or the like (HALOT 2-3 s.v.; BDB 1-2 s.v.; cf. KJV “a Syrian ready to perish”), a meaning “to go astray” or “to be lost” is also attested. The ambivalence in the Hebrew text is reflected in the versions where LXX Vaticanus reads ἀπέβαλεν (apebalen, “lose”) for a possibly metathesized reading found in Alexandrinus, Ambrosianus, ἀπέλαβεν (apelaben, “receive”); others attest κατέλειπεν (kateleipen, “leave, abandon”). “Wandering” seems to suit best the contrast with the sedentary life Israel would enjoy in Canaan (v. 9) and is the meaning followed by many English versions.

5.anchor: Aramean
5.note: sn A wandering Aramean. This is a reference to Jacob whose mother Rebekah was an Aramean (Gen 24:10; 25:20, 26) and who himself lived in Aram for at least twenty years (Gen 31:41-42).

5.anchor: was my ancestor,
5.note: tn Heb “father.”

5.anchor: and he went down to Egypt and lived there as a foreigner with a household few in number,
5.note: tn Heb “sojourned there few in number.” The words “with a household” have been supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons and for clarity.

7.anchor: , the God of our ancestors, and he
7.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 26:2.

8.anchor: brought us out of Egypt with tremendous strength and power,
8.note: tn Heb “by a powerful hand and an extended arm.” These are anthropomorphisms designed to convey God’s tremendously great power in rescuing Israel from their Egyptian bondage. They are preserved literally in many English versions (cf. KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV).

10.anchor: your God and worship before him.
10.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 26:2.

11.anchor: your God has given you and your family,
11.note: tn Or “household” (so NASB, NIV, NLT); Heb “house” (so KJV, NRSV).

12.anchor: When you finish tithing all
12.note: tn Heb includes “the tithes of.” This has not been included in the translation to avoid redundancy.

12.anchor: your income in the third year (the year of tithing), you must give it to the Levites, the resident foreigners, the orphans, and the widows
12.note: tn The terms “Levite, resident foreigner, orphan, and widow” are collective singulars in the Hebrew text (also in v. 13).

12.anchor: so that they may eat to their satisfaction in your villages.
12.note: tn Heb “gates.”

13.anchor: your God, “I have removed the sacred offering
13.note: tn Heb “the sacred thing.” The term הַקֹּדֶשׁ (haqqodesh) likely refers to an offering normally set apart for the Lord but, as a third-year tithe, given on this occasion to people in need. Sometimes this is translated as “the sacred portion” (cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV), but that could sound to a modern reader as if a part of the house were being removed and given away.

13.anchor: from my house and given it to the Levites, the resident foreigners, the orphans, and the widows just as you have commanded me.
13.note: tn Heb “according to all your commandment that you commanded me.” This has been simplified in the translation for stylistic reasons.

14.anchor: I have not eaten anything when I was in mourning, or removed any of it while ceremonially unclean, or offered any of it to the dead;
14.note: sn These practices suggest overtones of pagan ritual, all of which the confessor denies having undertaken. In Canaan they were connected with fertility practices associated with harvest time. See E. H. Merrill, Deuteronomy (NAC), 335-36.

14.anchor: I have obeyed you
14.note: tn Heb “the Lord my God.” See note on “he” in 26:2.

The question remains unanswered to this very day. Elsewhere the material is said to be “white like coriander seed” with “a taste like honey cakes” (Exod 16:31; cf. Num 11:7). Modern attempts to associate it with various desert plants are unsuccessful for the text says it was a new thing and, furthermore, one that appeared and disappeared miraculously (Exod 16:21-27).

9.note: sn A land whose stones are iron. Since iron deposits are few and far between in Palestine, the reference here is probably to iron ore found in mines as opposed to the meteorite iron more commonly known in that area.

5.note: tn Heb “take her as wife”; NRSV “taking her in marriage.”

5.anchor: and perform the duty of a brother-in-law.
5.note: sn This is the so-called “levirate” custom (from the Latin term levir, “brother-in-law”), an ancient provision whereby a man who died without male descendants to carry on his name could have a son by proxy, that is, through a surviving brother who would marry his widow and whose first son would then be attributed to the brother who had died. This is the only reference to this practice in an OT legal text but it is illustrated in the story of Judah and his sons (Gen 38) and possibly in the account of Ruth and Boaz (Ruth 2:8; 3:12; 4:6).

9.anchor: then his sister-in-law must approach him in view of the elders, remove his sandal from his foot, and spit in his face.
9.note: sn The removal of the sandal was likely symbolic of the relinquishment by the man of any claim to his dead brother’s estate since the sandal was associated with the soil or land (cf. Ruth 4:7-8). Spitting in the face was a sign of utmost disgust or disdain, an emotion the rejected widow would feel toward her uncooperative brother-in-law (cf. Num 12:14; Lev 15:8). See W. Bailey, NIDOTTE 2:544.
