29.anchor: For I know that after I die you will totally
29.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “totally.”

29.anchor: corrupt yourselves and turn away from the path I have commanded you to walk. Disaster will confront you in the days to come because you will act wickedly
, inciting him to anger because of your actions.”
29.note: tn Heb “the work of your hands.”

1.note: sn Beginning with 29:1, the verse numbers through 29:29 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 29:1 ET = 28:69 HT, 29:2 ET = 29:1 HT, 29:3 ET = 29:2 HT, etc., through 29:29 ET = 29:28 HT. With 30:1 the verse numbers in the ET and HT are again the same.

1.anchor: commanded Moses to make with the people of Israel in the land of Moab, in addition to the covenant he had made with them at Horeb.
1.note: sn Horeb is another name for Mount Sinai (which some English versions substitute here for clarity, cf. NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT).

2.anchor: did
2.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “to your eyes,” but this is redundant in English style (cf. the preceding “you have seen”) and is omitted in the translation.

3.anchor: Your eyes have seen the great judgments,
3.note: tn Heb “testings.” This is a reference to the plagues; see note at 4:34.

4.anchor: has not given you an understanding mind, perceptive eyes, or discerning ears!
4.note: tn Heb “a heart to know, eyes to see and ears to hear” (NASB similar); NAB, NRSV “a mind to understand, or eyes to see, or ears to hear.”

5.anchor: I have led you through the desert for forty years. Your clothing has not worn out
5.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “on you.” This has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.

5.anchor: nor have your sandals
5.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “from on your feet.”

6.anchor: You have eaten no bread and drunk no wine or beer – all so that you might know that I
6.note: tc The LXX reads “that he is the Lord your God.”

9.anchor: “Therefore, keep the terms
9.note: tn Heb “words.”

10.anchor: your God – the heads of your tribes,
10.note: tc Heb “your heads, your tribes.” The Syriac presupposes either “heads of your tribes” or “your heads, your judges,” etc. (reading שֹׁפְטֵכֶם [shofÿtekhem] for שִׁבְטֵיכֶם [shivtekhem]). Its comparative difficulty favors the originality of the MT reading. Cf. KJV “your captains of your tribes”; NRSV “the leaders of your tribes”; NLT “your tribal leaders.”

11.anchor: your infants, your wives, and the
11.note: tn Heb “your.”

12.anchor: your God is making with you today.
12.note: tn Heb “for you to pass on into the covenant of the Lord your God and into his oath, which the Lord your God is cutting with you today.”

13.anchor: Today he will affirm that you are his people and that he is your God,
13.note: tn Heb “in order to establish you today to him for a people and he will be to you for God.” Verses 10-13 are one long sentence in Hebrew. The translation divides this into two sentences for stylistic reasons.

13.anchor: just as he promised you and as he swore by oath to your ancestors
13.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in v. 25).

15.anchor: our God as well as those not with us here today.
15.note: tn This is interpreted by some English versions as a reference to generations not yet born (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT).

17.anchor: You have seen their detestable things
17.note: tn The Hebrew term שִׁקּוּץ (shiquts) refers to anything out of keeping with the nature and character of Yahweh and therefore to be avoided by his people Israel. It is commonly used with or as a synonym for תּוֹעֵבָה (to’evah, “detestable, abhorrent”; 2 Kgs 23:13; Jer 16:18; Ezek 5:11; 7:20; 11:18, 21; see note on the term “abhorrent” in Deut 7:25). See M. Grisanti, NIDOTTE 4:243-46.

17.anchor: and idols of wood, stone, silver, and gold.)
17.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “which were with them.” Verses 16-17 constitute a parenthetical comment.

18.anchor: our God today to pursue and serve the gods of those nations; beware that there is among you no root producing poisonous and bitter fruit.
18.note: tn Heb “yielding fruit poisonous and wormwood.” The Hebrew noun לַעֲנָה (la’anah) literally means “wormwood” (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB), but is used figuratively for anything extremely bitter, thus here “fruit poisonous and bitter.”

19.anchor: When such a person
19.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the subject of the warning in v. 18) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

19.anchor: hears the words of this oath he secretly
19.note: tn Heb “in his heart.”

19.anchor: blesses himself
19.note: tn Or “invokes a blessing on himself.” A formalized word of blessing is in view, the content of which appears later in the verse.

19.anchor: and says, “I will have peace though I continue to walk with a stubborn spirit.”
19.note: tn Heb “heart.”

19.anchor: This will destroy
19.note: tn Heb “thus destroying.” For stylistic reasons the translation begins a new sentence here.

19.anchor: the watered ground with the parched.
19.note: tn Heb “the watered with the parched.” The word “ground” is implied. The exact meaning of the phrase is uncertain although it appears to be figurative. This appears to be a proverbial observation employing a figure of speech (a merism) suggesting totality. That is, the Israelite who violates the letter and even spirit of the covenant will harm not only himself but everything he touches – “the watered and the parched.” Cf. CEV “you will cause the rest of Israel to be punished along with you.”

20.anchor: will be unwilling to forgive him, and his intense anger
20.note: tn Heb “the wrath of the Lord and his zeal.” The expression is a hendiadys, a figure in which the second noun becomes adjectival to the first.

20.anchor: will rage
20.note: tn Heb “smoke,” or “smolder.”

20.anchor: against that man; all the curses
20.note: tn Heb “the entire oath.”

20.anchor: written in this scroll will fall upon him
20.note: tn Or “will lie in wait against him.”

20.anchor: will obliterate his name from memory.
20.note: tn Heb “blot out his name from under the sky.”

21.anchor: will single him out
21.note: tn Heb “set him apart.”

21.anchor: for judgment
21.note: tn Heb “for evil”; NAB “for doom”; NASB “for adversity”; NIV “for disaster”; NRSV “for calamity.”

22.anchor: The generation to come – your descendants who will rise up after you, as well as the foreigner who will come from distant places – will see
22.note: tn Heb “will say and see.” One expects a quotation to appear, but it seems to be omitted. To avoid confusion in the translation, the verb “will say” is omitted.

23.anchor: destroyed in his intense anger.
23.note: tn Heb “the anger and the wrath.” This construction is a hendiadys intended to intensify the emotion.

24.anchor: done all this to this land? What is this fierce, heated display of anger
24.note: tn Heb “this great burning of anger”; KJV “the heat of this great anger.”

26.anchor: They went and served other gods and worshiped them, gods they did not know and that he did not permit them to worship.
26.note: tn Heb “did not assign to them”; NASB, NRSV “had not allotted to them.”

27.anchor: ’s anger erupted against this land, bringing on it all the curses

29.anchor: our God, but those that are revealed belong to us and our descendants
29.note: tn Heb “sons” (so NASB); KJV, ASV, NIV, NRSV “children.”

2.anchor: my sayings will drip like the dew,
2.note: tn Or “mist,” “light drizzle.” In some contexts the term appears to refer to light rain, rather than dew.

3.anchor: For I will proclaim the name
3.note: tc Smr and Tg read “in the name.”

4.anchor: he is fair
4.note: tn Or “just” (KJV, NAB, NRSV, NLT) or “righteous” (NASB).

5.anchor: They are a perverse
5.note: tn Heb “twisted,” “crooked.” See Ps 18:26.

6.anchor: Is this how you repay
6.note: tn Or “treat” (TEV).

7.note: tc The Syriac, Targum, and Vulgate read 2nd person masculine singular whereas the MT has 2nd person masculine plural. The former is preferred, the latter perhaps being a misreading (בִּינוּ [binu] for בִּינָה [binah]). Both the preceding (“remember”) and following (“ask”) imperatives are singular forms in the Hebrew text.
tn Heb “generation and generation.” The repetition of the singular noun here singles out each of the successive past generations. See IBHS 116 §7.2.3b.

8.anchor: according to the number of the heavenly assembly.
8.note: tc Heb “the sons of Israel.” The idea, perhaps, is that Israel was central to Yahweh’s purposes and all other nations were arranged and distributed according to how they related to Israel. See S. R. Driver, Deuteronomy (ICC), 355-56. For the MT יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּנֵי (bÿney yisra’el, “sons of Israel”) a Qumran fragment has “sons of God,” while the LXX reads ἀγγέλων θεοῦ (angelwn qeou, “angels of God”), presupposing בְּנֵי אֵל (bÿney ’el) or בְּנֵי אֵלִים (beney ’elim). “Sons of God” is undoubtedly the original reading; the MT and LXX have each interpreted it differently. MT assumes that the expression “sons of God” refers to Israel (cf. Hos. 1:10), while LXX has assumed that the phrase refers to the angelic heavenly assembly (Pss 29:1; 89:6; cf. as well Ps 82). The phrase is also attested in Ugaritic, where it refers to the high god El’s divine assembly. According to the latter view, which is reflected in the translation, the Lord delegated jurisdiction over the nations to his angelic host (cf. Dan. 10:13-21), while reserving for himself Israel, over whom he rules directly. For a defense of the view taken here, see M. S. Heiser, “Deuteronomy 32:8 and the Sons of God,” BSac 158 (2001): 52-74.

9.anchor: Jacob is his special possession.
9.note: tc Heb “the portion of his inheritance.” The LXX and Smr add “Israel” and BHS suggests the reconstruction: “The Lord’s allotment is Jacob, the portion of his inheritance is Israel” (cf. NAB). While providing good parallelism, it destroys a fine chiastic structure: “allotment” (a), “his people” (b), “Jacob (b’), and “inheritance” (a’).

10.anchor: he continually protected him
10.note: tn The distinctive form of the suffix on this verb form indicates that the verb is an imperfect, not a preterite. As such it draws attention to God’s continuing protection during the period in view. See A. F. Rainey, “The Ancient Hebrew Prefix Conjugation in the Light of Amarnah Canaanite,” Hebrew Studies 27 (1986): 15-16.

10.anchor: like the pupil
10.note: tn Heb “the little man.” The term אִישׁוֹן (’ishon) means literally “little man,” perhaps because when one looks into another’s eyes he sees himself reflected there in miniature. See A. Harman, NIDOTTE 1:391.

11.anchor: Lord
11.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

11.anchor: spread out his wings and took him,
11.note: tn The form of the suffix on this and the following verb forms (cf. “lifted him up”) indicates that the verbs are preterites, not imperfects. As such they simply state the action factually. The use of the preterite here suggests that the preceding verb (cf. “spread out”) is preterite as well.

12.anchor: alone was guiding him,
12.note: tn The distinctive form of the suffix on this verb form indicates that the verb is an imperfect, not a preterite. As such it draws attention to God’s continuing guidance during the period in view.

13.anchor: and olive oil
13.note: tn Heb “oil,” but this probably refers to olive oil; see note on the word “rock” at the end of this verse.

13.anchor: from the hardest of
13.note: tn Heb “flinty.”

13.anchor: rocks,
13.note: sn Olive oil from rock probably suggests olive trees growing on rocky ledges and yet doing so productively. See E. H. Merrill, Deuteronomy (NAC), 415; cf. TEV “their olive trees flourished in stony ground.”

15.anchor: you
15.note: tc The LXX reads the third person masculine singular (“he”) for the MT second person masculine singular (“you”), but such alterations are unnecessary in Hebrew poetic texts where subjects fluctuate frequently and without warning.

16.anchor: they enraged him with abhorrent idols.
16.note: tn Heb “abhorrent (things)” (cf. NRSV). A number of English versions understand this as referring to “idols” (NAB, NIV, NCV, CEV), while NLT supplies “acts.”

17.anchor: gods your ancestors
17.note: tn Heb “your fathers.”

18.anchor: You have forgotten
18.note: tc The Hebrew text is corrupt here; the translation follows the suggestion offered in HALOT 1477 s.v. שׁיה. Cf. NASB, NLT “You neglected”; NIV “You deserted”; NRSV “You were unmindful of.”

20.anchor: children
20.note: tn Heb “sons” (so NAB, NASB); TEV “unfaithful people.”

21.anchor: with a nation slow to learn
21.note: tn Heb “a foolish nation” (so KJV, NAB, NRSV); NIV “a nation that has no understanding”; NLT “I will provoke their fury by blessing the foolish Gentiles.”

22.anchor: and it burns to lowest Sheol;
22.note: tn Or “to the lowest depths of the earth”; cf. NAB “to the depths of the nether world”; NIV “to the realm of death below”; NLT “to the depths of the grave.”sn Sheol refers here not to hell and hell-fire – a much later concept – but to the innermost parts of the earth, as low down as one could get. The parallel with “the foundations of the mountains” makes this clear (cf. Pss 9:17; 16:10; 139:8; Isa 14:9, 15; Amos 9:2).

23.anchor: I will increase their
23.note: tn Heb “upon them.”

24.anchor: eaten by plague, and bitterly stung;
24.note: tn The Hebrew term קֶטֶב (qetev) is probably metaphorical here for the sting of a disease (HALOT 1091-92 s.v.).

25.anchor: they will destroy
25.note: tn A verb is omitted here in the Hebrew text; for purposes of English style one suitable to the context is supplied.

26.anchor: “I said, ‘I want to cut them in pieces.
26.note: tc The LXX reads “I said I would scatter them.” This reading is followed by a number of English versions (e.g., KJV, ASV, NIV, NCV, NRSV, NLT, CEV).

27.note: tn Heb “Our hand is high.” Cf. NAB “Our own hand won the victory.”

30.anchor: unless their Rock had delivered them up,
30.note: tn Heb “sold them” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

31.note: tn Heb “their,” but the referent (enemies) is specified in the translation for the sake of clarity.

32.anchor: and from the fields of Gomorrah.
32.note: sn Sodom…Gomorrah. The term “vine” is a reference to the pagan deities which, the passage says, find their ultimate source in Sodom and Gomorrah, that is, in the soil of perversion exemplified by these places (cf. Gen 18:20; 19:4-28; Isa 1:10; 3:9; Jer 23:14; Lam 4:6; Ezek 16:44-52; Matt 10:15; 11:23-24).

34.note: tn Verses 34-35 appear to be a quotation of the Lord and so the introductory phrase “says the Lord” is supplied in the translation.

35.note: tn Heb “prepared things,” “impending things.” See BDB 800 s.v. עָתִיד.

36.anchor: and will change his plans concerning
36.note: tn The translation understands the verb in the sense of “be grieved, relent” (cf. HALOT 689 s.v. נחם hitp 2); cf. KJV, ASV “repent himself”; NLT “will change his mind.” Another option is to translate “will show compassion to” (see BDB 637 s.v. נחם); cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV.

41.anchor: and repay those who hate me!
41.note: tn The Hebrew term שָׂנֵא (sane’, “hate”) in this covenant context speaks of those who reject Yahweh’s covenant overtures, that is, who disobey its stipulations (see note on the word “rejecting” in Deut 5:9; also see Deut 7:10; 2 Chr 19:2; Ps 81:15; 139:20-21).

42.note: tn Or “head” (the same Hebrew word can mean “head” in the sense of “leader, chieftain” or “head” in the sense of body part).

44.note: tn Heb “Hoshea” (so KJV, ASV), another name for the same individual (cf. Num 13:8, 16).

49.note: sn Abarim. This refers to the high plateau region of the Transjordan, the highest elevation of which is Mount Pisgah (or Nebo; cf. Deut 34:1). See also the note on the name “Pisgah” in Deut 3:17.

49.anchor: hill country, to Mount Nebo (which is in the land of Moab opposite Jericho
49.note: map For the location of Jericho see Map5-B2; Map6-E1; Map7-E1; Map8-E3; Map10-A2; Map11-A1.

50.note: tn In the Hebrew text the forms translated “you will die…and join” are imperatives, but the actions in view cannot really be commanded. The imperative is used here in a rhetorical, emphatic manner to indicate the certainty of Moses’ death on the mountain. On the rhetorical use of the imperative see IBHS 572 §34.4c.

50.anchor: on the mountain that you ascend and join your deceased ancestors,
50.note: tn Heb “be gathered to your people.” The same phrase occurs again later in this verse.

50.anchor: just as Aaron your brother died on Mount Hor
50.note: sn Mount Hor. See note on the name “Moserah” in Deut 10:6.

51.note: tn The use of the plural (“you”) in the Hebrew text suggests that Moses and Aaron are both in view here, since both had rebelled at some time or other, if not at Meribah Kadesh then elsewhere (cf. Num 20:24; 27:14).

51.anchor: rebelled against me among the Israelites at the waters of Meribah Kadesh in the desert of Zin when you did not show me proper respect
51.note: tn Heb “did not esteem me holy.” Cf. NIV “did not uphold my holiness”; NLT “failed to demonstrate my holiness.”

1.anchor: said to me, “Carve out for yourself two stone tablets like the first ones and come up the mountain to me; also make for yourself a wooden ark.
1.note: tn Or “chest” (so NIV, CEV); NLT “sacred chest”; TEV “wooden box.” This chest was made of acacia wood; it is later known as the ark of the covenant.

2.anchor: I will write on the tablets the same words
2.note: sn The same words. The care with which the replacement copy must be made underscores the importance of verbal precision in relaying the Lord’s commandments.

3.anchor: So I made an ark of acacia
3.note: sn Acacia wood (Heb “shittim wood”). This is wood from the acacia, the most common timber tree of the Sinai region. Most likely it is the species Acacia raddiana because this has the largest trunk. See F. N. Hepper, Illustrated Encyclopedia of Bible Plants, 63.

4.anchor: Lord
4.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

4.anchor: then wrote on the tablets the same words,
4.note: tn Heb “according to the former writing.” See note on the phrase “the same words” in v. 2.

4.anchor: the ten commandments,
4.note: tn Heb “ten words.” The “Ten Commandments” are known in Hebrew as the “Ten Words,” which in Greek became the “Decalogue.”

4.anchor: which he
4.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” The pronoun has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons to avoid redundancy.

4.anchor: had spoken to you at the mountain from the middle of the fire at the time of that assembly, and he
4.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” earlier in this verse.

6.anchor: “During those days the Israelites traveled from Beeroth Bene-Yaaqan
6.note: sn Beeroth Bene-Yaaqan. This Hebrew name could be translated “the wells of Bene-Yaaqan” or “the wells of the sons of Yaaqan,” a site whose location cannot be determined (cf. Num 33:31-32; 1 Chr 1:42).

6.anchor: to Moserah.
6.note: sn Moserah. Since Aaron in other texts (Num 20:28; 33:38) is said to have died on Mount Hor, this must be the Arabah region in which Hor was located.

7.anchor: From there they traveled to Gudgodah,
7.note: sn Gudgodah. This is probably the same as Haggidgad, which is also associated with Jotbathah (Num 33:33).

sn Jotbathah. This place, whose Hebrew name can be translated “place of wadis,” is possibly modern Ain Tabah, just north of Eilat, or Tabah, 6.5 mi (11 km) south of Eilat on the west shore of the Gulf of Aqaba.

8.anchor: set apart the tribe of Levi
8.note: sn The Lord set apart the tribe of Levi. This was not the initial commissioning of the tribe of Levi to this ministry (cf. Num 3:11-13; 8:12-26), but with Aaron’s death it seemed appropriate to Moses to reiterate Levi’s responsibilities. There is no reference in the Book of Numbers to this having been done, but the account of Eleazar’s succession to the priesthood there (Num 20:25-28) would provide a setting for this to have occurred.

8.anchor: to serve him, and to formulate blessings
8.note: sn To formulate blessings. The most famous example of this is the priestly “blessing formula” of Num 6:24-26.

9.anchor: Therefore Levi has no allotment or inheritance
9.note: sn Levi has no allotment or inheritance. As the priestly tribe, Levi would have no land allotment except for forty-eight towns set apart for their use (Num 35:1-8; Josh 21:1-42). But theirs was a far greater inheritance, for the Lord himself was their apportionment, that is, service to him would be their full-time and lifelong privilege (Num 18:20-24; Deut 18:2; Josh 13:33).

9.anchor: among his brothers;
9.note: tn That is, among the other Israelite tribes.

11.anchor: Then he
11.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 10:4.

11.anchor: said to me, “Get up, set out leading
11.note: tn Heb “before” (so KJV, ASV); NAB, NRSV “at the head of.”

11.anchor: the people so they may go and possess
11.note: tn After the imperative these subordinated jussive forms (with prefixed vav) indicate purpose or result.

11.anchor: the land I promised to give to their ancestors.”
11.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 15, 22).

12.anchor: your God require of you except to revere him,
12.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 10:4.

12.anchor: to obey all his commandments,
12.note: tn Heb “to walk in all his ways” (so KJV, NIV, NRSV); NAB “follow his ways exactly”; NLT “to live according to his will.”

12.anchor: to love him, to serve him
12.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in 10:4.

12.anchor: with all your mind and being,
12.note: tn Heb “heart and soul” or “heart and being”; NCV “with your whole being.” See note on the word “being” in Deut 6:5.

13.anchor: and to keep the Lord’s commandments and statutes that I am giving
13.note: tn Heb “commanding” (so NASB, NRSV). For stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy, “giving” has been used in the translation.

15.anchor: However, only to your ancestors did he
15.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 10:4.

15.anchor: show his loving favor,
15.note: tn Heb “take delight to love.” Here again the verb אָהַב (’ahav, “love”), juxtaposed with בָחַר (bakhar, “choose”), is a term in covenant contexts that describes the Lord’s initiative in calling the patriarchal ancestors to be the founders of a people special to him (cf. the note on the word “loved” in Deut 4:37).

15.anchor: and he chose you, their descendants,
15.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “after them,” but it is redundant in English style and has not been included in the translation.

16.anchor: Therefore, cleanse
16.note: tn Heb “circumcise the foreskin of” (cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV). Reference to the Abrahamic covenant prompts Moses to recall the sign of that covenant, namely, physical circumcision (Gen 17:9-14). Just as that act signified total covenant obedience, so spiritual circumcision (cleansing of the heart) signifies more internally a commitment to be pliable and obedient to the will of God (cf. Deut 30:6; Jer 4:4; 9:26).

16.anchor: your heart and stop being so stubborn!
16.note: tn Heb “your neck do not harden again.” See note on the word “stubborn” in Deut 9:6.

18.anchor: who justly treats
18.note: tn Or “who executes justice for” (so NAB, NRSV); NLT “gives justice to.”

21.anchor: He is the one you should praise;
21.note: tn Heb “your praise.” The pronoun is subjective and the noun “praise” is used here metonymically for the object of their praise (the Lord).

22.anchor: your God has made you as numerous as the stars of the sky.
22.note: tn Or “heavens.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.

1.note: tn The Hebrew text uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis, which the translation indicates with “indeed.”

1.anchor: your God and are careful to observe all his commandments I am giving
1.note: tn Heb “commanding”; NAB “which I enjoin on you today” (likewise in v. 15).

2.anchor: All these blessings will come to you in abundance
2.note: tn Heb “come upon you and overtake you” (so NASB, NRSV); NIV “come upon you and accompany you.”

3.anchor: You will be blessed in the city and blessed in the field.
3.note: tn Or “in the country” (so NAB, NIV, NLT). This expression also occurs in v. 15.

4.anchor: Your children
4.note: tn Heb “the fruit of your womb” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV).

6.anchor: You will be blessed when you come in and blessed when you go out.
6.note: sn Come in…go out. To “come in” and “go out” is a figure of speech (merism) indicating all of life and its activities.

7.anchor: will cause your enemies who attack
7.note: tn Heb “who rise up against” (so NIV).

7.anchor: you to be struck down before you; they will attack you from one direction
7.note: tn Heb “way” (also later in this verse and in v. 25).

8.anchor: will decree blessing for you with respect to your barns and in everything you do – yes, he will bless you in the land he
8.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God.” Because English would not typically reintroduce the proper name following a relative pronoun (“he will bless…the Lord your God is giving”), the pronoun (“he”) has been employed here in the translation.

9.anchor: will designate you as his holy people just as he promised you, if you keep his commandments
9.note: tn Heb “the commandments of the Lord your God.” See note on “he” in the previous verse.

9.anchor: and obey him.
9.note: tn Heb “and walk in his ways” (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).

10.anchor: Lord,
10.note: tn Heb “the name of the Lord is called over you.” The Hebrew idiom indicates ownership; see 2 Sam 12:28; Isa 4:1, as well as BDB 896 s.v. קָרָא Niph. 2.d.(4).

11.anchor: will greatly multiply your children,
11.note: tn Heb “the fruit of your womb” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV); CEV “will give you a lot of children.”

11.anchor: the offspring of your livestock, and the produce of your soil in the land which he
11.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 28:8.

11.anchor: promised your ancestors
11.note: tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 36, 64).

12.anchor: will open for you his good treasure house, the heavens, to give you rain for the land in its season and to bless all you do;
12.note: tn Heb “all the work of your hands.”

13.anchor: will make you the head and not the tail, and you will always end up at the top and not at the bottom, if you obey his
13.note: tn Heb “the Lord your God’s.” See note on “he” in 28:8.

13.anchor: commandments which I am urging
13.note: tn Heb “commanding” (so NRSV); NASB “which I charge you today.”

14.anchor: But you must not turn away from all the commandments I am giving
14.note: tn Heb “from all the words which I am commanding.”

14.anchor: you today, to either the right or left, nor pursue other gods and worship
14.note: tn Heb “in order to serve.”

15.anchor: “But if you ignore
15.note: tn Heb “do not hear the voice of.”

15.anchor: your God and are not careful to keep all his commandments and statutes I am giving you today, then all these curses will come upon you in full force:
15.note: tn Heb “and overtake you” (so NIV, NRSV); NAB, NLT “and overwhelm you.”

18.anchor: Your children
18.note: tn Heb “the fruit of your womb” (so NAB, NIV, NRSV).

19.anchor: You will be cursed when you come in and cursed when you go out.
19.note: sn See note on the similar expression in v. 6.

20.anchor: will send on you a curse, confusing you and opposing you
20.note: tn Heb “the curse, the confusion, and the rebuke” (NASB and NIV similar); NRSV “disaster, panic, and frustration.”

20.anchor: in everything you undertake
20.note: tn Heb “in all the stretching out of your hand.”

20.anchor: until you are destroyed and quickly perish because of the evil of your deeds, in that you have forsaken me.
20.note: tc For the MT first person common singular suffix (“me”), the LXX reads either “Lord” (Lucian) or third person masculine singular suffix (“him”; various codices). The MT’s more difficult reading probably represents the original text.tn Heb “the evil of your doings wherein you have forsaken me”; CEV “all because you rejected the Lord.”

21.anchor: will plague you with deadly diseases
21.note: tn Heb “will cause pestilence to cling to you.”

22.anchor: He
22.note: tn Heb “The Lord.” See note on “he” in 28:8.

22.anchor: will afflict you with weakness,
22.note: tn Or perhaps “consumption” (so KJV, NASB, NRSV). The term is from a verbal root that indicates a weakening of one’s physical strength (cf. NAB “wasting”; NIV, NLT “wasting disease”).

22.anchor: fever, inflammation, infection,
22.note: tn Heb “hot fever”; NIV “scorching heat.”

22.anchor: sword,
22.note: tn Or “drought” (so NIV, NRSV, NLT).

23.anchor: The
23.note: tc The MT reads “Your.” The LXX reads “Heaven will be to you.”

23.anchor: sky
23.note: tn Or “heavens” (also in the following verse). The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.

25.anchor: will allow you to be struck down before your enemies; you will attack them from one direction but flee from them in seven directions and will become an object of terror
25.note: tc The meaningless MT reading זַעֲוָה (za’avah) is clearly a transposition of the more commonly attested Hebrew noun זְוָעָה (zÿva’ah, “terror”).

28.anchor: will also subject you to madness, blindness, and confusion of mind.

29.anchor: You will feel your way along at noon like the blind person does in darkness and you will not succeed in anything you do;
29.note: tn Heb “you will not cause your ways to prosper.”

30.anchor: You will be engaged to a woman and another man will rape
30.note: tc For MT reading שָׁגַל (shagal, “ravish; violate”), the Syriac, Targum, and Vulgate presume the less violent שָׁכַב (shakhav, “lie with”). The unexpected counterpart to betrothal here favors the originality of the MT.

32.anchor: Your sons and daughters will be given to another people while you look on in vain all day, and you will be powerless to do anything about it.
32.note: tn Heb “and there will be no power in your hand”; NCV “there will be nothing you can do.”

36.anchor: will force you and your king
36.note: tc The LXX reads the plural “kings.”

40.anchor: You will have olive trees throughout your territory but you will not anoint yourself with olive oil, because the olives will drop off the trees while still unripe.
40.note: tn Heb “your olives will drop off” (נָשַׁל, nashal), referring to the olives dropping off before they ripen.

42.note: tn The Hebrew term denotes some sort of buzzing or whirring insect; some have understood this to be a type of locust (KJV, NIV, CEV), but other insects have also been suggested: “buzzing insects” (NAB); “the cricket” (NASB); “the cicada” (NRSV).

43.note: tn Heb “the foreigner.” This is a collective singular and has therefore been translated as plural; this includes the pronouns in the following verse, which are also singular in the Hebrew text.

45.anchor: your God by keeping his commandments and statutes that he has given
45.note: tn Heb “commanded”; NAB, NIV, TEV “he gave you.”

46.note: tn Heb “they”; the referent (the curses mentioned previously) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

46.anchor: will be a perpetual sign and wonder with reference to you and your descendants.

48.anchor: instead in hunger, thirst, nakedness, and poverty

48.note: tn Heb “he” (also later in this verse). The pronoun is a collective singular referring to the enemies (cf. CEV, NLT). Many translations understand the singular pronoun to refer to the Lord (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NCV, NRSV, TEV).

49.anchor: will raise up a distant nation against you, one from the other side of the earth
49.note: tn Heb “from the end of the earth.”
tn Some translations understand this to mean “like an eagle swoops down” (e.g., NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT), comparing the swift attack of an eagle to the attack of the Israelites’ enemies.

51.note: tn Heb “it” (so NRSV), a collective singular referring to the invading nation (several times in this verse and v. 52).

51.anchor: will devour the offspring of your livestock and the produce of your soil until you are destroyed. They will not leave you with any grain, new wine, olive oil, calves of your herds,

52.anchor: They will besiege all of your villages

53.anchor: You will then eat your own offspring,
53.note: tn Heb “the fruit of your womb” (so NAB, NRSV); NASB “the offspring of your own body.”

53.anchor: your God has given you, because of the severity of the siege

55.anchor: He will withhold from all of them his children’s flesh that he is eating (since there is nothing else left), because of the severity of the siege by which your enemy will constrict
55.note: tn Heb “besiege,” redundant with the noun “siege.”

56.note: tc The LXX adds σφόδρα (sfodra, “very”) to bring the description into line with v. 54.

56.anchor: tender and delicate of your women, who would never think of putting even the sole of her foot on the ground because of her daintiness,

57.anchor: and will secretly eat her afterbirth
57.note: tn Heb includes “that which comes out from between her feet.”
tn Heb “her sons that she will bear.”
tn Heb includes “in her need for everything.”

58.note: tn Heb “If you are not careful to do.”

60.anchor: He will infect you with all the diseases of Egypt
60.note: sn These are the plagues the Lord inflicted on the Egyptians prior to the exodus which, though they did not fall upon the Israelites, must have caused great terror (cf. Exod 15:26).

60.anchor: that you dreaded, and they will persistently afflict you.
60.note: tn Heb “will cling to you” (so NIV); NLT “will claim you.”

61.anchor: will bring upon you every kind of sickness and plague not mentioned in this scroll of commandments,
61.note: tn The Hebrew term תּוֹרָה (torah) can refer either (1) to the whole Pentateuch or, more likely, (2) to the book of Deuteronomy or even (3) only to this curse section of the covenant text. “Scroll” better reflects the actual document, since “book” conveys the notion of a bound book with pages to the modern English reader. Cf. KJV, NASB, NRSV “the book of this law”; NIV, NLT “this Book of the Law”; TEV “this book of God’s laws and teachings.”

62.anchor: There will be very few of you left, though at one time you were as numerous as the stars in the sky,
62.note: tn Or “heavens.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.
tn Heb “have not listened to the voice of.”

63.anchor: delighted to do good for you and make you numerous, he
63.note: tn Heb “the Lord.” See note on “he” in 28:8.

66.anchor: Your life will hang in doubt before you; you will be terrified by night and day and will have no certainty of surviving from one day to the next.
66.note: tn Heb “you will not be confident in your life.” The phrase “from one day to the next” is implied by the following verse.

1.anchor: Then Moses ascended from the deserts of Moab to Mount Nebo, to the summit of Pisgah, which is opposite Jericho.
1.note: sn For the geography involved, see note on the term “Pisgah” in Deut 3:17.map For the location of Jericho see Map5-B2; Map6-E1; Map7-E1; Map8-E3; Map10-A2; Map11-A1.

2.anchor: and all of Naphtali, the land of Ephraim and Manasseh, all the land of Judah as far as the distant
2.note: tn Or “western” (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); Heb “latter,” a reference to the Mediterranean Sea (cf. NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT).

4.anchor: said to him, “This is the land I promised to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob when I said, ‘I will give it to your descendants.’
4.note: tn Heb “seed” (so KJV, ASV).

4.anchor: I have let you see it,
4.note: tn The Hebrew text includes “with your eyes,” but this is redundant in English and is left untranslated.

6.anchor: He
6.note: tc Smr and some LXX mss read “they buried him,” that is, the Israelites. The MT reads “he buried him,” meaning in the context that “the Lord buried him.” This understanding, combined with the statement at the end of the verse that Moses’ burial place is unknown, gave rise to traditions during the intertestamental period that are reflected in the NT in Jude 9 and in OT pseudepigraphic works like the Assumption of Moses.

7.anchor: Moses was 120 years old when he died, but his eye was not dull
7.note: tn Or “dimmed.” The term could refer to dull appearance or to dimness caused by some loss of visual acuity.
tn Heb “sap.” That is, he was still in possession of his faculties or liveliness.

9.anchor: Now Joshua son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom, for Moses had placed his hands on him;
9.note: sn See Num 27:18.

10.anchor: face to face.
10.note: sn See Num 12:8; Deut 18:15-18.

11.anchor: He did
11.note: tn Heb “to,” “with respect to.” In the Hebrew text vv. 10-12 are one long sentence. For stylistic reasons the translation divides this into two, using the verb “he did” at the beginning of v. 11 and “he displayed” at the beginning of v. 12.

12.anchor: and he displayed great power
12.note: tn Heb “strong hand.”

12.anchor: and awesome might in view of all Israel.
12.note: tn The Hebrew text of v. 12 reads literally, “with respect to all the strong hand and with respect to all the awesome greatness which Moses did before the eyes of all Israel.”

2.anchor: With his right hand he gave a fiery law
2.note: tc The mispointed Hebrew term אֵשְׁדָּת (’eshdat) should perhaps be construed as אֵשְׁהַת (’eshhat) with Smr.

3.anchor: each receiving
3.note: tn The singular verbal form in the Hebrew text (lit. “he lifts up”) is understood in a distributive manner, focusing on the action of each individual within the group.

4.anchor: Moses delivered to us a law,
4.note: tn The Hebrew term תּוֹרָה (torah) here should be understood more broadly as instruction.

5.anchor: the tribes of Israel together.
5.note: sn The following blessing is given to the tribes in order, although the tribe of Simeon is curiously missing from the list.

6.anchor: and may his people multiply.
6.note: tn Heb “and [not] may his men be few” (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV).

7.note: tn The words “the blessing” are supplied in the translation for clarity and stylistic reasons.

8.anchor: and with whom you argued at the waters of Meribah.
8.note: sn Meribah means “contention, argument” in Hebrew; this is another wordplay on the incident that took place there. Cf. Num 20:13, 24; Ps 106:32.

9.anchor: He said to his father and mother, “I have not seen him,”
9.note: sn This statement no doubt alludes to the Levites’ destruction of their own fellow tribesmen following the golden calf incident (Exod 32:25-29).

11.anchor: undercut the legs
11.note: tn Heb “smash the sinews [or “loins,” so many English versions].” This part of the body was considered to be center of one’s strength (cf. Job 40:16; Ps 69:24; Prov 31:17; Nah 2:2, 11). See J. H. Tigay, Deuteronomy (JPSTC), 325.

12.anchor: Lord
12.note: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

12.anchor: places him on his chest.
12.note: tn Heb “between his shoulders.” This suggests the scene in John 13:23 with Jesus and the Beloved Disciple.

13.anchor: with the harvest produced by the sky,
13.note: tn Heb “from the harvest of the heavens.” The referent appears to be good crops produced by the rain that falls from the sky.

14.anchor: and by
14.note: tn Heb “and from the harvest of the yield of.” This has been simplified in the translation to avoid redundancy.

14.anchor: the moonlight;
14.note: tn Heb “the moon.” Many English versions regard this as a reference to “months” (“moons”) rather than the moon itself (cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV, NLT).

15.anchor: with the best
15.note: tn Heb “head” or “top.”

16.anchor: and on the top of the head of the one set apart
16.note: sn This apparently refers to Joseph’s special status among his brothers as a result of his being chosen by God to save the family from the famine and to lead Egypt.

17.anchor: They are the ten thousands of Ephraim,
17.note: sn Ephraim and Manasseh were the sons of Joseph who became founders of the two tribes into which Joseph’s descendants were split (Gen 48:19-20). Jacob’s blessing granted favored status to Ephraim; this is probably why Ephraim is viewed here as more numerous than Manasseh.

19.anchor: and the hidden treasures of the shores.
19.note: tn Heb “of the sand” (so NRSV, NLT); CEV “the sandy beach.”

20.anchor: he will tear at an arm – indeed, a scalp.
20.note: tn Heb “forehead,” picturing Gad attacking prey.

21.anchor: he came with the leaders
21.note: tn Heb “heads” (in the sense of chieftains).

22.anchor: he will leap forth from Bashan.
22.note: sn He will leap forth from Bashan. This may refer to Dan’s conquest of Laish, a region just to the west of Bashan (Judg 18:27-28).

24.anchor: and may he dip his foot in olive oil.
24.note: sn Dip his foot in olive oil. This is a metaphor for prosperity, one especially apt in light of the abundance of olive groves in the area settled by Asher. The Hebrew term refers to olive oil, which symbolizes blessing in the OT. See R. Way, NIDOTTE 4:171-73.

25.anchor: The bars of your gates
25.note: tn The words “of your gates” have been supplied in the translation to clarify the referent of “bars.”

26.anchor: who rides through the sky
26.note: tn Or “(who) rides (on) the heavens” (cf. NIV, NRSV, NLT). This title depicts Israel’s God as sovereign over the elements of the storm (cf. Ps 68:33). The use of the phrase here may be polemical; Moses may be asserting that Israel’s God, not Baal (called the “rider of the clouds” in the Ugaritic myths), is the true divine king (cf. v. 5) who controls the elements of the storm, grants agricultural prosperity, and delivers his people from their enemies. See R. B. Chisholm, Jr., “The Polemic against Baalism in Israel’s Early History and Literature,” BSac 151 (1994): 275.

27.anchor: and underneath you are his eternal arms;
27.note: tn Heb “and from under, arms of perpetuity.” The words “you” and “his” are supplied in the translation for clarification. Some have perceived this line to be problematic and have offered alternative translations that differ significantly from the present translation: “He spread out the primeval tent; he extended the ancient canopy” (NAB); “He subdues the ancient gods, shatters the forces of old” (NRSV). These are based on alternate meanings or conjectural emendations rather than textual variants in the mss and versions.

28.note: tn Or “skies.” The Hebrew term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.
tn Or perhaps “drizzle, showers.” See note at Deut 32:2.
